summary: This chunk defines HIEE and its importance for certain sports, outlining training methods for developing it and acknowledging the potential for interval training to improve both LIEE and HIEE.

content: Impairments in technical and tactical development usually occur as a result of accumulated fatigue, which is easily avoided through development of the appropriate physiological base via structured physical training. These concepts are among the best-kept secrets of the Eastern European training system.
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of structured physical training for preventing fatigue-related impairments in technical and tactical development, referencing Eastern European training methods.

content: HIEE requires the athlete to sustain or repeat high-intensity exercise with exercise durations of less than 2 min. HIEE can be developed through the performance of repeated sprints, sprint interval training, and resistance training. Recent research has shown that interval training has the potential to improve LIEE without compromising HIEE.
summary: HIEE involves high-intensity exercise lasting less than 2 minutes and can be developed through sprints, interval training, and resistance training. Interval training can also improve LIEE.

content: For certain sports athletes must develop appropriate endurance to maximize performance. During the preparation phase, SSPT includes higher volumes of sprint or interval training and tactical drills that are specific to the sport. One strategy is to create scenarios during SSPT that model the conditions experienced in competition. One might model an American football game by using 15 sprint intervals with a work to rest ratio of 1:10 (i.e., 5 s work, 50 s rest) to simulate what is encountered during competition.
summary: Athletes need appropriate endurance for performance, and SSPT during the preparation phase involves sprint/interval training and sport-specific drills. Scenarios can simulate competition conditions.

content: The implementation of SSPT in the preparatory phase of training depends on many factors including the athlete’s training age, chronological age, and the requirements of the sport. Physiological adaptations to SSPT can occur rapidly, in as little as 2 weeks of training. SSPT may be undertaken for 2 months or more, depending on the characteristics of the sport and the athlete’s level of development.
summary: SSPT implementation depends on factors like athlete age and sport requirements. Adaptations occur quickly, and SSPT duration varies based on sport and athlete development.

content: An exercise is a motor act that can be used to target general physiological adaptations, movement patterns, or specific muscle groups that are related to the performance of the athletic skill. To simulate maximal amounts of physiological adaptation, the athlete must train for 8 to 12 years to optimize performance. During these training years, exercises must be repeated systematically to stimulate adaptations that will improve performance.
summary: Exercises target physiological adaptations, movement patterns, or muscle groups for athletic skill. Maximal adaptation requires 8-12 years of systematic training.

content: Many training exercises are available to the coach when constructing a training plan. The coach should choose exercises that target the athlete’s needs and the demands of the sport. Exercises can be classified as general or specific in regard to the development of precise biomotor abilities. Both general and specific exercises will be used throughout the training year, but their contribution to the training plan will vary between training cycles and depending on the athlete’s training age.
summary: Coaches choose exercises based on athlete needs and sport demands. Exercises are classified as general or specific, with their contribution varying throughout the training year.

content: Exercises for general physical development are nonspecific exercises that contribute to the athlete’s physical development. These exercises develop strength, flexibility, mobility, aerobic fitness, and anaerobic capacity. Exercises for general physical development lay the foundation for further training by improving basic motor qualities that are central components of a multilateral program. Exercises for general physical development are central to the training plans of children and young athletes. These exercises are also important during the early part of the preparatory phase of training or with athletes who lack a solid training base.
summary: General physical development exercises improve overall fitness, including strength, flexibility, and aerobic/anaerobic capacity. They are foundational for further training and crucial for young athletes and those with limited training experience.

content: The first classification of exercises for general physical development consists of exercises that are performed without implements (calisthenics) or are performed with objects that are not used in competition (e.g., stall bars, benches, skipping rope, medicine balls). The second classification includes exercises derived from the actual sport or related sporting events. A contemporary interpretation of this concept can be seen in cross-training: During certain parts of the training year, athletes participate in sporting activities that are related to the sport in which they compete. For example, a cyclist may participate in cross-country skiing in the off-season to develop cardiovascular fitness.
summary: General physical development exercises can be calisthenics or use non-competition objects. They can also be sport-related, exemplified by cross-training, where athletes participate in related sports to develop fitness.

content: General physical development exercises are tools for developing overall fitness. Athletes need a balanced program in which muscular strength, flexibility, and endurance (HIEE or LIEE depending on the sport) are developed. When strength training, an athlete can use high-volume, low-intensity training plans to target general physical development. This type of training, if done appropriately, can increase muscular strength, muscular endurance (HIEE and LIEE), and flexibility (if undertaken through a full range of motion), which can lay the foundation for specialized training that targets precise biomotor abilities.
summary: General physical development exercises contribute to overall fitness, including strength, flexibility, and endurance. High-volume, low-intensity training can enhance these qualities and prepare athletes for specialized training.

content: Exercises for specific biomotor development target physiological adaptations, movement patterns, or muscle groups that are necessary for the sporting activity. This type of exercise is central to the concept of specificity of training. Specificity of training is the degree of similarity between the training exercise and the activities used in the sport. The more similar the characteristics of the training exercise are to the sport, the greater the transfer of training effects to the sport will be.
summary: Specific biomotor development exercises target the physiological adaptations, movement patterns, and muscle groups needed for the sport. This aligns with the principle of specificity of training, where exercise similarity to the sport enhances transferability.

content: When assessing the transferability of a training exercise to a sporting activity, the coach should consider the bioenergetics, movement patterns, and factors related to overload. The concept of movement pattern specificity reveals that the type of muscle action, kinematic characteristics (i.e., movement patterns), kinetic characteristics (i.e., forces, rate of force development, power output), muscle groups activated, and acceleration or velocity characteristics of the movement all contribute to the exercise’s ability to transfer to the sporting activity. Particularly important to training specificity are the movement pattern and the primary muscles used in the sporting activity.
summary: When evaluating exercise transferability, coaches consider bioenergetics, movement patterns, and overload. Movement pattern specificity emphasizes the importance of matching exercise movement and muscle activation to the sport.

content: For example, the primary movers related to sprinting performance are the muscles of the lower body. Therefore, a coach who works with sprinters should use exercises that target the development of lower-body muscles. However, the coach should also consider the synergistic muscles that are used in concert with the muscles of the leg.
summary: Exercise selection for sprinters should focus on lower-body development, but also consider synergistic muscles that support sprinting movements.

content: The concept of movement pattern specificity reveals that the type of muscle action, kinematic characteristics (i.e., movement patterns), kinetic characteristics (i.e., forces, rate of force development, power output), muscle groups activated, and acceleration or velocity characteristics of the movement all contribute to the exercise’s ability to transfer to the sporting activity. Particularly important to training specificity are the movement pattern and the primary muscles used in the sporting activity. For example, the primary movers related to sprinting performance are the muscles of the lower body. Therefore, a coach who works with sprinters should use exercises that target the development of lower-body muscles. However, the coach should also consider the synergistic muscles that are used in concert with the muscles of the leg. The best way to accomplish this is to target movement patterns. For example, the sprinter might use the power clean as a training exercise, because it has a power, force, and velocity profile similar to that used in sprinting. Additionally, the power clean activates the muscles of the trunk and other synergistic muscles that affect running performance.
summary: Training specificity emphasizes the importance of movement patterns and primary muscle groups used in a sport. Coaches should select exercises that mimic these characteristics, such as the power clean for sprinters.

content: Many exercises activate the prime movers and synergistic muscles related to sprinting performance, including jumping (plyometrics), squatting (back squat, one-leg squat, front squat), and weighted sled pulling. In the scientific literature, sprinting performance has been significantly related to power clean performance (1), back squat performance (11), and vertical jumping performance (5, 11).
summary: Exercises like plyometrics, squats, and weighted sled pulls effectively target muscles crucial for sprinting performance, as evidenced by research linking these exercises to improved sprinting outcomes.

content: The use of exercises that are external to the athlete’s sport is important, because sport performance alone will not give the athlete a great enough training stimulus to maximize performance gains (e.g., leg power, speed, or force-generating capacity). For example, the best high jumpers in the world do not perform more than 800 jumps per year, and this number of jumps is insufficient to develop leg power. To maximize performance gains, these athletes perform tens of thousands of exercises aimed at developing leg power (e.g., back squats, power cleans, plyometric exercises). However it is important to keep those exercises sport-specific; the higher the number of exercises, the lower the rate of specific adaptation.
summary: While sport-specific training is essential, incorporating exercises outside the athlete's sport can provide a greater training stimulus for developing key attributes like leg power. However, maintaining sport specificity is crucial to avoid diminishing returns.

content: Sport-specific exercises are essential to maximize the transfer of training effects from training to sport performance. These exercises are not only very important in the preparatory phase of training but also should be considered essential components of the competitive phase of training. Some coaches and athletes exclude sport-specific exercises during the competitive phase of the periodized training plan, choosing to only perform technical training during this time. This practice is problematic because the exclusion of sport-specific training exercises during the competitive phase may lead to a loss of preparedness that reduces performance as the season progresses. The coach and athlete should consider sport-specific exercises as essential components of every phase of the training plan because these exercises transfer directly to sporting performance.
summary: Sport-specific exercises are vital throughout all training phases, including the competitive phase, to ensure athletes maintain preparedness and maximize performance transfer.

content: One element that discriminates various sporting activities is the technique (i.e., motor skills) required. Technique encompasses all of the movement patterns, skills, and technical elements that are necessary to perform the sport. Technique can be considered the manner of performing a skill or physical exercise. Athletes must continually strive to establish perfect technique to create the most efficient movement patterns. The more perfect or biomechanically sound the technique is, the more efficient or economical the athlete will be. For example, less energy is expended when an athlete has good running economy or technique (28). Trained runners have been reported to be more economical and to consume 20% to 30% less oxygen compared with novice runners who are running at the same submaximal speed (10, 14, 27). Biomechanists have suggested running economy is affected by stride length (8), stride rate (23), vertical stiffness (13), net vertical impulse of ground reaction forces (20), and ground contact time (28). Thus, if a runner
summary: Technique, encompassing movement patterns and skills, is crucial for efficient performance. Athletes should strive for perfect technique to minimize energy expenditure and maximize performance.

content: Proper technique allows an athlete to efficiently perform a skill. Technical training must be included in training plans. Every sporting activity has a technical standard or technical model that is accepted as being perfect or as close as possible to perfect and represents the accepted model of performance. A model of performance must be biomechanically sound and physiologically efficient to be widely accepted. The model is generally not developed based on the technique of elite or champion athletes because their technique may not be biomechanically or physiologically sound. Simply copying the technique of a champion is not advisable. A technical model should exhibit some flexibility because it should be constantly updated based on new research findings. The technical model should be used as a point of comparison for an athlete’s performance. The coach can develop a training plan that targets deficiencies using the technical model. Although the technical model is invaluable for training purposes, the athlete likely will develop her own individualized performance style. Individual technical styles are simply adaptations of an accepted model of performance that occur in response to technical problems in performing a motor act. The Fosbury flop changed the technique of high jumping dramatically.
summary: Technical models, based on sound biomechanics and physiology, serve as benchmarks for athletes. While coaches should use these models to guide training, athletes often develop unique styles based on individual needs and adaptations.

content: The technical model should be used as a point of comparison for an athlete’s performance. The coach can develop a training plan that targets deficiencies using the technical model. Although the technical model is invaluable for training purposes, the athlete likely will develop her own individualized performance style. Individual technical styles are simply adaptations of an accepted model of performance that occur in response to technical problems in performing a motor act.
summary: Technical models provide a benchmark for performance and guide training, but athletes often develop unique styles based on individual needs and challenges.

content: The Fosbury flop changed the technique of high jumping dramatically. The Fosbury flop is considered the optimal model for high jumping.
summary: The Fosbury flop revolutionized high jump technique and is now considered the most effective method.

content: Shot distribution, execution, and rally length in net sports can be analyzed and used to develop a model of performance.
summary: Performance in net sports can be analyzed through shot patterns and rally dynamics to create a performance model.

content: Not all techniques are useful for all athletes. A novice athlete will use a more simplified technique than a world-class athlete. Technique is developed in stages, whereby simplified techniques are introduced first.
summary: Technique varies based on experience level, with simpler methods used initially and progressing to more complex ones as athletes develop.

content: When working with a young discus thrower, a coach begins with perfecting the standing throw. Once the standing throw is mastered, the coach can add other elements, such as a 4/4 turn or footwork drills, to begin teaching the athlete the rotational technique needed to be a successful discus thrower.
summary: Discus technique development progresses from mastering the standing throw to incorporating rotational elements.

content: Novice athletes generally use techniques that are vastly different than those of elite athletes as a result of their developmental status.
summary: Technique differences between novice and elite athletes are due to developmental stages.

content: Variations can exist in the performance of a technical skill. Cyclic sports often exhibit fewer interindividual technical differences, whereas acyclic sports have a greater potential for variations in technique.
summary: Technical variations exist across athletes, with cyclic sports showing less variation than acyclic ones.

content: Al Oerter tended to hold the discus in a lower position during his rotation than did most discus throwers, which is generally considered to be a technical flaw. Al Oerter's individual technical pattern was highly effective because of his highly developed upper-body strength and fast leg speed.
summary: Al Oerter's unconventional discus technique, while considered flawed, was successful due to his physical attributes.

content: Technique is developed based on the athlete’s abilities, physiological and mechanical characteristics, and level of development.
summary: Technique development is individualized based on an athlete's capabilities and stage of development.

content: If the thrower does not possess an adequate strength base, he may not be strong enough to keep his trunk vertical throughout the throwing movement.
summary: Physical strength is essential for maintaining proper technique during throwing movements.

content: Physical training is the foundation of all training factors. Interruptions in training generally affect the athlete’s physical capacity, which may result in slight alterations in technique as a result of lost preparedness. When athletes experience a decline in physical capacity, a concomitant deterioration in technique often occurs.
summary: Physical fitness is crucial for maintaining proper technique; declines in physical capacity can negatively impact technique.

content: High levels of fatigue can negatively affect an athlete’s technique or ability to perfect technique.
summary: Fatigue can impair an athlete's ability to execute and refine technique.

content: Some suggest that technical training should occur before conditioning, and a heavy conditioning day should not precede a technique day.
summary: Prioritizing technical training over conditioning, especially on days with heavy conditioning, is recommended.

content: Learning technique is a process in which an athlete acquires mechanical skill, perfects the skill, and then ingrains the skill.
summary: Technique acquisition involves three stages: learning, perfecting, and ingraining the skill.

content: The ability of an athlete to learn new mechanical skills depends on many factors including the athlete’s current technical skill and the complexity of the skill that is being targeted. The athlete’s physical attributes or level of development will affect the ability to learn new skills.
summary: An athlete's ability to learn new skills depends on their current skill level, the complexity of the skill, and their physical development.

content: Many other factors such as the athlete’s learning style or the teaching methods used can also affect how easily the athlete acquires the new skill set.
summary: Learning style and teaching methods can influence an athlete's skill acquisition.

content: The learning of a new skill set has been suggested to be a three-part process. During the first part of learning a new skill, the athlete should receive a detailed explanation of the skill and observe the skill being performed. After the initial demonstration and explanation, the athlete begins to develop the rough technical aspects of the skill, paying particular emphasis to the most crucial phases of the movement pattern.
summary: Learning a new skill involves three phases: initial explanation and observation, developing the basic technique, and refining the movement.

content: During the second phase of the learning process, the athlete begins to refine the skill, a long-term process in which many repetitions of the movement are performed. During this phase technical errors are continually addressed and the athlete strives to perfect the movement pattern and minimize or eliminate technical deficiencies.
summary: The second phase of skill learning focuses on refinement through repetition and addressing technical errors.

content: In the third phase of learning the skill, the athlete begins to ingrain the movement pattern so that the skill is automated and happens naturally; this requires large amounts of repetitive practice undertaken for significant amounts of time.
summary: The final stage of skill learning involves automating the movement through extensive practice.

content: Technique continues to evolve as technological and creative innovations are introduced into the sporting environment. Over time, training practices and techniques change, and what was once an advanced technique may become outdated.
summary: Technique evolves with technological advancements and changing training practices.

content: Technical innovations in sport can come from the coach’s imagination or from scientific inquiry into the physiological and mechanical aspects of the sport.
summary: New techniques can arise from coaching creativity or scientific research.

content: New techniques may work well in ideal situations or in practice, but they must be translated into the competitive arena before they become accepted as a technical model. Not all new techniques or ideas will translate into the competitive arena because this environment is distinctly unique, attributable to its high levels of physical and psychological stress and its random nature.
summary: New techniques must be proven effective in competitive settings, which are distinct from training environments due to their unique pressures.

content: When coaches and athletes attempt to improve and perfect technique, they must model the technique not only in ideal situations but also in competition.
summary: Technique refinement should be practiced in both ideal and competitive situations.

content: Over time, training practices and techniques change, and what was once an advanced technique may become outdated. New techniques may work well in ideal situations or in practice, but they must be translated into the competitive arena before they become accepted as a technical model. Not all new techniques or ideas will translate into the competitive arena because this environment is distinctly unique, attributable to its high levels of physical and psychological stress and its random nature. When coaches and athletes attempt to improve and perfect technique, they must model the technique not only in ideal situations but also in competition.
summary: Training techniques evolve, and new ones must be proven effective in competition to gain acceptance.

content: Technical innovations in sport can come from the coach’s imagination or from scientific inquiry into the physiological and mechanical aspects of the sport.
summary: New sports techniques can arise from coaching creativity or scientific research.

content: Tactics and strategy are important concepts in coaching and athletics. Both terms are derived from the military vocabulary and have a Greek origin. The word tactics is derived from the Greek word taktika, which refers to the how things are arranged. Strategy comes from the Greek word strategos, which means “general” or “the art of the general.” In the theory of warfare, strategies focus on wide spaces, long periods, and large movements of forces, whereas tactics address smaller spaces, times, and forces. When examined in a hierarchical perspective, strategies precede war planning and the actual tactics that are used on the battlefield.
summary: Tactics and strategy, rooted in military terminology, are hierarchical concepts in sports coaching.

content: Tactics and strategies can be used during training or in a competition with direct or indirect opponents. Strategy is the organization of training, play, or competition that is based on a philosophy or way of approaching a problem (e.g., training or competition). Within the strategic framework are tactics, or the training or game plans. A good example of the interrelationship between strategies and tactics can be seen in the training process, where strength and conditioning coaches induce physiological responses by using tactics that are organized into rational systems. The simplest approach to strategy and tactics is to consider strategy as the art of projecting and directing training or competitive plans, and tactics as the organization of these plans.
summary: Strategies and tactics are applied in training and competition, with strategy encompassing the broader approach and tactics forming the specific plans within it.

content: Tactical training refers to training offensive and defensive objectives (e.g., scoring, a specific play) that are germane to a sport. Skills that are considered part of tactical training in soccer include passing, pace of attacks, tackling, pass distribution, dribbling skills, and length of passes. Each sport requires certain skills, and thus tactical training may be different for each sporting activity. Tactical actions are part of the strategic framework used to train the athlete and prepare for competition.
summary: Tactical training focuses on sport-specific skills and strategies to achieve objectives.

content: The basis of any successful tactical plan, regardless of the sporting activity, is a high level of technical proficiency. Technique is a limiting factor for all tactical maneuvers, and tactics are a function of an athlete’s technique. Technical abilities are based on the physiological adaptations that occur in response to physical training. Physical training is the foundation for technical and tactical training.
summary: Technical skill is fundamental to effective tactical execution, and physical training forms the base for both.

content: For most elite athletes, there is very little difference between their physiological development and their technical skill. Often, when all other factors are held equal, the winning athlete uses more mature, advanced, and rational tactics. Even though tactical training relies heavily on physical and technical training, there appears to be an important link between psychological and tactical training. Tactical mastery is founded on deep theoretical knowledge and the capacity to apply tactics appropriate for the competitive environment.
summary: Elite athletes demonstrate a high level of integration between physiological, technical, and tactical abilities.

content: Tactical training may include studying strategic elements of the principle sport, studying the rules and regulations for competition in the sport or event, evaluating the tactical abilities of the best athletes in the sport, researching the strategies used by opponents, evaluating the physical and psychological attributes and potential of opponents, evaluating the facilities and environment of competition sites, developing individual tactics that are based on personal strengths and weaknesses, critically analyzing past performances against specific opponents, developing an individualized tactical model with appropriate variations to meet multiple competitive demands, and practicing a tactical model in training until it becomes ingrained.
summary: Tactical training involves a multifaceted approach encompassing theoretical knowledge, opponent analysis, and practical application.

content: The development of tactical skills is accomplished with the same basic steps outlined earlier in this chapter, in the section titled Learning and Skill Formation. Traditionally, athletes approach tactical skills training after developing the appropriate physiological basis (physical training) and technical skills. It is also possible to develop all three factors simultaneously as a result of proper planning and training program integration.
summary: Tactical skill development follows a structured process, typically building upon physical and technical foundations.

content: Most sporting activities can be classified into five basic groups as a result of their tactical similarities. Group 1: Sports in which athletes compete separately, with no direct contact with opponents. These sports generally require athletes to perform in a predetermined order. Examples include alpine skiing, track cycling (individual events such as the 1,000 m or 4,000 m pursuit), cycling (time trial), figure skating, gymnastics, diving, in-line skating, and weightlifting. Group 2: Sports in which athletes start the competition at the same time, in either large or small groups. In these sports, some cooperation with teammates is possible, thus adding a tactical element that requires some teamwork. Examples include running, track and field, cross-country skiing, cycling (track and road cycling), Nordic skiing, cross country running, and swimming. Group 3 of sports includes those characterized by direct competition between two opponents. Examples of Group 3 sports include boxing, wrestling, tennis, fencing, and mixed martial arts.
summary: Sports can be categorized into five groups based on their tactical similarities, ranging from individual performance to direct competition.

content: There are 60 events in track and field, cross-country skiing, cycling (track and road cycling), Nordic skiing, cross country running, and swimming. Group 1 of sports includes those characterized by individual performance. Group 2 of sports includes those characterized by individual performance over a set distance or time. Group 3 of sports includes those characterized by direct competition between two opponents. Examples of Group 3 sports include boxing, wrestling, tennis, fencing, and mixed martial arts. Group 4 of sports includes those in which the opponents are in teams and the athletes have direct contact during the game or competition. Examples of Group 4 sports include baseball, soccer, American football, hockey, and rugby. Group 5 of sports includes those that require athletic participation in a combination of different sporting activities. Examples of Group 5 sports include the heptathlon and decathlon in track and field, the biathlon (shooting and Nordic skiing), the triathlon, and the modern pentathlon.
summary: This chunk defines five groups of sports based on their characteristics: individual performance, individual performance over distance/time, direct competition between two, team competition with contact, and combined sporting activities.

content: The ability to maintain tactical proficiency under conditions of fatigue is an important determinant of competitive success. An athlete’s tactical training must include sessions that require the athlete to perform under conditions of fatigue. A coach can create fatigue conditions by extending practice after the athlete has become fatigued. Another way to create fatigue conditions is to use several rested sparring partners during training. The physical training base provides the foundation for the athlete’s ability to perform under conditions of fatigue. The higher the physical training base, the greater the work capacity.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of fatigue training for athletes, highlighting how it improves tactical performance and is built upon a strong physical foundation.

content: In close races or games, success often depends on the athlete’s capacity to mobilize all forces and give everything for the final moments of the competition. Coaches can create scenarios that require the athlete to maximize effort in simulated end-of-competition situations.
summary: This chunk focuses on the importance of maximizing effort in crucial moments of competition and how coaches can simulate these scenarios in training.

content: Athletes often have to perform under adverse or unusual environmental conditions. Examples of adverse conditions include a wet field, a strong wind, cold water, or a noisy environment. Athletes should perform skills and tactical maneuvers correctly and efficiently under unusual or simulated conditions.
summary: This chunk stresses the need for athletes to adapt and perform well in challenging environmental conditions, both in training and competition.

content: Athletes should organize exhibition games or competitions with partners who follow the same tactics as future opponents. The ability to demonstrate tactical discipline is essential in training. In competition, an athlete may experience a tactical problem that was not addressed or simulated by the coach. Athletes can create a repertoire of tactical solutions by being exposed to various situations in training and exhibition competitions.
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of tactical discipline, preparing for unexpected situations, and developing a diverse range of tactical solutions through training.

content: The cohesive interaction of a team is essential for success in sports classified in groups 2 and 4. Using techniques such as limiting external conditions (e.g., decreasing the available time or playing space) can force a team to interact and cooperate. Additional stress can be introduced by adding fatigue to training scenarios. An additional strategy is to perform tactical maneuvers against a conventional opponent who is attempting to counteract the play. The coach should instruct opposing players to behave as if they are not familiar with the applied tactics.
summary: This chunk focuses on team cohesion and training techniques that promote cooperation and adaptability within a team.

content: Reserve players should participate in the preparation of game tactics because changes in the team lineup increase the potential of a breakdown in cooperation and tactics. It is useful during practices to replace key players with spare players. Replacing key players allows reserve players to become familiar with the team tactics and the other players. Replacing key players allows the existing group to see how reserve players operate and how the team’s tactics will change with the reserve players’ presence. These techniques allow the team to develop new tactical combinations that can improve the team’s competitive capabilities.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of reserve players in team training, highlighting how their involvement strengthens team tactics and prepares for lineup changes.

content: To maximize team cooperation, the coach should introduce changes in the team’s tactics that will increase its tactical flexibility. The team can use tactical flexibility to create scenarios that will surprise opponents. Tactical variations include substituting different tactics at predetermined times or in response to signals from a coach or designated player (e.g., captain). Tactical variations include substituting players who bring a new and unexpected game change to the team.
summary: This chunk focuses on the benefits of tactical flexibility for teams, enabling them to adapt, surprise opponents, and utilize various strategic approaches.

content: Exposing the team to exhibition games against teams that use various styles of play allows the team to prepare for these scenarios in future games and develop tactical solutions to the style of play encountered. A central component of tactical training is developing tactical thinking skills.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the value of diverse training experiences and the importance of developing tactical thinking skills for athletes.

content: The team can use tactical flexibility to create scenarios that will surprise opponents. Tactical variations include substituting different tactics at predetermined times or in response to signals from a coach or designated player (e.g., captain). Tactical variations include substituting players who bring a new and unexpected game change to the team.
summary: Tactical flexibility involves varying tactics, including pre-determined substitutions and player swaps, to surprise opponents.

content: Exposing the team to exhibition games against teams that use various styles of play allows the team to prepare for these scenarios in future games and develop tactical solutions to the style of play encountered.
summary: Playing exhibition games against diverse opponents helps teams prepare for various tactical scenarios.

content: A central component of tactical training is developing tactical thinking skills. To think tactically, the athlete must learn to realistically and correctly evaluate opponents as well as himself. To think tactically, the athlete must instantly recall technical skills and combinations of skills that can be used in game situations. To think tactically, the athlete must anticipate his opponent’s tactics and use the appropriate tactics to counteract the opponent. To think tactically, the athlete must disguise or conceal tactics that may prevent the opponent from sensing and counteracting the plan of attack. To think tactically, the athlete must perfectly coordinate individual actions within the team tactics.
summary: Tactical thinking involves evaluating opponents, recalling skills, anticipating tactics, disguising plans, and coordinating actions.

content: The competition or game plan is based on analysis of tactical trends and the opponent’s strengths and weaknesses. Components of the game plan are then integrated into the tactical training portion of the training plan. The game plan is usually introduced progressively over the last two or three microcycles so that it can be perfected by the time of competition.
summary: The game plan is developed based on opponent analysis and integrated into training, progressively introduced over time.

content: The game or competition plan is important for several reasons. The game or competition plan instills confidence and optimism about the upcoming competition. The game or competition plan informs the athlete about the place, facilities, and conditions under which the contest will be organized. The game or competition plan introduces the strengths and weaknesses of future opponents into each training factor. The game or competition plan uses the athlete’s past performance as a reference from which to build confidence emphasizing the strong points from which to build a realistic optimism, without disregarding the athlete’s weaknesses. The game or competition plan develops realistic objectives for the competition using all of the preceding factors.
summary: The game plan provides confidence, information, opponent insights, builds on past performance, and sets realistic objectives.

content: The implementation of the game or competition plan occurs in several phases. First, a preliminary game plan is developed. The coach develops this plan after comprehensively analyzing potential tactical difficulties that the athlete or team is likely to encounter during the game or competition. Tactical solutions or objectives are then created in response to the potential tactical difficulties revealed during the critical analysis. In the context of the tactical plan, individual tactical objectives are assigned to players based on their strengths and weaknesses. The tactical objectives are then practiced as part of the tactical training plan.
summary: Game plan implementation involves developing a preliminary plan, analyzing potential difficulties, creating solutions, assigning individual objectives, and practicing them.

content: In the days prior to the game, the athlete should avoid changing habits because this may adversely affect game-time performance. Two or three days before the competition, the coach should reinforce the game plan and the tactics that have been developed, using structured practices that allow for the development of good technical and tactical performances. When possible, the training lesson should mirror the competitive model. The coach should acknowledge good performances to develop confidence, create motivation, and increase competitive desire. As the competition approaches, the coach should focus only on a few major points of the game plan without overwhelming the athlete with too many instructions.
summary: Pre-game preparation involves maintaining routines, reinforcing the game plan, mirroring competitive conditions, providing positive feedback, and focusing on key points.

content: No matter how detailed the preliminary game plan, there is always a potential for unforeseen technical and tactical occurrences. Therefore the plan must be flexible enough to allow the athlete to respond to these challenges. The second phase of the game plan is the implementation of the general plan in an actual game situation. The initial phase of the game is generally used to test the main elements of the tactical plan. In this portion of the game, the team will strive to unveil the opponent’s game plan while hiding their own plan. The athlete will need to be able to analyze and comprehend the tactical situations that arise and choose a tactical action to apply.
summary: The game plan must be flexible to adapt to unforeseen circumstances. Implementation involves testing the plan, uncovering opponent strategies, and making tactical decisions based on the situation.

content: After the game is completed, the plan is comprehensively analyzed, allowing for further refinement of the plan and its tactical components.
summary: Post-game analysis allows for refining the game plan and its tactical components.

content: The initial phase of the game is generally used to test the main elements of the tactical plan. In this portion of the game, the team will strive to unveil the opponent’s game plan while hiding their own plan.
summary: The initial phase of a game focuses on testing the tactical plan and gathering information about the opponent's strategy.

content: The second phase of the game plan is the implementation of the general plan in an actual game situation. The athlete will need to be able to analyze and comprehend the tactical situations that arise and choose a tactical action to apply. The ability to comprehend these tactical situations will depend on the athlete’s tactical knowledge, experience, team dynamics, and tactical preparation.
summary: The second phase involves applying the tactical plan in a game, requiring athletes to analyze situations and make tactical decisions based on their knowledge and experience.

content: These attributes will allow the athlete to solve problems by instantaneously working through periods of analysis, synthesis (i.e., combining separate parts into a whole), comparison, and generalization. This process allows the athlete to determine the most appropriate solutions to the tactical demands of the game. The individual decision-making processes will occur in concert with the group. The coordinated efforts between each individual on a team allow for rational, original, rapid, economical, and efficient solutions to the fluctuating tactical challenges that arise during the game.
summary: Athletes solve tactical problems through analysis, synthesis, and comparison, working together to find efficient solutions to in-game challenges.

content: The coach should closely examine how the game plan was developed, the effectiveness of the individual tactical roles in the plan, the success of the tactical plan, and, if the game plan did not succeed, the reasons why. The most appropriate time to analyze the game plan and discuss the results of the analysis with the athletes depends on the outcome of the game or competition. If the result was favorable, analysis of the game can occur soon after the game’s completion, and discussion of the results of the analysis can occur during the first practice session after the game. Conversely, if the outcome of the game is unfavorable, the analysis should be delayed to allow for a critical examination of the performance. The coach should discuss the analysis with the athletes 2 or 3 days after the competition to allow time for the psychological wounds to heal.
summary: Coaches analyze game plans after competition, adjusting their approach based on the outcome and allowing time for athletes to process the experience.

content: When discussing the analysis with the athletes, the coach should be clear and reasonable and should highlight the positive aspects of the performance. The coach should also project optimism and propose a few tactical elements to emphasize in subsequent training.
summary: Coaches provide constructive feedback to athletes after analysis, focusing on both positive aspects and areas for improvement.

content: Both technique and strategy in sport are in continual flux. Technical and tactical knowledge is continually changing in direct response to the evolution of sport science and practical experience. This increase in technical and tactical knowledge increases the effectiveness of training.
summary: Technique and strategy in sports are constantly evolving, requiring ongoing learning and adaptation to improve training effectiveness.

content: To achieve technical and tactical mastery, the coach and athlete must optimize three relationships between conflicting concepts: integration–differentiation, stability–variability, and standardization–individualization.
summary: Coaches and athletes must balance integration and differentiation, stability and variability, and standardization and individualization to achieve mastery.

content: Integration refers to combining the individual skill or tactical maneuvers into a whole process, whereas differentiation involves analytically processing each component of the whole process. When learning a new technique or skill, the athlete progresses from simple technical or tactical elements to complex elements. To master a skill or tactical maneuver that has already been learned, the process is reversed: The athlete and coach must analyze the whole skill or tactical maneuver by breaking it down into subunits to determine whether technical errors exist.
summary: Learning involves progressing from simple to complex elements, and mastery requires analyzing and refining each component.

content: If the athlete and coach determine that each subunit is free of technical faults, it is likely that errors exist in how the individual subunits are unified into the whole system. If the examination of the connections between the subunits does not reveal technical errors, further differentiation of the skill is necessary to isolate the sources of error. Once the sources of error are isolated, the coach and athlete must develop strategies to eliminate the error. The integration–differentiation process can be used to perfect or change the technical or tactical model being used.
summary: Identifying errors in skill execution involves analyzing both individual components and their integration into the whole.

content: If the coach determines that a technical skill or tactical maneuver is insufficient, it may be warranted to alter the performance model. The coach must determine why an error occurred and critically analyze the model to determine which components can be removed or modified. Determining technical errors occurs with the same differentiation process presented earlier. Once technical errors are isolated and the coach has decided that the model of performance must be altered, the technical error must be “unlearned” and a new technical skill or element taught. Once the athlete learns the new element or skill, she must practice the skill until it becomes automatic; then the skill is reintroduced into the whole system of performance, and the athlete practices the skill until it is mastered.
summary: Coaches modify performance models based on identified errors, teaching new skills and integrating them into the overall system.

content: When training an athlete, there is a constant trade-off between stability and variability. The optimal training stimulus occurs in response to a systematic variation in training load, intensity, or content. If, however, the training stimulus or workloads are prescribed in a monotonous fashion, the athlete will experience accommodation or stagnation problems. Preparation for training should include planned variation, whereby novel or seminovel tasks are introduced or reintroduced periodically throughout the annual training plan. The introduction of novel or seminovel tasks will result in a greater stimulatory effect and greater adaptation. Variability in training (e.g., changes to volume, load, exercises, and workout frequency) provides a stabilizing effect in regard to performance and skill acquisition.
summary: Training effectiveness requires balancing stability and variability, incorporating novel tasks and adjusting training parameters to prevent stagnation.

content: There is a constant conflict between the standardization of a skill set and the individual traits and characteristics of the athlete.
summary: Coaches must balance standardized training methods with individual athlete needs and characteristics.

content: Preparation for training should include planned variation, whereby novel or seminovel tasks are introduced or reintroduced periodically throughout the annual training plan. Variability in training (e.g., changes to volume, load, exercises, and workout frequency) provides a stabilizing effect in regard to performance and skill acquisition.  The introduction of novel or seminovel tasks will result in a greater stimulatory effect and greater adaptation. If, however, the training stimulus or workloads are prescribed in a monotonous fashion, the athlete will experience accommodation or stagnation problems.
summary: Training should incorporate planned variation and avoid monotony to optimize performance and skill acquisition.

content: The athlete’s ability to perfect technique and tactics is a direct result of the coach’s knowledge and teaching skill, which can include the use of preparatory and progressive drills and audiovisual aids. The athlete’s ability to learn new skills is also related to her ability to process new information and her biomotor abilities. There is a constant conflict between the standardization of a skill set and the individual traits and characteristics of the athlete. The coach must develop and stabilize the athlete’s technical skills while accounting for the individual’s psychological and biological characteristics.
summary: Coaches must effectively teach skills, considering individual athlete traits and abilities.

content: Athletes improve technical and tactical skills in three distinct stages. In the first stage, the main objective is to perfect the individual components and technical elements of a skill (differentiation). As the components are mastered, they are integrated progressively into the whole system. Skill perfection develops in concert with the development and perfection of the dominant or supporting biomotor abilities. The development of these biomotor abilities is essential because technique is a function of physical preparation or capacity.
summary: Skill development occurs in three stages: differentiation, integration, and refinement, alongside biomotor ability development.

content: The acquisition of new skills and techniques is best suited for the preparatory phase of the annual plan. When skill acquisition is a central focus, it is inadvisable for the athlete to participate in competitions. In the second stage, the main objective is to perfect the whole skill under standardized conditions similar to those seen during a competition. This can be accomplished by participating in either exhibition or simulated competitions. The athlete must maintain dominant biomotor abilities during this phase so he will have an adequate physical training base to continue skill development. This stage of perfecting a skill can be integrated into the annual training plan near the end of the preparatory phase.
summary: Skill acquisition is best prioritized during the preparatory phase, with simulated competitions used for refinement.

content: The final stage of perfecting a skill focuses on stabilizing the whole skill and translating it to competitive performance. The coach must create an environment (e.g., including noise, fatigue) to simulate competitive conditions. This stage of perfecting a skill should be implemented into the annual training plan during the competitive phase. If a coach fails to concentrate on correcting an athlete’s technical errors, they are only perfecting those errors. Often technical improvement or skill mastery is impaired because the athlete learns the skill incorrectly. If the technical skill is not taught correctly, the athlete’s ability to correct technical errors is greatly impaired. The coach must strive to eliminate as many technical errors as possible to maximize the athlete’s development.
summary: The final stage of skill development focuses on competition simulation and error correction.

content: Technical or tactical errors can occur for many reasons, but generally they fall into four broad areas. Many factors can impair an athlete’s ability to learn or perfect a skill. Two interrelated factors that impair an athlete’s ability to learn or perfect a skill are an insufficient physical training base and lack of correlation between biomotor abilities. A poorly developed physical training base or insufficient development of biomotor abilities can delay skill acquisition and development. Athletes who have not adequately developed a physical training base are more apt to develop fatigue when working on skill acquisition. Fatigue, which can result from a poor training base or a poorly implemented training plan, can impede learning or result in a deterioration of technical skills. Simply improving the athlete’s physical training base may improve her ability to learn new skills. The development of biomotor abilities may also facilitate the acquisition of skills.
summary: Technical and tactical errors can stem from various factors, including insufficient physical training and biomotor ability development.

content: One of the major biomotor abilities that can affect skill acquisition is strength. A gymnast may not be able to learn or master a specific element (e.g., iron cross) if he does not have the appropriate level of strength to perform or practice the skill. Therefore, simply increasing strength as part of the physical training base will increase the athlete’s ability to learn or master the targeted skill set.
summary: Strength is a crucial biomotor ability for skill acquisition.

content: Psychological factors, such as self-confidence, morale, desire, and beliefs, appear to be significantly related to the athlete’s ability to perform or develop skills. Athletes who set goals that are task oriented, such as working hard to perfect a skill, generally achieve greater success than athletes who are ego driven (i.e., perform for individual notoriety).
summary: Psychological factors, particularly task-oriented goals, influence skill development and performance.

content: One of the major biomotor abilities that can affect skill acquisition is strength. A gymnast may not be able to learn or master a specific element (e.g., iron cross) if he does not have the appropriate level of strength to perform or practice the skill. Therefore, simply increasing strength as part of the physical training base will increase the athlete’s ability to learn or master the targeted skill set.
summary: Strength is a crucial biomotor ability for skill acquisition, as demonstrated by a gymnast's need for strength to master elements.

content: Psychological factors, such as self-confidence, morale, desire, and beliefs, appear to be significantly related to the athlete’s ability to perform or develop skills. Athletes who set goals that are task oriented, such as working hard to perfect a skill, generally achieve greater success than athletes who are ego driven (i.e., perform for individual notoriety). Athletes who are ego driven tend to perceive failure as an inability to perform a task, which may result in a withdrawal from training. Conversely, task-oriented athletes will respond to failure by increasing their effort in training because they equate success with hard work.
summary: Psychological factors, particularly goal orientation, significantly influence skill development and response to setbacks.

content: The coach’s teaching method causes technical faults. The coach may use inappropriate teaching methods or demonstrate the technique incorrectly when introducing the skill or may fail to completely explain the technical aspects of the skill. Some coaches neglect to tailor skill instruction to the individual’s learning capacity and biomotor ability. Additionally, the coach’s personality, coaching style, and character can affect the athlete’s ability to acquire technical skills. If the coach does not allow the athlete sufficient time to learn a skill, the potential for developing technical errors is magnified.
summary: Coaching methods and factors, including time allocation and individualization, directly impact skill acquisition and error development.

content: The environment must foster the acquisition of proper technique, and the training session must be properly planned. Equipment must be appropriate and must function properly during each training session. Appropriate facilities (e.g., field, court) must be available for training, because an adverse environment can impair skills acquisition.
summary: The training environment, including equipment, facilities, and planning, significantly influences skill development.

content: Periodization is a phase of the annual plan. The stress of competition is absent during the periodization phase. Time during the periodization phase can be dedicated to addressing technical issues.
summary: Periodization, a phase in the annual plan, provides dedicated time for addressing technical issues without competitive stress.

content: Fatigue usually has a negative effect on learning. It is best to address technical errors or teach new skills immediately after the warm-up. Another strategy for addressing technical errors is to increase the amount of rest between repetitions of the drills.
summary: Fatigue negatively impacts learning, so addressing technical errors and teaching new skills after warm-up and with adequate rest is beneficial.

content: The first step in addressing a technical error is to isolate the error to be corrected from other technical skills. Once a technical error is isolated, the coach can introduce the correction or new element that will address the error. The athlete then practices the new skill. When the athlete has acquired or mastered the new skill, it is integrated back into the system or whole skill.
summary: Correcting technical errors involves isolating the issue, introducing corrections, practicing, and integrating the skill back into the whole.

content: While addressing technical errors, the athlete must maintain or develop the biomotor abilities that are necessary to support the skill being perfected. Another issue to consider when addressing technical errors is the intensity or velocity at which the exercises are performed. Coaches usually concentrate on correcting technique with low-intensity or low-velocity movements. Sporting events often occur at higher velocities and intensities. After the athlete becomes proficient at the new skill or corrected skill with low intensities and velocities, she must practice the skill at progressively higher velocities and intensities until the skill can be used in competition.
summary: Addressing technical errors requires maintaining biomotor abilities, considering intensity, and progressing to competitive velocities.

content: Visualization or mental practice is an excellent tool for correcting technical errors. The scientific literature has shown that athletes who use mental practice perform significantly better than those who do not. The coach should consider incorporating mental practice into the training plan to maximize the correction of technical errors and ultimately improve performance.
summary: Mental practice, supported by research, is a valuable tool for correcting technical errors and enhancing performance.

content: Whether athletes need to understand the theoretical basis of training and sport is of great debate. Some coaches believe that athletes only need to concern themselves with training and competing. Approaching the development of athletes in this fashion may delay the athlete’s skill and performance improvement. The coach should consider the development of the athlete, which includes educating the athlete about the sport, training theory, and why they are doing certain things in training.
summary: Educating athletes about training theory and sport science can contribute to their skill and performance development.

content: To effectively educate athletes, the coach must stay up-to-date with theoretical knowledge by reading sport science literature, attending sport science and coaching conferences, and interacting with other coaches. The coach should educate the athlete in the following areas: The rules and regulations governing the sport. The scientific basis for understanding and analyzing the technique of the sport because understanding basic biomechanics allows the athlete to analyze movement and ensure proper mechanics, thus decreasing the risk of injury.
summary: Coaches should stay current in sport science and educate athletes on rules, sport biomechanics, and injury prevention.

content: Approaching the development of athletes in this fashion may delay the athlete’s skill and performance improvement. The coach should consider the development of the athlete, which includes educating the athlete about the sport, training theory, and why they are doing certain things in training. To effectively educate athletes, the coach must stay up-to-date with theoretical knowledge by reading sport science literature, attending sport science and coaching conferences, and interacting with other coaches.
summary: Effective athlete development requires a holistic approach that includes education about the sport, training theory, and staying current with sport science.

content: The coach should educate the athlete in the following areas: The rules and regulations governing the sport. The scientific basis for understanding and analyzing the technique of the sport because understanding basic biomechanics allows the athlete to analyze movement and ensure proper mechanics, thus decreasing the risk of injury. The scientific and methodological basis of biomotor abilities. The planning of training and how periodization of training is used to prepare the athlete for competition. The physiological adaptations that occur in response to training.
summary: Coaches should educate athletes on various aspects of their sport, including rules, biomechanics, biomotor abilities, training planning, and physiological adaptations.

content: The coach should educate the athlete in the following areas: The causes, methods of prevention, and basic treatments for injuries. The sociology of sport (i.e., intergroup relationships). The psychological aspects of sport, including communication skills, behavior modification, stress management, and relaxation techniques. The effect of nutrition on training adaptations and how to use dietary interventions before, during, and after training or competition (with care undertaken to design a nutrition strategy by consulting a nutrition specialist).
summary: Athlete education should encompass injury prevention, sports sociology, psychology, and nutrition.

content: Developing the athlete’s theoretical knowledge about the sport and how to prepare for the sport is an ongoing process that should include discussions before, during, and after training. The process should include activities such as film analysis, where the coach teaches the athlete how to critically analyze performance parameters. Athletes should be encouraged to become students of their sport.
summary: Continuous education and active learning are crucial for athlete development.

content: This can be accomplished through attending clinics, interacting with other coaches and athletes, reading periodicals and other pertinent texts, and engaging in detailed discussions with their personal coach.
summary: Athletes can enhance their knowledge and skills through various avenues like clinics, networking, and self-study.

content: The preparation of athletes includes physical, technical, tactical, psychological, and theoretical training. Physical training is the foundation of every training program. An inadequately developed physical capacity will usually result in fatigue, which impairs technical and tactical performance during training and competition. Therefore, it is essential that the athlete’s physical capacity be addressed with sound physical training.
summary: Athlete preparation encompasses physical, technical, tactical, psychological, and theoretical training, with physical training serving as the foundation.

content: The athlete must continually strive to attain perfect technique. The more technically proficient an athlete, the more efficient she will be and the less energy she will expend during practice and performance. Technical skills also affect the athlete’s tactical capacity. Therefore, the training plan must provide for the continued development and refinement of technique.
summary: Continuous technical development is essential for efficiency, performance, and tactical proficiency.

content: The competitive game plan needs to be developed in advance of the competition to allow for the development of the tactical training plan. The coach should integrate tactical training into the training plan to allow adequate time for the athlete to perfect the tactics prior to competition.
summary: Tactical training should be integrated into the plan well in advance of competition to allow for sufficient practice and refinement.

content: The efficiency of a physical training program results from the manipulations of volume, intensity, and frequency. Volume refers to duration, distance, repetitions, or tonnage. Intensity refers to load, velocity, or power output. Frequency refers to the density of training.
summary: Training efficiency is influenced by manipulating volume, intensity, and frequency.

content: The coach must decide which training variable to emphasize to meet the performance objective. The training program should emphasize training variables in proportion to the athlete’s needs. Volume is a primary component of training because it is a prerequisite for high technical, tactical, and physical achievement.
summary: Training program design should prioritize specific variables based on the athlete's needs and performance goals.

content: The volume of training incorporates the time or duration of training, the distance covered or the tonnage in strength training, and the number of repetitions of an exercise or technical element an athlete performs in a given time. The most simplistic definition of volume is the total quantity of activity performed in training. Volume can also be considered the sum of work performed during a training session or phase.
summary: Training volume encompasses various aspects of training workload and is essential for achieving high performance.

content: The total volume of training must be quantified and monitored, given its impact on adaptations and the athlete’s ability to recover from training. The accurate assessment of training volume depends on the sport or activity. In endurance sports, the appropriate unit for determining training volume is the distance covered. In weightlifting or strength training, the tonnage or metric tons of training expressed in kilograms is the appropriate unit.
summary: Training volume should be carefully monitored and tailored to the specific sport and athlete's needs.

content: Volume can also be considered the sum of work performed during a training session or phase. The total volume of training must be quantified and monitored, given its impact on adaptations and the athlete’s ability to recover from training. The accurate assessment of training volume depends on the sport or activity. In endurance sports, the appropriate unit for determining training volume is the distance covered. In weightlifting or strength training, the tonnage or metric tons of training expressed in kilograms is the appropriate unit. Alternatively, a very practical unit for determining training volume is the total number of repetitions for each intensity zone. The number of repetitions can also be used to calculate volume in activities such as plyometrics, throws in baseball, and track and field.
summary: This chunk defines training volume, its importance, and various units for measuring it across different sports and activities.

content: Across an athlete’s career, the volume of training increases. Periodization is the process of stimulating physiological improvement to increase performance. Once the top level has been reached, physiological adaptations are further stimulated by increasing the amount of specific training within the annual plan, rather than indefinitely increasing training volume. An increase in volume over time is particularly important for the development of aerobic athletes. An increase in technical and tactical skills training over time is also necessary because high numbers of repetitions are needed to improve performance.
summary: This chunk discusses the progressive increase in training volume throughout an athlete's career and the role of periodization in optimizing training adaptations.

content: Three effective methods for increasing the athlete’s volume of training are increasing the frequency of training (i.e., density), increasing the volume within the training session, and increasing both the frequency of training and the volume within the training session. Researchers have suggested that it is important to increase the frequency of training as much as possible without inducing overtraining. Other researchers have definitively stated that more frequent training results in significantly greater training-induced adaptations. Increasing the number of training sessions in a single day appears to offer a physiological benefit. It is not uncommon for elite athletes to perform 6 to 12 training sessions per week with multiple sessions each training day.
summary: This chunk outlines strategies for increasing training volume, emphasizing the benefits of higher training frequency.

content: The athlete’s ability to recover from the training volume is the most important factor dictating how much volume is used in the training plan. Advanced athletes can tolerate high training volumes because they can recover more quickly from the training load. The preparatory phase in particular should not be turned into a test of the athlete’s work tolerance using general (not sport-specific) means, as this would lead to maladaptation.
summary: This chunk highlights the crucial role of recovery in determining training volume and cautions against excessive training in the preparatory phase.

content: The time that athletes spend training has consistently increased over the decades. Between 1970 and 2001, the volume of training increased by 22% in Norwegian international-class rowers. Mostly due to the professionalization of sports, more and more coaches in top and elite-level power sports have started to apply the methodological concept of minimum effective volume, which implies a smaller fluctuation of volume and intensity within the annual plan. Technology now allows for the optimization of training volume according to the athlete’s ability to recover and adapt; this also implies a smaller fluctuation of the athlete’s readiness status within the annual plan.
summary: This chunk examines the historical trend of increasing training volume and discusses modern approaches that emphasize individualized training plans and recovery optimization.

content: The planned training volume should take into account the sport, training objectives, athlete’s needs, athlete’s training age, athlete’s stage of development, and phase of the annual training plan.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of considering various factors when determining training volume.

content: Intensity, or the qualitative component of work an athlete performs, is another important training variable. Komi (39, 40) defined intensity in relation to power output (i.e., energy expenditure or work per unit of time), opposing force, or velocity of progression. According to this definition, the more work the athlete performs per unit of time, the higher the intensity. Intensity is a function of neuromuscular activation, with greater intensities (e.g., higher power outputs, higher external loads) requiring greater neuromuscular activation. The neuromuscular activation pattern will be dictated by external load, speed of performance, amount of fatigue developed, and type of exercise undertaken. An additional factor to consider is the psychological strain of an exercise.
summary: This chunk defines training intensity and its relationship to power output, neuromuscular activation, and psychological strain.

content: The assessment of intensity is specific to the exercise and the sport. Exercises that involve speed usually are assessed in meters per second, rate per minute, degrees per second, or power output (watts). When resistance is used in the activity, the intensity is typically quantified in kilograms, kilograms lifted 1 m against the force of gravity (kg/m), or power output (watts). In team sports, the intensity of play is often quantified as the average heart rate, heart rate in relation to anaerobic threshold, percentage of maximum heart rate (11, 29, 71), or, more accurately, metabolic power zones (57).
summary: This chunk explains how intensity is assessed differently depending on the type of exercise and sport.

content: The training program should include varied intensities within the microcycle. Training intensity can be quantified as a percentage of the best performance. The best performance would then represent a maximum intensity. If an athlete completes a 100 m dash in 10 s, which corresponds to a velocity of 10 m/s, and can generate a higher velocity (e.g., 10.2 m/s) over a shorter distance, the intensity would be considered supermaximal because it is more than 100% of the maximum velocity. Exercises performed with strength training loads greater than 105% of maximum would most likely be either isometric or eccentric muscle actions and thus would be considered supermaximal.
summary: This chunk discusses the importance of incorporating varied intensities in training and defines supermaximal intensity.

content: Training intensity can be quantified as a percentage of the best performance. The best performance would then represent a maximum intensity. If an athlete completes a 100 m dash in 10 s, which corresponds to a velocity of 10 m/s, and can generate a higher velocity (e.g., 10.2 m/s) over a shorter distance, the intensity would be considered supermaximal because it is more than 100% of the maximum velocity. Exercises performed with strength training loads greater than 105% of maximum would most likely be either isometric or eccentric muscle actions and thus would be considered supermaximal.
summary: This chunk defines training intensity as a percentage of best performance and explains the concept of supermaximal intensity exceeding 100%.

content: When training for endurance (e.g., 5,000-10,000 m), the athlete may run shorter distances at a much faster rate and thus may perform at intensities greater than 125% of the mean velocity achieved during the actual race.
summary: This chunk discusses how endurance training can involve intensities exceeding 125% of race pace.

content: An alternative method of evaluating intensity is based on the primary energy system engaged during the activity. A six-tier intensity classification can be constructed based on the biochemical responses to different types of exercise bouts.
summary: This chunk introduces an alternative method of classifying intensity based on the primary energy system used.

content: Exercises in intensity zone 1 last for up to 6 seconds (e.g., short accelerations, Olympic lifts, shot put throw, average play in American football, discus throw). Intensity zone 1 is marked by the highest power outputs and thus should be considered the highest intensity of exercise. The intensity of work in intensity zone 1 is substantially higher than the athlete’s V.O2max (maximal oxygen uptake). Work in intensity zone 1 requires any work to be supported primarily by anaerobic energy supply. The phosphagen (ATP-PC) system is the primary supplier of energy in intensity zone 1. The ATP-PC system is capable of supplying energy only for very short periods of time because it relies exclusively on muscular stores of ATP and phosphocreatine (PCr). The reliance on anaerobic energy supply creates a large oxygen deficit as a result of the rapid demand for energy that cannot be met by aerobic mechanisms. An increase in oxygen consumption, or what is termed the excess postexercise oxygen consumption (EPOC), occurs following exercise to replenish the ATP and PCr stores. Exercise performed in intensity zone 1 is usually limited by the muscular stores of ATP and PCr.
summary: This chunk describes intensity zone 1, characterized by very short durations (up to 6 seconds), highest power output, and reliance on the ATP-PC system.
