Platonov found that sequential integration lowered the probability of peaking at the most important competition of the year to just 5% to 15% (30).
summary: Different sports may benefit from varying periodization approaches, with sequential integration potentially less suitable for all sports.

content: The objectives, content, and methods of a strength training program will change throughout the phases of an annual training plan. These changes aim to maximize the development of the specific type of strength required in a sport while considering the individual characteristics of the athlete in order to achieve optimum performance. These changes also depend on the phase of the annual training program and the targeted physiological adaptations for that phase.
summary: Strength training programs should be dynamic, adapting to the specific needs and phases of an athlete's annual training plan.

content: After the transition phase, when most athletes do very little strength training, it is advisable to begin a strength program to build a foundation from which future training practices are developed. This is typically accomplished during the anatomical adaptation phase of a strength training program. In this early preparatory phase, several key objectives are targeted: Stimulate involvement of most muscle groups, including the stabilizer muscles. Increase short-term work capacity, which will reduce fatigue in the later stages of training when intensity of training and the volume of technique-oriented work are high. Begin to work on the technical aspects of the fundamental lifts that will represent the core of the strength training program. Prepare the muscles, ligaments, and tendons for the stressful activities to come and, consequently, produce an injury-free athlete.
summary: The anatomical adaptation phase is crucial for building a foundation of strength and preparing the body for more intense training.

content: When the preparatory phase, specifically the anatomical adaptation subphase, is inadequate, the risk of injury increases. This phase of a strength training program is a crucial part of the general preparation subphase of the preparatory phase. The anatomical adaptation phase is marked by a high volume of work (e.g., two or three sets of 8-20 repetitions) performed at low intensities (40%-65% of one-repetition maximum, or 1RM). The number of exercises performed will depend on the training experience of the athlete: Usually beginners will perform more exercises than experienced athletes. This subphase should last up to 12 weeks for beginners and 2 to 4 weeks for experienced athletes, in order to achieve the physiological adaptations targeted. For junior athletes or athletes who do not possess a strength training background, a longer anatomical adaptation phase (9-12 weeks) may be warranted; for professional team sports, 2 to 4 weeks could be sufficient.
summary: The anatomical adaptation phase should be tailored to the athlete's experience level and sport demands, emphasizing high volume and low intensity.

content: All sports require power (e.g., long jump), muscular endurance (e.g., 400-800 m swimming), or some combination of both (e.g., rowing, Nordic skiing). Both muscular power and endurance depend directly on maximum strength. Stronger athletes generally produce higher power outputs (38) and express higher levels of muscular endurance (26). Power is the product of both force and velocity.
summary: Maximum strength is foundational for both power and muscular endurance, which are essential for various sports.

content: All sports require power (e.g., long jump), muscular endurance (e.g., 400-800 m swimming), or some combination of both (e.g., rowing, Nordic skiing). Both muscular power and endurance depend directly on maximum strength.
summary: All sports necessitate power, endurance, or a combination thereof, with both power and endurance stemming from maximum strength.

content: Stronger athletes generally produce higher power outputs (38) and express higher levels of muscular endurance (26). Power is the product of both force and velocity.
summary: Stronger athletes exhibit greater power output and muscular endurance, as power is a product of force and velocity.

content: The maximum strength phase is a critical component of the preparation phase because it builds on the general adaptations stimulated in the anatomical adaptation phase and develops the neuromuscular attributes necessary for the development of muscular power (10, 43). The maximum strength phase can range from 1 to 3 months depending on the sport, the athlete’s needs, and the annual training plan. For athletes whose sports depend heavily on maximum strength, such as American football or shot put, this phase can be on the longer end of the spectrum (3 months). In a sport for which muscular endurance is the determinant of final performance, such as cycling, triathlon, or marathon canoeing, this phase can be shorter (1 month).
summary: The maximum strength phase, lasting 1-3 months, is crucial for developing power and neuromuscular attributes. Its duration varies based on the sport, with longer durations for strength-dependent sports and shorter durations for endurance-focused sports.

content: The development of maximum strength is best accomplished by working first on the development of the intermuscular coordination component of strength (2 to 6 repetitions with a load between 70% and 80% of 1RM), and then on the intramuscular component of strength (1 to 3 reps with a load between 80% and 90% of 1RM).
summary: Maximum strength development involves prioritizing intermuscular coordination (2-6 reps, 70-80% 1RM) before focusing on intramuscular strength (1-3 reps, 80-90% 1RM).

content: The conversion phase transforms maximum strength into power or muscle endurance (chapter 10). The conversion phase also provides a transition between the preparatory phase and competitive phase and equips athletes with the neuromuscular abilities vital for the achievement of the highest performance. The athlete will gradually convert the strength developed in the maximum strength phase into the type of power needed for the targeted sport. The appropriate power training methods for this phase include medicine balls, speed training, and plyometrics. Maximum strength levels must be maintained during this phase. If maximum strength levels decline, the ability to maximize power-generating capacity will also decline.
summary: The conversion phase bridges the preparatory and competitive phases, transforming maximum strength into sport-specific power or endurance. Maintaining maximum strength is crucial for power development.

content: If maximum strength levels decline during the competitive phase, speed and agility will also decrease. The physiological characteristics of the sport dictate the type of power or endurance that needs to be developed during this phase of training. Most sports require some combination of power and endurance. The ratio between power and endurance must match the demands of the sport. The ratio may be almost equal for a wrestler. Power or endurance would dominate for a canoeist competing in the 200 m and 500 m events. Muscular endurance should prevail for rowing (race duration 6-8 min), swimming events of longer duration (400-1,500 m), Nordic skiing, and the triathlon.
summary: The competitive phase requires maintaining maximum strength to preserve speed and agility. Power and endurance ratios vary based on the sport, with some emphasizing power, others endurance, and most requiring a balance.

content: This phase of the annual training plan is designed to maintain the physiological and performance standards achieved during previous phases. It is very difficult to maintain these gains. Strength has been shown to decrease across the competitive season, especially when inappropriate training methods are used. The maintenance phase must contain a high enough strength training volume to maintain strength gains while avoiding the development of high levels of fatigue. The training intensity for maximum strength is 60%-80% of 1RM. The training intensity for power is 30%-80%. The training intensity for muscular endurance is 30%-60%.
summary: The maintenance phase focuses on preserving performance standards achieved in previous phases, requiring sufficient training volume to avoid strength decline and fatigue. Training intensities vary based on the targeted attribute.

content: The maintenance program depends largely on the physiological requirements of the targeted sport. An American football player or shot putter would focus his strength training on maximum strength maintenance and power development. An endurance athlete would focus on power and endurance development. The breakdown of training sessions that target these attributes is difficult to recommend because it depends on the competitive season. Generally, the maintenance phase contains training units with a small number of exercises (two to five exercises) that are performed for two to four sets of one to six repetitions, with a wide range of training intensities.
summary: The maintenance phase's focus and training structure vary based on the sport's demands. American football and shot put athletes prioritize strength and power, while endurance athletes focus on power and endurance. Training sessions typically involve a few exercises performed for 2-4 sets of 1-6 reps with varying intensities.

content: A top soccer player will maintain strength and power by using three essential exercises: half squats, back hyperextensions, and toe raises. One may also add hip thrusts (glutes) to the training. The frequency of strength training during this phase can be 2 days per week, for a duration of 30 minutes. In individual power sports, a common approach is planning two power sessions, on Mondays and Fridays, and a strength maintenance session on Wednesdays, during the weeks that do not have a competition in the weekend.
summary: Soccer players maintain strength and power through exercises like half squats, back hyperextensions, toe raises, and hip thrusts, training 2 days per week for 30 minutes. Individual power sports often incorporate two power sessions and one strength maintenance session per week.

content: For junior athletes or athletes who do not possess a strength training background, a longer anatomical adaptation phase (9-12 weeks) may be warranted; for professional team sports, 2 to 4 weeks could be sufficient.
summary: The anatomical adaptation phase, crucial for building a foundation, lasts 9-12 weeks for junior athletes or those new to strength training, while professional team sports may require only 2-4 weeks.

content: The training intensity for muscular endurance is 30%-60%. A top soccer player will maintain strength and power by using three essential exercises: half squats, back hyperextensions, and toe raises. One may also add hip thrusts (glutes) to the training.
summary: This chunk describes the training intensity for muscular endurance and lists key exercises for a top soccer player.

content: The frequency of strength training during this phase can be 2 days per week, for a duration of 30 minutes. In individual power sports, a common approach is planning two power sessions, on Mondays and Fridays, and a strength maintenance session on Wednesdays, during the weeks that do not have a competition in the weekend. For the competition week, one or two low-volume power sessions are planned.
summary: This chunk outlines the frequency and structure of strength training for individual power sports, including adjustments for competition weeks.

content: It is usually recommended, mostly for individual sports, that the strength training program end 5 to 7 days prior to the main competition. This reduces the athlete’s level of cumulative fatigue, decreases levels of stress, and facilitates physiological and psychological supercompensation. This recommendation may not be adequate for all sports. Athletes in sports with a high strength-and power requirement (field events in track and field, wrestling) may benefit from simply reducing the number of sessions during the week prior to the major competition. Such a program would contain a very low volume and a medium intensity, but the program should be performed explosively.
summary: This chunk discusses the timing of strength training in relation to competitions, with specific recommendations for individual sports and those requiring high strength and power.

content: The compensation phase completes the annual training plan and coincides with the transition phase. The main objective of the transition phase is to remove fatigue and allow the athlete to recover (via the use of active rest) before initiating the next annual training plan. The preparatory phase is designed to induce regeneration. For injured athletes, the preparatory phase is used for rehabilitation and restoration of movement capacity. During the preparatory phase, all athletes should consider an active rest training plan that includes some strength training. The strength training during the preparatory phase should address the stabilizing musculature and target areas of weakness that could increase the athlete’s potential risk for injury. The preparatory phase is also the time to work on compensation of other muscle groups that are not primarily used during the competitive phase.
summary: This chunk outlines the phases of an annual training plan, focusing on the compensation, transition, and preparatory phases.

content: Power refers to the quick application of force against resistance. Agility is perceived as an athlete’s ability to swiftly accelerate and decelerate and quickly change directions during a game or other sports contests. Movement time refers to an athlete’s ability to quickly move a limb in the desired direction. Agility evolves from speed. An athlete will never be agile before being fast. An athlete will never be fast before first gaining strength. When an athlete develops a higher level of relative strength, he is capable of moving his body more quickly with less effort.
summary: This chunk defines power, agility, and movement time, highlighting the relationship between strength, speed, and agility.

content: The training methods required to produce a high level of maximum strength normally aim to develop the ability of the nervous system to recruit a high number of fast-twitch muscle fibers. The training methods required to produce a high level of maximum strength also expose the nervous system to training with low angular velocities. In order to maximize the power capability and, consequently, the agility of the athlete, it is necessary to follow the maximum strength phase with a conversion to a power and agility phase. Agility does not evolve from speed. Agility improves visibly only after maximum strength is developed. Speed will never increase without increasing maximum strength first.
summary: This chunk explains the training methods for developing maximum strength and the subsequent transition to power and agility training.

content: Athletes are exposed to heavy loads during the maximum strength phase at 70%-90% of 1RM. Fast-twitch muscle fibers are fully recruited into action in higher amounts during the maximum strength phase. This physiological adaptation ensures that the athlete will successfully overcome high resistance. Changes of directions (acceleration–deceleration) increase the discharge rate to fast-twitch muscle fibers.
summary: This chunk describes the physiological adaptations that occur during the maximum strength phase.

content: After 4 to 8 weeks of power and agility training, the athlete has maximized her abilities which increases her readiness for competitions. Both power and agility training have a positive effect on movement time, or the capacity of the athlete to move a limb in the desired direction. As the arms and legs become stronger and react faster to a given signal, the speed of movement increases. Another component of agility is perceptual decision making, which is related to reaction and movement time (e.g., visual scanning, anticipation, pattern recognition, and knowledge of situations). This component has been termed reactive agility and is marked by the ability to react to a situation; reactive agility appears to differentiate players of different levels in various sports.
summary: This chunk describes the benefits of power and agility training and the role of perceptual decision making in agility.

content: After 4 to 8 weeks of power and agility training, the athlete has maximized her abilities which increases her readiness for competitions. Both power and agility training have a positive effect on movement time, or the capacity of the athlete to move a limb in the desired direction. As the arms and legs become stronger and react faster to a given signal, the speed of movement increases.
summary: Power and agility training enhance movement time and readiness for competition.

content: Another component of agility is perceptual decision making, which is related to reaction and movement time (e.g., visual scanning, anticipation, pattern recognition, and knowledge of situations). This component has been termed reactive agility and is marked by the ability to react to a situation; reactive agility appears to differentiate players of different levels in various sports.
summary: Agility involves perceptual decision-making, contributing to reactive agility and differentiating player skill levels.

content: The periodization of speed depends on the characteristics of the sport, including the performance level and the competitive schedule. The development of speed for a team sport athlete is very different from that of a sprinter. Team sport athletes usually follow a monocycle annual plan, whereas sprinters usually undergo a bi-cycle annual training plan as a result of having both indoor and outdoor championship competitions.
summary: Speed training periodization varies based on sport type and competitive schedule.

content: Regardless of the type of individual or team sport, the periodization of speed training may follow several distinct subphases: a general speed phase; an acceleration phase; a maximum speed phase; and an anaerobic endurance phase. For team sports, there is also a specific speed, agility, and reactive agility phase, which directly follows the acceleration phase.
summary: Speed training periodization typically includes general speed, acceleration, maximum speed, anaerobic endurance, and sport-specific phases.

content: The first step in developing speed is to establish a physiological and technical base that provides the athlete with the skills needed for moving fast. This type of training is typical of the general preparation phase of the annual training plan and is designed to increase the technical proficiency of the athletes during the first part of the expression of speed (i.e., the acceleration phase). Technical improvement is achieved by incorporating coordination drills (such as skipping or dribbling) with speed training at various intensities: moderate intensity technical runs over step-hurdles (15-30 m), high-intensity uphill accelerations (5-30 m) with full or partial rest intervals, and heavy sled towing (20-30 m) with full recovery.
summary: Initial speed development focuses on establishing a physiological and technical base through drills and varied intensity training.

content: For track and field athletes, this is the time to train on the grass several times a week, always with flats. According to the complex integration approach, the general speed phase (speed) is planned simultaneously with the anatomical adaptation phase (strength) and the aerobic endurance phase (endurance).
summary: General speed training for track athletes involves grass training and simultaneous development of strength and aerobic endurance.

content: The general speed phase is followed by the acceleration phase. During the acceleration phase, short-distance sprints (5-30 m) are performed at high intensities (95%-100% of maximum), with long rest intervals between repetitions (1 to 4 min). Track athletes begin using spikes at this phase. Uphill short sprints or medium- to light-load sled towing can still be used.
summary: The acceleration phase focuses on short sprints at high intensity with long rest intervals, incorporating spikes and uphill sprints.

content: This type of training relies on the anaerobic alactic system. During these phases, the previous training means are used simultaneously with tempo training. Tempo training progresses from extensive to intensive, in order to elevate the general and anaerobic endurance capacity of the athlete, thereby progressively creating the metabolic adaptations necessary for the maximization of specific endurance.
summary: Acceleration phase training utilizes the anaerobic alactic system and incorporates tempo training to enhance endurance.

content: In sports where aerobic endurance is not part of its ergogenesis, such as sprinting in track and field, traditional aerobic endurance training is not a physiological necessity. Speed of movement or sprinting ability depends largely on the rate of energy supply. Methods that compromise anaerobic function are not recommended for sports where aerobic endurance is not part of its ergogenesis.
summary: Aerobic training is not essential for sports where sprinting is primary, and anaerobic function is crucial.

content: The metabolic adaptations induced by tempo training have a great degree of sport specificity and translate into a physiological improvement for many individual and team sports. Interval-based training methods have been reported to enhance both anaerobic and aerobic metabolism.
summary: Tempo and interval training improve metabolic adaptations specific to the sport, enhancing both anaerobic and aerobic metabolism.

content: As training progresses from the general preparatory to specific preparatory phase, additional sport-specific activities are incorporated. For team sports training, tactical drills of 2 to 5 minutes can be performed nonstop to develop sport-specific endurance. In soccer, sport-specific endurance can be developed via small-sided games (2 versus 2; 3 versus 3; 5 versus 5) or a soccer-specific dribble track.
summary: Training progresses to incorporate sport-specific activities and tactical drills to develop endurance.

content: As the competitive phase approaches, training becomes more intensive, event-specific, refined, and specialized. Training will include work designed to maximize speed, mostly via specific technical and tactical drills. Speed can be developed with short-distance sprints (40-80 m) performed at high intensities (90%-100% of maximum), with longer rest intervals between repetitions (3 to 8 min) and sets (6 to 20 min). This type of training will stress the anaerobic systems, especially the phosphagen system.
summary: Training intensifies as competition nears, focusing on event-specific drills and maximizing speed through high-intensity sprints.

content: It is paramount that the athlete develops speed before he starts training speed endurance. Speed is the neural prerequisite for speed endurance, in the same way that aerobic power precedes lactic capacity (anaerobic endurance). For team sport athletes, maximum speed training will also have an injury prevention function, especially for the hamstrings. Having maximum speed repetitions in training will ensure that the neuromuscular system will be ready if called upon for a long lasting maximum sprint during a game.
summary: Developing speed precedes speed endurance, and maximum speed training benefits injury prevention and neuromuscular readiness.

content: Speed endurance training uses various distances and rest intervals. The lactic system power and lactate tolerance can be targeted via the use of high-intensity (95%-100% of maximum) short-distance sprints (<80 m), performed with short rest intervals between repetitions (1 to 2 min) and longer rest between sets (6 to 20 min).
summary: Speed endurance training utilizes various distances and rest intervals, targeting lactic system power and tolerance.

content: Speed is the neural prerequisite for speed endurance, in the same way that aerobic power precedes lactic capacity (anaerobic endurance). For team sport athletes, maximum speed training will also have an injury prevention function, especially for the hamstrings. Having maximum speed repetitions in training will ensure that the neuromuscular system will be ready if called upon for a long lasting maximum sprint during a game.
summary: This chunk discusses the importance of maximum speed training for both performance and injury prevention in team sports.

content: Speed endurance training uses various distances and rest intervals. The lactic system power and lactate tolerance can be targeted via the use of high-intensity (95%-100% of maximum) short-distance sprints (<80 m), performed with short rest intervals between repetitions (1 to 2 min) and longer rest between sets (6 to 20 min). The lactic system power and lactate tolerance can also be targeted via the use of high intensity (95%-100% of maximum) moderate-distance sprints (120-200 m) with long rest intervals between repetitions (lactic power: 12-20 min, depending on the performance level, the targeted event, and the level of intensity; lactate tolerance: 3-6 min, depending on the performance level, the targeted event, and the level of intensity).
summary: This chunk describes different training methods for developing speed endurance, focusing on the lactic system.

content: For most team sports, specific speed can be described as acceleration with changes of direction. Sports periodization should include a phase dominated by linear accelerations at sport-specific distances and slightly longer. Strength and conditioning coaches should plan acceleration drills with cuts and changes of direction after a phase dominated by linear accelerations. Accelerations in team sports are usually less than 3 seconds or 20 meters long.
summary: This chunk explains the concept of specific speed in team sports and its place in periodization.

content: Rest intervals in the acceleration phase will be short between repetitions (30 seconds to 1 minute) and longer between sets (3 to 5 minutes). One way to measure and progress the muscular load of agility drills is to calculate the total degrees of change of direction in a session.
summary: This chunk details rest intervals and progression methods for acceleration and agility drills.

content: Reactive agility can be trained using visual and auditory stimuli (external cues) applied during complex agility drills. Multicolored cones can be laid down after an agility course (e.g., slalom runs), with the coach giving the directional cue by calling out a color. Another agility drill can be performed by placing four hoops at the start of the agility course, assigning a number to each, and then calling a random sequence of the four numbers, which the players have to reproduce by rapidly stepping inside the hoop with alternating feet. Various starting positions can be utilized for agility drills: facing the other way, lying down, sitting, etc.
summary: This chunk provides examples of agility drills that incorporate visual and auditory cues.

content: Endurance is developed in several distinct phases across the annual plan. Within an annual training plan that constrains one peak, endurance would be developed in three phases: (1) aerobic (oxidative) endurance, (2) aerobic and specific endurance, and (3) specific endurance (ergogenesis).
summary: This chunk outlines the three phases of endurance development within an annual training plan.

content: Aerobic (oxidative) endurance is developed throughout the transition phase and the early preparatory phase (1 to 3 months). Each sport requires slight alterations, as the aerobic endurance developed throughout this phase can be aerobic capacity, aerobic power, or both, depending on the specific endurance to be developed at later stages. Aerobic capacity can be achieved via the use of a uniform and steady method with moderate intensities, whereas aerobic power can be achieved via moderate- to high-intensity interval training. Individual sports that require medium or long endurance might start this phase by incorporating long, steady runs, followed by long, moderate-intensity intervals. For team sports, combat sports, racket sports, and individual sports that require only short endurance, it is appropriate to start with moderate-intensity, medium to short intervals and progress to high-intensity, short intervals at later stages.
summary: This chunk details the development of aerobic endurance during the transition and early preparatory phases.

content: The aerobic power developed in this phase is useful for power sports, as it allows the athlete to resynthesize phosphocreatine more rapidly between bouts of anaerobic efforts. The development of aerobic endurance provides the following benefits: Enhanced Cardiorespiratory Function, Increased capillarization, which allows for an increase in oxygen and nutrient delivery, Increased hemoglobin concentration, red blood cell number, and blood volume, Decreased submaximal heart rate and resting blood pressure, Increased maximal aerobic power (V. O2max), Increased cardiac output, Increased stroke volume, Increased blood flow to working muscles, Increased oxygen exchange at the lungs, Decreased submaximal respiratory rate.
summary: This chunk highlights the benefits of aerobic endurance development and its relevance to power sports.

content: Periodization enhances musculoskeletal system function. Increased type I fiber content is an adaptation resulting from periodization. Increased oxidative enzyme capacity is an adaptation resulting from periodization. Increased mitochondrial density and size is an adaptation resulting from periodization. Increased myoglobin concentration is an adaptation resulting from periodization. Increased muscular endurance capacity is an adaptation resulting from periodization. These adaptations improve endurance capacity as a result of more efficient use of fuel substrates (carbohydrate and fat). These adaptations are stimulated in response to the workload, especially the training volume.
summary: This chunk explains the positive effects of periodization on the musculoskeletal system and endurance capacity.

content: Aerobic and specific endurance are components of the endurance capacity of the athlete. Training plans should introduce endurance elements that target the dominant energy systems used in the sport after building an endurance base to maximize the development of specific endurance. Endurance is developed in this phase of training through the use of uniform, alternative, and interval training (short, medium, and long duration). During the early part of this phase, the emphasis is on aerobic endurance; in the later portion of this phase, the emphasis shifts to the development of specific endurance with the use of high-intensity interval training or sport-specific, interval-based training methods. The shift to specific endurance development allows for a transfer of training effects, which enhances performance gains during the competitive phase of the annual training plan. The development of event- or sport-specific endurance training coincides with the late preparatory and competitive phases of the annual training plan. The sport-specific activity is the preferred training mean during this phase.
summary: This chunk describes the development of aerobic and specific endurance throughout the training plan, culminating in sport-specific endurance training during the competitive phase.

content: Endurance is developed in this phase of training through the use of uniform, alternative, and interval training (short, medium, and long duration). During the early part of this phase, the emphasis is on aerobic endurance; in the later portion of this phase, the emphasis shifts to the development of specific endurance with the use of high-intensity interval training or sport-specific, interval-based training methods. The shift to specific endurance development allows for a transfer of training effects, which enhances performance gains during the competitive phase of the annual training plan. The development of event- or sport-specific endurance training coincides with the late preparatory and competitive phases of the annual training plan. The sport-specific activity is the preferred training mean during this phase.
summary: This phase focuses on developing endurance, transitioning from aerobic to sport-specific endurance for performance gains during competition.

content: The appropriate training parameters depend on the bioenergetic characteristics of the sport and the needs of the individual athlete. For many sports, the coach must emphasize training intensity so that it often exceeds competition or game intensity. Small-sided games and a soccer-specific ‘dribbling track’ can be used to couple tactical and conditioning activities in one training session for team sports like soccer. For combat sports, sparring can be performed alternating ‘under time’ days, during which the regular duration of rounds is fractioned, with short intra-round rest intervals, and long inter-rounds rest intervals. ‘Overtime’ days can be used during which the duration of rounds is longer than the duration used in official competitions. Both ‘undertime’ and ‘overtime’ session parameters can be progressed over time to match the specific requirements of the event.
summary: Training parameters are tailored to the sport and athlete, often exceeding competition intensity. Specific drills and sparring variations are used to enhance tactical and conditioning aspects.

content: Not all sessions should be high intensity and sport-specific; alternating intensities facilitates recovery between training sessions, leading to lower fatigue levels and an optimal peak for competitions. An athlete is a complex being who requires not only physical, technical, and tactical training, but also an integrated periodization, in which psychological training and nutrition plans are integrated with other activities.
summary: Training intensity should be varied to optimize recovery and performance. Athletes require a holistic approach encompassing physical, technical, tactical, psychological, and nutritional training.

content: To maximize the coach’s ability to construct and deliver appropriately designed training plans, an interdisciplinary team of experts may be needed, including sport scientists, sport psychologists, nutritionists, biomechanics, and sports medicine professionals. The integration of all training components into a comprehensive annual training plan requires the coach and sports enhancement team to evaluate the athlete and the training goals, which will allow them to appropriately sequence training factors. Depending on the phase of the periodization plan, the training emphasis will shift to develop specific characteristics and manage fatigue. A truly comprehensive plan includes dietary recommendations and psychological training.
summary: Effective training plans require collaboration between coaches and experts in various fields. A comprehensive plan integrates all training aspects, including nutrition and psychology, and adapts to the athlete's needs and training goals.

content: The text describes a 12-month integrated periodization plan for a speed and power sport. The plan includes three main training phases: Preparatory, Competitive, and Transition. The Preparatory phase is further divided into subphases: Preparatory, Specific, Precompetitive, and Official and league competitions. The Transition phase includes Unloading and Play, fun.
summary: A 12-month periodization plan is outlined for a speed and power sport, encompassing preparatory, competitive, and transition phases with specific subphases.

content: The periodization plan focuses on the development of speed, anaerobic and aerobic endurance, agility, reactive agility, and sport-specific preparations. The plan also includes periodization of strength, power, and mental training. Nutrition periodization is also included, with variations in carbohydrate and protein intake based on the training phase. The plan emphasizes the importance of integrating all aspects of training for optimal performance.
summary: The periodization plan targets various performance aspects, including speed, endurance, agility, and mental training, with integrated nutrition periodization for optimal performance.

content: The text highlights the historical evolution of training planning, citing examples from ancient Greece and Rome. The author emphasizes the importance of planning in coaching, stating that it is the most important tool a coach has. A well-organized and scientifically based training plan eliminates random and aimless training practices. The goal of the training plan is to stimulate specific physiological responses according to a structured program. Periodization is a planned design to achieve specific performance outcomes at appropriate times.
summary: The text emphasizes the historical significance and importance of training planning in coaching. A well-structured plan aims to elicit specific physiological responses and achieve performance goals through periodization.

content: Training consists of introducing a training stimulus, causing a physiological response, and inducing recovery for adaptation. The training process can be considered an adaptations management system with intense training periods interspersed with easier training or rest periods. Coaches must predict athletes' physiological and psychological responses to training stimuli and fatigue. Training planning involves systematically manipulating the training stimulus to optimize physiological adaptations.
summary: Training involves a cyclical process of stimulus, response, and recovery. Coaches must understand these adaptations and manipulate training stimuli to optimize performance.

content: The effectiveness of a training plan is largely dictated by the coach’s expertise and experience.
summary: A coach's expertise and experience are crucial for the effectiveness of a training plan.

content: Training consists of introducing a training stimulus, causing a physiological response, and inducing recovery for adaptation. The training process can be considered an adaptations management system with intense training periods interspersed with easier training or rest periods.
summary: Training involves a stimulus-response-recovery cycle, managed through periods of intensity and rest to optimize adaptations.

content: Coaches must predict athletes' physiological and psychological responses to training stimuli and fatigue. Training planning involves systematically manipulating the training stimulus to optimize physiological adaptations. The effectiveness of a training plan is largely dictated by the coach's expertise and experience.
summary: Coaches must understand athlete responses and strategically plan training to maximize adaptations, relying on their experience for effective planning.

content: Coaches should understand exercise physiology, training theory and methodology, athlete development, physiological and psychological responses to training, recovery techniques, nutrition, and motor learning. Training plans are adjusted based on objective and subjective data collected during training sessions.
summary: Effective coaching requires a broad knowledge base, including physiology, training principles, and athlete development, with plans adjusted based on collected data.

content: To create an effective training plan, coaches must establish a long-term path of development that optimizes the athlete's potential. Coaches must monitor athletes during training, competitions, and with specialized tests to adjust training stimuli. It can take 8 to 14 years of dedicated training to maximize an athlete's competitive performance.
summary: Coaches develop long-term plans to maximize athlete potential, continuously monitoring and adjusting training based on athlete progress and testing.

content: A long-term training plan guides the athlete's development over many years of athletic activities. A major goal of long-term planning is to facilitate the progressive and continual development of the athlete's motor potential, skills, and performance. The long-term training plan is a prognostication, and its success depends on the coach's ability to forecast future developments and implement appropriate training objectives at the correct times.
summary: Long-term plans guide athlete development over years, focusing on progressive skill and performance growth, requiring the coach to anticipate future needs and adjust training accordingly.

content: Annual training plans are extracted from a long-term training structure. A long-term training model for volleyball shows the ages to start or end work on specific abilities. A specific guide of training for a long-term training plan should start from early years of athletic involvement through all of the stages illustrated in figure 6.1 (Initiation, Athletic Formation, Specialization, and High Performance). Figure 6.1 exemplifies a hypothetical, long-term training plan for volleyball. The duration of the plan in Figure 6.1 is from ages 8 to 35.
summary: Long-term training models, like the one for volleyball, provide a framework for annual plans, outlining age-appropriate training stages from initiation to high performance.

content: The four phases of training listed in Figure 6.1 are initiation (8 to 15 years), athletic formation (15 to 18 years), specialization (18 to 21 years) and high performance (21 to 35 years). The main scope of training for the Initiation phase is skill acquisition. Technical skill is the factor that limits performance for young athletes and prevents the young athlete from progressing and enjoying a sporting activity. The main objective of the Initiation phase is to learn both technical and tactical skills.
summary: The long-term plan includes four phases: initiation (skill acquisition), athletic formation, specialization, and high performance, each with specific training focuses.

content: After the first few years of primarily transforming a beginner into a player (ages 15 to 18, and sometimes 1 to 2 years earlier), the coach should try to transform a player into an athlete. Following the end of the Athletic Formation phase, performance is affected by refinement of technical and tactical skills and the ability to improve sport-specific physical attributes. Transforming a player into a top athlete (strong, powerful, faster, more agile and reactive) capable of playing effectively for a longer period of time (specific endurance) is accomplished by utilizing training methods and means that start as multilateral and gradually increase in specificity throughout the phase.
summary: The training phases progress from beginner to athlete, focusing on skill development, physical attributes, and sport-specific training.

content: Specialization could be considered the road to High Performance, or the stage of refining the position-specific technical and tactical skills and physical qualities. During Specialization and High Performance, the limiting factors for performance are technical and tactical excellence, maximum strength, power, and agility (reaction) time.
summary: Specialization and high performance phases focus on refining position-specific skills and physical qualities, with technical and tactical mastery being key performance factors.

content: The more effective the coach’s training methodology, the higher the chances of the athletes reaching objectives of high performance. Following the High Performance phase, the determinant scope of training is not to have six volleyball players on the court, but to have six athletes playing volleyball!
summary: Effective coaching methodology is crucial for athletes to reach their full potential, and training should focus on developing well-rounded athletes beyond just sport-specific skills.

content: The suggested long-term plan in figure 6.1 refers to all elements of training: skill acquisition (technical and tactical), training (coordination, flexibility, agility, speed, strength, and endurance training), and the progression of competition.
summary: The long-term plan encompasses all aspects of training: skill development, physical conditioning, and competitive progression.

content: An often-overlooked but highly important part of the long-term plan is a comprehensive monitoring program in which periodic testing is conducted to track the athlete’s development. The inclusion of a monitoring program eliminates the random approach to training that is sometimes present in modern athlete development.
summary: A comprehensive monitoring program with regular testing is essential for tracking athlete progress and avoiding random training approaches.

content: An inclusive monitoring program allows the coach to examine the effects of the training program and quantify factors that scientific literature has shown to be related to performance. The testing program should be completely integrated into the training plan, contain tests that target the athlete’s development, and focus on the athlete’s objectives (i.e., performance outcomes).
summary: Monitoring programs allow coaches to evaluate training effectiveness and tailor testing to individual athlete needs and goals.

content: The testing battery for any sport should include tests that reveal the athlete’s progress toward her performance goals. Monitoring should occur continuously and at periodic intervals throughout the training year. Testing should be conducted at the beginning of each new training phase.
summary: Testing should be ongoing and integrated into the training plan, focusing on athlete progress towards performance goals.

content: Certain factors (e.g., resting heart rate, heart rate variability, mood status, sleep patterns, and force capability measured with a hand-grip dynamometer or accelerometer) should be monitored daily as they can yield information about the athlete’s recovery status and readiness for the planned training. The training log is a simple tool to track daily responses to training stimuli.
summary: Daily monitoring of factors like heart rate, mood, and sleep, along with training logs, provides insights into athlete recovery and readiness for training.

content: The analysis of daily training logs, coupled with data collected from specialized tests and performance results, will allow the coach to help the athlete maximize her performance gains.
summary: Analyzing training logs, test data, and performance results allows coaches to optimize training for maximum athlete performance gains.

content: Monitoring should occur continuously and at periodic intervals throughout the training year. Certain factors (e.g., resting heart rate, heart rate variability, mood status, sleep patterns, and force capability measured with a hand-grip dynamometer or accelerometer) should be monitored daily as they can yield information about the athlete’s recovery status and readiness for the planned training. The training log is a simple tool to track daily responses to training stimuli. The analysis of daily training logs, coupled with data collected from specialized tests and performance results, will allow the coach to help the athlete maximize her performance gains.
summary: Continuous monitoring of athlete factors, including daily logs, coupled with performance data, helps coaches optimize training.

content: Testing should be conducted at the beginning of each new training phase. Performance results and test data will reveal which training factors have not improved or have deteriorated as a result of the training plan. Training factors that fall behind the athlete’s mean development rate are the weakest links in training and must be addressed via a redistribution of training efforts.
summary: Regular testing at the start of each training phase helps identify areas for improvement and guide training adjustments.

content: In gymnastics, technical improvement depends on strength development. If a gymnast is not strong enough to perform a technical skill, the coach should increase training activities designed to improve muscular strength. The training program should be designed to target training factors that address the athlete’s weaknesses.
summary: Strength development is crucial for gymnasts, and training programs should address individual weaknesses.

content: The coach must carefully examine the results of the monitoring program to determine the athlete’s weaknesses. The coach’s ability to organize and use appropriate planning tools will dictate the success of the training programs.
summary: Coaches use monitoring data and planning tools to identify weaknesses and design effective training programs.

content: Many planning tools are available, including the individual training session, microcycle, macrocycle, annual training plan, and long-term training plan. Long-term plans (4 to 8 years) are essential for the multi-year development of young athletes and Olympic athletes.
summary: Various planning tools, from daily sessions to long-term plans, are used to structure athlete development.

content: The terms used to describe the planning of training are not consistent throughout the world. Russian (Matveyev, 36-38; Zatsiorsky, 63-64) and many American authors refer to the annual plan as the macrocycle. Tudor Bompa uses the term annual training plan. Annual plans are used as guides or training projections for the year to come. Especially in individual sports and for youth athletes, they are constructed in the context of the long-term training goals and plans established by the coach.
summary: Terminology for training plans varies, but annual plans serve as guides for yearly development.

content: The annual plan is divided into smaller phases, described by Russian scientists as mesocycles (Matveyev, 38). Bompa describes the phases as grosse or macrocycles, terminology employed by the Germans in their preparation for the 1936 Olympic Games. The macrocycle content (training direction) is based on the phase of the annual plan and is designed in concert with the development of the microcycle (small cycle, or kleine in German), which is the most important functional tool for planning.
summary: Annual plans are divided into phases (mesocycles, macrocycles) and aligned with the microcycle, the primary planning tool.

content: The fluctuation of the load within the macrocycle determines its structure. The microcycle is a short training cycle that can range from 3 to 7 days depending on the phase of training. An equally functional type of plan for a coach is the daily training session, where all the athletic activities are programmed.
summary: Macrocycle structure is influenced by load fluctuation, while the microcycle (3-7 days) and daily training sessions provide further detail.

content: During an individual training session or lesson, the coach explains the scope and methods used in the daily program and then attempts to apply them in training. The training session can be classified based on the tasks within the session.
summary: Daily training sessions involve explanation, application, and classification based on specific tasks.

content: There are four basic classifications of training sessions: learning, repetition, skill perfection, and assessment.
summary: Training sessions are categorized as learning, repetition, skill perfection, or assessment.

content: The athlete’s main task in a learning session is to acquire new skills or tactical maneuvers. The coach begins the learning session by explaining the objectives of the session and then directing the athlete through a warm-up. After the warm-up, the athlete focuses on acquiring a specific skill set. After completing the main portion of the learning session, the athlete cools down, and the coach gives the athlete pertinent feedback regarding his progress toward developing the skill.
summary: Learning sessions focus on acquiring new skills, involving explanation, warm-up, skill practice, and feedback.

content: A repetition session is very similar to a learning session, in that a specific skill set or tactical maneuver is practiced. The major difference between a repetition session and a learning session is that during the repetition session, the athlete continues to learn the skill and attempts to improve the skill set.
summary: Repetition sessions build upon learned skills, focusing on improvement and refinement.

content: The skill perfection session is an extension of the repetition session, in that the athlete attempts to improve the overall skill set. The major difference between a skill perfection session and a repetition session is that, at this point, the skill has been learned and the athlete now attempts to perfect the skill to maximize performance.
summary: Skill perfection sessions aim to refine and optimize previously learned skills for peak performance.

content: Assessment sessions should be conducted periodically. Assessment sessions can include tests that evaluate the athlete’s physiological responses to training and performance, or the sessions can include exhibition or practice competitions to gauge the athlete’s preparedness.
summary: Periodic assessment sessions evaluate athlete progress through physiological tests or practice competitions.

content: The major difference between a repetition session and a learning session is that during the repetition session, the athlete continues to learn the skill and attempts to improve the skill set. The skill perfection session is an extension of the repetition session, in that the athlete attempts to improve the overall skill set. The major difference between a skill perfection session and a repetition session is that, at this point, the skill has been learned and the athlete now attempts to perfect the skill to maximize performance.
summary: This chunk describes the progression of training sessions from learning a new skill to perfecting it.

content: Assessment sessions should be conducted periodically. Assessment sessions can include tests that evaluate the athlete’s physiological responses to training and performance, or the sessions can include exhibition or practice competitions to gauge the athlete’s preparedness. The results of assessment sessions can be used to fine-tune an athlete’s training or as a tool for selecting athletes for competitions.
summary: This chunk outlines the purpose and function of assessment sessions in athletic training.

content: The coach may organize training sessions in several forms to accommodate individuals and groups of athletes. Group sessions are organized for several athletes, although not necessarily an entire team. Individual sessions allow the coach to focus attention on one athlete to address physical or psychological problems. Individual sessions provide the coach the opportunity to precisely evaluate the individual athlete and adjust her skill development. Individual sessions are most appropriate during the preparatory phase of the annual plan.
summary: This chunk details the different types of training sessions a coach can organize.

content: Mixed sessions are a combination of group and individual sessions. In a mixed session, the whole team may warm up together and then divide into individualized training sessions. After completing the individualized portion of the training session, the athletes come back together to either practice as a team or cool down.
summary: This chunk explains the structure and flow of a mixed training session.

content: Training sessions generally last approximately 2 hours, although they can be prolonged up to over 3 hours, depending on the sport and the phase of the annual training plan. There is an inverted relationship between intensity and duration. Training sessions for team sports are usually consistent in duration, whereas training sessions for individual sports tend to vary in duration.
summary: This chunk discusses the typical duration of training sessions and how it can vary based on sport and training phase.

content: Training sessions fall into three categories of duration: short sessions (30 to 60 minutes), medium sessions (90 minutes to 2 hours), and long sessions (longer than 2 hours). The duration of the training session depends on the tasks that are required during the session, the activities being performed, and the athlete’s physical potential. A sprinter may perform a training session that lasts only 1 to 1.5 hours during the competitive phase, whereas a marathon runner may undertake a session that lasts 2 to 2.5 hours.
summary: This chunk provides a more detailed breakdown of training session durations and factors influencing them.

content: Training can also be broken into smaller subunits throughout the training day. When a training session is broken into smaller subunits, the total time spent training may be slightly longer, about 2 to 2.5 hours. A training session consists of several parts that are sequenced in a way that allows the coach and athlete to follow the principle of a progressive increase and decrease in work across the training session.
summary: This chunk describes the option of breaking down training into smaller subunits and the general structure of a training session.

content: A three-part training session contains a warm-up (preparation), a workout, and a cool-down (conclusion); a four-part training session consists of an introduction, a warm-up, a workout, and a cool-down. The selection of which structure to use in a training session depends on the training task and content, the phase of training, and the athlete’s training level.
summary: This chunk details the different structures of training sessions and factors influencing their choice.

content: Periodization is used during the preparatory phase of training for most athletes, especially beginners or novice athletes. Advanced athletes generally use the three-part model, especially during the competitive period of the annual training plan. The only substantial difference between the two structures is the inclusion of an introduction.
summary: This chunk explains the use of periodization and different training structures based on athlete experience and training phase.

content: The introduction of a training session is initiated by gathering the athletes into a group to facilitate communication. Managerial tasks undertaken during the introduction include taking attendance, relaying pertinent information to athletes' training or competitive schedules, and explaining the objectives of the training session. The coach should explain the methods that will be used to accomplish the training objectives. The coach must attempt to increase the athletes’ motivation and focus on the day’s training tasks.
summary: This chunk describes the purpose and content of the introduction phase of a training session.

content: After the general objectives are explained, the group should be broken into smaller subgroups according to individual or position-based goals. Beginner and novice athletes will require a 5 to 10 minute introduction; as athletes become more developed the length of the introduction can be reduced. The coach must always be prepared and organized when explaining the training objectives. Some coaches find it useful to use audiovisual aids or handouts explaining parts of the session. Handouts may cover specific goals or objectives that need to be highlighted in the session and may be considered an expansion of the overall training plan.
summary: This chunk provides further details on the introduction phase, including its duration and potential aids.

content: Athletes should receive the training program well in advance of the training session so they can familiarize themselves with and mentally prepare for the session. The training program should also be posted so that athletes can easily refer to it prior to and during the training session. Allowing the athletes to become familiar with the organization of the training session gives them a sense of shared responsibility and increases the likelihood that they will give a focused effort during training.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of providing athletes with the training program in advance and its benefits.

content: Some coaches find it useful to use audiovisual aids or handouts explaining parts of the session. Handouts may cover specific goals or objectives that need to be highlighted in the session and may be considered an expansion of the overall training plan. Athletes should receive the training program well in advance of the training session so they can familiarize themselves with and mentally prepare for the session. The training program should also be posted so that athletes can easily refer to it prior to and during the training session. Allowing the athletes to become familiar with the organization of the training session gives them a sense of shared responsibility and increases the likelihood that they will give a focused effort during training. It is also likely that this process will allow the athletes to develop dependability and willpower.
summary: Effective training session preparation involves providing athletes with clear information, including training programs and handouts, well in advance to promote understanding, mental preparation, and a sense of responsibility.

content: It is widely accepted by athletes and coaches that a warm-up is essential for optimal performance during either training or competition. The contemporary scientific literature supports this contention and suggests that the composition of the warm-up can affect the degree of performance improvement. It is well documented that an appropriate warm-up will improve muscle function and prepare the athlete for the demands of exercise or competition.
summary: A warm-up is crucial for performance enhancement, with scientific evidence supporting its benefits in improving muscle function and preparing athletes for training or competition.

content: Warm-ups can be broadly classified as either passive or active. A passive warm-up uses external means (e.g., saunas, hot showers, hot baths, heating pads, or diathermy) to elevate the muscle and core temperature without depleting energy substrates. An active warm-up uses some form of physical activity to elevate muscle and core temperature.
summary: Warm-ups can be categorized as passive, utilizing external heat sources, or active, employing physical activity to elevate body temperature.

content: The active warm-up can be further divided into general and specific components. General warm-up procedures include activities such as jogging, calisthenics, or cycling, whereas the specific warm-up component uses activities specific to the sport. For most athletes it is impractical to use passive warm-up procedures; therefore, athletes who are preparing for training or competition typically use active warm-ups.
summary: Active warm-ups consist of general and specific components. General warm-ups involve light activities, while specific warm-ups incorporate sport-specific movements.

content: The composition of a warm-up depends on factors such as the activity being prepared for, the athlete’s physical capacity, the environmental conditions, and the restrictions of the sporting activity. During the general warm-up, the athlete should participate in activities that will elevate muscle and core temperature, such as light jogging. For most athletes, the general warm-up should be performed at low intensity (40%-60% of V.O2max) for 5 to 10 minutes. A low intensity is recommended for the general warm-up because this level of activity will increase temperature while limiting the reduction in phosphate degradation. Temperature begins to elevate within 3 to 5 minutes of the initiation of the warm-up and plateaus at about 10 to 20 minutes.
summary: Warm-up composition is individualized based on factors like activity type, athlete fitness, and environmental conditions. General warm-ups, performed at low intensity, aim to elevate muscle and core temperature.

content: The coach may need to tailor the warm-up to the individual athlete because poorly conditioned athletes can achieve the same degree of temperature elevation with less warm-up time. Highly trained athletes may need to increase the duration and intensity of the warm-up to achieve the appropriate elevation of temperature. A good guide to determine whether the athlete is adequately warmed up is the presence of perspiration.
summary: Warm-up duration and intensity should be adjusted based on individual athlete fitness levels. Perspiration is a good indicator of adequate warming up.

content: After completing the general warm-up, the athlete should shift to a specific warm-up. A specific warm-up is designed to prepare the athlete for a certain activity. The warm-up features progressively increasing intensities and may involve postactivation potentiation activities in some instances. The inclusion of short, intense sprint activities has been shown to improve running and kayak performance provided sufficient recovery time is available between the cessation of the warm-up and the competition or training bout. At least 5 minutes of recovery is necessary when transitioning from the warm-up to training or competition.
summary: The specific warm-up focuses on preparing the athlete for their specific activity, gradually increasing intensity and potentially incorporating postactivation potentiation techniques. Sufficient recovery time is crucial after the warm-up.

content: A good guide to determine whether the athlete is adequately warmed up is the presence of perspiration. After completing the general warm-up, the athlete should shift to a specific warm-up. A specific warm-up is designed to prepare the athlete for a certain activity. The warm-up features progressively increasing intensities and may involve postactivation potentiation activities in some instances. The inclusion of short, intense sprint activities has been shown to improve running and kayak performance provided sufficient recovery time is available between the cessation of the warm-up and the competition or training bout. At least 5 minutes of recovery is necessary when transitioning from the warm-up to training or competition.
summary: A specific warm-up prepares the athlete for their activity, gradually increasing intensity and potentially incorporating postactivation potentiation techniques. Sufficient recovery time is crucial after the warm-up.

content: The specific warm-up is important when preparing for competition because it includes specific skill sets. A gymnast may include certain technical elements in the warm-up in order to prepare physically and mentally for the competition. It has been recommended that an athlete spend 10 to 15 minutes undergoing specific activities during the warm-up period. As the complexity of the sport increases, the athlete may need to spend more time on the specific warm-up.
summary: The specific warm-up is crucial for competition preparation, incorporating sport-specific skills and drills. The duration of the specific warm-up depends on the sport's complexity.

content: The total warm-up should take between 20 and 50 minutes and include a general warm-up designed to increase body temperature and a specific warm-up designed to prepare the athlete for his chosen sporting activity. The duration of the warm-up can be increased or decreased based on the athlete's needs and the demands of the activity. An athlete who participates in high-intensity activities such as sprinting may require up to 45 min to effectively warm up, although most of the activities will be interspersed with adequate rest intervals. An endurance athlete will have a shorter, yet denser warm-up because her metabolic systems are highly trained and her neuromuscular system does not need to be prepared for high-intensity activities.
summary: The total warm-up duration (20-50 minutes) encompasses both general and specific components, adjusted based on individual needs and activity demands. High-intensity athletes may require longer warm-ups, while endurance athletes benefit from shorter, denser warm-ups.

content: An athlete who is in poor shape will probably have to decrease the duration of the warm-up or use a lower frequency (longer rest intervals). One interesting application of the warm-up is to use longer warm-ups as conditioning tools during the preparatory phase of training.
summary: Warm-up duration and frequency can be adjusted for athletes with varying fitness levels. Longer warm-ups can also serve as conditioning tools during training.

content: The primary improvement associated with a warm-up is the elevation of muscle and core temperature. Some authors claim that the elevation in temperature likely increases the nerve conduction rate and speed metabolic reactions, which can then increase the speed and force of muscle contractions.
summary: The primary benefit of a warm-up is increased muscle and core temperature, which may enhance nerve conduction, metabolic reactions, and muscle contraction speed and force.

content: An endurance athlete will have a shorter, yet denser warm-up because her metabolic systems are highly trained and her neuromuscular system does not need to be prepared for high-intensity activities. An athlete who is in poor shape will probably have to decrease the duration of the warm-up or use a lower frequency (longer rest intervals).
summary: Warm-up duration and intensity are adjusted based on an athlete's fitness level.

content: One interesting application of the warm-up is to use longer warm-ups as conditioning tools during the preparatory phase of training.
summary: Longer warm-ups can serve as conditioning tools during training preparation.

content: The primary improvement associated with a warm-up is the elevation of muscle and core temperature. Some authors claim that the elevation in temperature likely increases the nerve conduction rate and speed metabolic reactions, which can then increase the speed and force of muscle contractions. As temperature elevates, the amount of oxygen that is delivered to the muscle increases as a result of increased vasodilation and blood flow. Of particular interest is a postactivation potentiating effect created by the warm-up protocol.
summary: A warm-up primarily elevates muscle and core temperature, which enhances nerve conduction, metabolic reactions, and oxygen delivery, leading to postactivation potentiation.

content: Postactivation potentiation has been defined as the increased contractile ability of a muscle after a contraction. Potentiation effects are most likely related to phosphorylation of myosin regulatory chains or an elevation of calcium (Ca2+) in the cytosol. The effects of postactivation potentiation appear to be most prevalent in strength and power performance. Some investigators have found that these effects are limited to highly trained athletes.
summary: Postactivation potentiation, the increased muscle contractility after a contraction, is linked to phosphorylation and calcium elevation, primarily benefiting strength and power performance in trained athletes.

content: Another warm-up–induced response involves the athlete’s psychological state. Investigators reported a lack of performance improvements when athletes were hypnotized to forget they had gone through a warm-up. Other investigators have shown that athletes who used mental imagery as a warm-up tool demonstrated enhanced physiological performance characteristics. It appears that the warm-up prepares the athlete not only physiologically but also psychologically.
summary: A warm-up positively impacts an athlete's psychological state, enhancing performance through mental imagery and preparation.

content: An appropriate warm-up reduces rates of injury. Increased temperature during a warm-up reduces the potential for injury. A 1 °C (33.8 °F) increase in temperature reduces the occurrence of skeletal muscle injury.
summary: A warm-up reduces injury risk by increasing muscle temperature.

content: Contemporary scientific literature has demonstrated that dynamic stretching is superior to static stretching during the warm-up period. Performance decrements associated with static stretching are most noted in strength and power sports, but static stretching decreases performance in all activities. The force production inhibitory effect of static stretching can be avoided if it is placed at the end of the general component of the warm-up. The progressively increasing intensity of the following exercises will dissipate the inhibition of static stretching.
summary: Dynamic stretching is superior to static stretching during warm-ups, as static stretching can inhibit performance. Placing static stretching at the end of the warm-up and progressively increasing exercise intensity can mitigate this inhibition.

content: The warm-up should include dynamic stretching activities that involve sport-specific movements, whereas attempts to increase flexibility should be reserved for the cool-down. The gluteus maximus is the primary hip extensor. The gluteus maximus operates in conjunction with the semimembranosus muscle, the semitendinosus muscle, and long head of the biceps femoris as synergistic movers.
summary: Warm-ups should focus on dynamic, sport-specific movements, while flexibility exercises are better suited for the cool-down. The gluteus maximus is the primary hip extensor, working synergistically with other muscles.

content: An increasing number of people suffer from what Stuart McGill calls ‘gluteal amnesia,’ which can be found in several populations of athletes. Gluteal amnesia is defined as the inability to engage the glutei in the extension of the hip. Gluteal amnesia transfers a higher workload to the hamstrings and low back. Gluteal amnesia could be one of the main reasons for the high incidence of hamstring injuries in sporting activities, especially in the presence of high eccentric loads.
summary: Gluteal amnesia, the inability to engage the gluteus muscles, increases hamstring and lower back strain, potentially contributing to hamstring injuries.

content: We suggest the implementation of glutei activation exercises within the warm-up, possibly before the athletic drills. Glutei activation exercises include a straight-knee supine march, one or two leg bridges, and quadruped hip extensions with bent knee.
summary: Incorporating gluteal activation exercises into the warm-up can help address gluteal amnesia and reduce injury risk.

content: A variety of athletic drills should be considered and used as dynamic stretches, technical refinement drills, and movement assessment tools. Athletic drills include skipping, shuffling, lateral walking, crossover, and backward walks, backpedaling, rear kicks, straight-knee runs, and cariocas.
summary: Athletic drills serve multiple purposes: dynamic stretching, technical refinement, and movement assessment.

content: The use of these drills as movement assessment tools is often overlooked by coaches who rely on static exercises for testing batteries. Coaches should assess movement quality at every training session via athletic drills, speed training, or technical-tactical training.
summary: Coaches should regularly assess movement quality through drills, speed training, and technical-tactical training.

content: Specific training objectives are addressed in the main body of the training session. The athlete learns new skills and tactical maneuvers, develops specific biomotor abilities, and enhances psychological qualities in the main body of the training session. The content of the main body of the training session depends on many factors including the athlete’s training status, gender, and age; the sporting activity; and the phase of training. The main body of the training session may stress technique while simultaneously focusing on biomotor abilities and psychological traits.
summary: The main training session focuses on specific objectives, including skill development, biomotor ability enhancement, and psychological training, tailored to the athlete's individual needs and training phase.

content: Coaches should assess movement quality at every training session via athletic drills, speed training, or technical-tactical training. The athlete should be well rested when learning new skills, and technical and tactical elements should be addressed early in the main body of the training session. Fatigue will impede the athlete’s ability to master motor skills (46). When the athlete is attempting to sequence learning or perfect technical and tactical elements, she should consolidate elements or skills acquired in previous lessons. She should work on perfecting the most important technical elements or skills and then conclude by applying those skills in simulated competitions. If perfecting a technique requires heavy, fatiguing work, the athlete should perform these activities later in the main body of the training session. It is advisable to precede these activities with speed exercises.
summary: Coaches should assess movement quality throughout training and prioritize learning new skills and perfecting techniques early in the session when the athlete is well-rested.

content: The content of the main body of the training session depends on many factors including the athlete’s training status, gender, and age; the sporting activity; and the phase of training. The main body of the training session may stress technique while simultaneously focusing on biomotor abilities and psychological traits. For less advanced athletes, a general recommendation for the main body of the training session is: 1. Learn and perfect a technical or tactical element. 2. Develop speed and agility. 3. Develop strength. 4. Develop endurance.
summary: The structure of the main training session is tailored to the athlete's individual needs, experience level, and training goals.

content: Activities that are designed to improve speed and agility are usually of a high intensity and are undertaken for relatively short durations. These types of exercise are extremely taxing, and the athlete should perform them when well rested. These activities usually are performed in the early part of the session (when the athlete is still fresh) and precede both strength and endurance training. If speed development is the primary focus of the session, then speed-based exercises should immediately follow the warm-up. If coordination or agility is the major focus, it should be addressed early in the training session because fatigue can significantly affect motor skills (46).
summary: Speed and agility exercises are high-intensity and should be performed early in the training session when the athlete is fresh.

content: Strength-developing exercises usually are placed after technique development and speed exercises. Several studies suggest that performing a heavy-load strength exercise (70%-90% of one-repetition maximum) prior to sprint activities results in a postactivation potentiation response that manifests itself as an increase in running velocity (40, 61).
