This technique appears to be very effective at increasing sprint performance if the volume of the strength activity is low. This method of sequencing strength training only appears to be effective with highly trained athletes (12).
summary: Strength training is typically performed after technique and speed development, and pre-sprint heavy lifting can enhance sprint performance in trained athletes.

content: Exercises for developing general or specific endurance should be placed in the last portion of the main body of the training session. These activities generate large amounts of fatigue, which will impede the athlete’s ability to acquire or perfect movement or tactical skills, develop speed and agility, and maximize strength development. This sequencing should not be confused with the strategy of practicing certain drills under fatigue to prepare the athlete for a specific game scenario.
summary: Endurance exercises should be placed last in the training session to avoid hindering other training objectives.

content: If learning (which requires a high level of concentration) is the dominant objective for beginning athletes, the training sequence should be technique, speed, strength, and endurance. A single training session should rarely include all four of these elements. This same general sequence for training can be used with elite athletes; however, advanced athletes may benefit from altering the order of training depending on the objectives of the individual training session and the objectives of the microcycle. The structure of the main body of training will be dictated by the objectives established for the individual training session. Each training session should focus on only two or three objectives, because it is very difficult to effectively target more than three training objectives. Trying to target too many objectives will likely impair the athlete’s rate of improvement and may result in the occurrence of overtraining. The individual training session objectives should be linked to the microcycle and macrocycle objectives, the athlete’s performance level, and the athlete’s potential. Although it may be advisable to plan objectives derived from different training factors (e.g., technical, tactical, physical, or psychological), these factors should be chosen based on the needs of the sport and the abilities of the athlete.
summary: Training sequences can be adapted for different athlete levels, prioritizing specific skills and focusing on 2-3 objectives per session.

content: The coach can plan 15 to 20 min of supplementary physical development, or what is sometimes referred to as a conditioning program, to take place after the athlete has achieved the objectives of a given training session. This addition should be considered for less demanding training sessions that do not challenge the athlete. This supplementary development should be specific to the dominant biomotor abilities of the sport and should address factors that are limiting the athlete’s rate of improvement.
summary: Supplementary conditioning can be added to training sessions to address specific weaknesses and enhance athletic development.

content: The cool-down period begins the recovery process and facilitates the body’s return to homeostasis. The postexercise recovery period is a time when the body must remove waste products, replenish energy stores, and initiate tissue repair. Depending on the intensity and volume of the session, recovery can require up to 48 hours. To initiate and speed up the posttraining or postcompetition recovery, the athlete needs to undergo a structured cool-down session that is designed to stimulate recovery. The cool-down should last for 20 to 40 minutes and consist of two major parts.
summary: The cool-down period initiates recovery by facilitating the body's return to homeostasis and should last 20-40 minutes.

content: The cool-down period begins the recovery process and facilitates the body’s return to homeostasis. The postexercise recovery period is a time when the body must remove waste products, replenish energy stores, and initiate tissue repair. Depending on the intensity and volume of the session, recovery can require up to 48 hours. To initiate and speed up the posttraining or postcompetition recovery, the athlete needs to undergo a structured cool-down session that is designed to stimulate recovery.
summary: The cool-down period initiates recovery by helping the body return to homeostasis after exercise.

content: The cool-down should last for 20 to 40 minutes and consist of two major parts. The first part of the cool-down entails active recovery exercise and lasts for 10 to 20 minutes. This active recovery exercise should be of a low intensity (<50% of the athlete’s maximum heart rate). Although limited data are available in the scientific literature, it appears that active recovery is much more effective in inducing postexercise recovery than is passive recovery.
summary: A structured cool-down consists of 20-40 minutes of active recovery followed by stretching.

content: The second part of the cool-down should contain 10 to 20 minutes of stretching. Although static stretching is not usually recommended during the warm-up, the cool-down is an excellent time for this activity. There are several reasons to include stretching in the postexercise period. First, stretching brings the muscles back to their anatomical length progressively, facilitates metabolic exchanges, and speeds up recovery processes. Furthermore, stretching can improve flexibility without compromising performance. It appears that the stretching portion of a cool-down can improve range of motion (flexibility) as a result of increased muscle temperature. Second, the inclusion of a postexercise stretching protocol has been demonstrated to reduce the onset of muscle soreness that can be stimulated by a training session. Third, it has been reported that the combination of active recovery followed by a period of stretching significantly increases the rate of recovery from training and competitive stress.
summary: Stretching during the cool-down improves flexibility, reduces muscle soreness, and speeds up recovery.

content: The length of a training session depends on many factors, but sessions usually last about 2 hours. The duration of each component of the session will depend on the type of session; the athlete’s age, gender, stage of development, and athletic experience; the characteristics of the sport; and the phase of training in which the session occurs.
summary: Training sessions typically last 2 hours, with component durations varying based on factors like sport, athlete characteristics, and training phase.

content: Fatigue is a multifactorial response to some sort of exercise, training, or competitive stress. Conceptually fatigue is defined as an acute impairment in exercise performance, which ultimately can impair the ability to produce maximal force or control motor function. Fatigue can occur in response to an exercise bout or competition when one or more of the following situations arise: a reduction in energy substrate availability, an accumulation of metabolic by-products, neuromuscular transmission failure, impairments in Ca2+ handling by the sarcoplasmic reticulum, central disruption, and a response to conscious perception.
summary: Fatigue is a multifactorial impairment in exercise performance caused by various factors like energy depletion, metabolic byproducts, and central nervous system changes.

content: Research exploring peripheral fatigue has centered on the availability of fuel substrates during a bout of exercise or competition. When exercise bouts are intense or undertaken for long durations, the availability of fuel substrates such as carbohydrates can become compromised. This can substantially reduce the athlete’s ability to maintain high levels of performance. The second type of fatigue that is discussed in the literature, central fatigue, is related to the brain. Central fatigue is often associated with a failure of the central nervous system to recruit skeletal muscle. It appears that fatigue-induced alterations in neurotransmitters such as dopamine, serotonin, and possibly acetylcholine have the potential to alter the ability of neural impulses to reach the muscle. For example, it has been suggested that exercise-induced increases in serotonin (5-HT) can lead to central fatigue and possibly mental fatigue, which could impair performance.
summary: Fatigue can be peripheral, caused by depleted fuel substrates, or central, caused by the brain's reduced ability to recruit muscles.

content: Along these lines, it appears that the conscious perception of body functions such as breathing, increased cardiac output (pounding of the heart), body temperature, and sweating can affect fatigue. These cognitive sensations also are believed to affect motivation, which appears to be linked to previous exposure to similar situations. It is clear that both central and peripheral fatigue can accumulate in response to a training or competitive bout.
summary: Conscious perception of bodily sensations and motivation can also contribute to fatigue.

content: Gandelsman and Smirnov (21) suggested there are two major phases of fatigue: latent and evident. In the early portions of a competition or training bout, physiological changes occur in order to meet the demands of the exercise bout. During this phase, latent fatigue can result, in response to the increased neuromuscular activity and metabolic stress stimulated by the bout of exercise. If the exercise bout is prolonged at the same intensity, fatigue will accumulate, resulting in the occurrence of evident fatigue. Consequently, the athlete’s ability to maintain maximal work capacity will progressively decrease.
summary: Fatigue develops in two phases: latent, occurring early in exercise, and evident, accumulating over prolonged exertion.

content: Many strategies can be used to deal with fatigue, ranging from modifying the structure of the training session to including dietary supplements to offset reductions in energy substrates. For example, increasing the length of the rest interval can decrease the occurrence of latent fatigue, which may facilitate the development of specific biomotor abilities such as power. Under some circumstances, training under conditions of latent fatigue can help prepare the athlete for the end of competitions, when fatigue is high. Such training also may enable the athlete to develop the psychological tools to deal with an accumulation of training-induced latent fatigue, which may improve her performance in the later stages of a competition.
summary: Various strategies can manage fatigue, including modifying training structure, using supplements, and training under fatigue conditions to build resilience.

content: The athlete’s ability to dissipate fatigue and recover from a training session or microcycle of intense training depends on many factors. The athlete’s physical preparedness and training age may dictate his ability to tolerate training. For example, an athlete who lacks the appropriate physical development will experience greater levels of fatigue, which may result in an inability to tolerate training stress. Therefore, the coach must modify the training lesson to accommodate this athlete’s deficiencies. This process may require more variations in the training plan with greater fluctuations in training intensity and volume in an attempt to dissipate fatigue and stimulate restoration.
summary: An athlete's recovery depends on their physical preparedness and training age. Coaches must adapt training plans to individual needs, potentially modifying intensity and volume to manage fatigue.

content: The intensity of the training session will affect the amount of fatigue that is developed, and the session should be structured to address this. A session that contains high-intensity activities should have only a few objectives and be of short duration. Conversely, a session containing lower-intensity bouts of exercise could have multiple training objectives and last longer. For example, the session may focus on perfecting a technical element, incorporating those elements into the team’s tactical scheme, and doing tactical drills with a high endurance component.
summary: Training session structure should consider intensity. High-intensity sessions should be shorter and focused, while lower-intensity sessions can be longer and incorporate multiple objectives.

content: Even with this format, athletes can experience latent fatigue as a result of the metabolic disturbances stimulated by the volume of work.  Consequently, the athlete’s ability to maintain maximal work capacity will progressively decrease. Many strategies can be used to deal with fatigue, ranging from modifying the structure of the training session to including dietary supplements to offset reductions in energy substrates. For example, increasing the length of the rest interval can decrease the occurrence of latent fatigue, which may facilitate the development of specific biomotor abilities such as power. Under some circumstances, training under conditions of latent fatigue can help prepare the athlete for the end of competitions, when fatigue is high. Such training also may enable the athlete to develop the psychological tools to deal with an accumulation of training-induced latent fatigue, which may improve her performance in the later stages of a competition.
summary: Athletes can experience fatigue even with structured training. Strategies include modifying session structure, dietary supplements, and training under fatigue to prepare for competition.

content: Strategies to deal with evident fatigue can include a structured cool-down period, as outlined previously in this chapter. Recovery techniques such as massage and contrast baths may alleviate this form of fatigue as well.
summary: Evident fatigue can be addressed with cool-down periods and recovery techniques like massage and contrast baths.

content: The greater the training intensity and volume, the greater the accumulated fatigue and the more time needed before readiness is elevated. Most athletes want to maximize the time spent training while minimizing the occurrence of overtraining. One way to maximize training time and increase training volume is to use supplementary training sessions, which can consist of individual training sessions or special group sessions, such as training camps. These training sessions can be performed during the early morning, before school or work. It is probably advisable that the athlete consume a small amount of food before training, especially when the session lasts longer than 30 minutes. The time spent in these sessions depends on the athlete’s schedule. Even though each session may be short in duration (30-60 minutes), over the course of the training year these small increases in training volume can result in a substantial increase in yearly training volume. An athlete who trains an extra 30 to 60 minutes each day will accumulate an additional 150 to 300 training hours per year.
summary: Athletes can maximize training time with supplementary sessions, which can be incorporated into their schedule. These sessions, even if short, contribute significantly to yearly training volume.

content: Although these sessions are considered supplemental, they must fit into the structure of the training plan designed by the coach. The coach will prescribe the content and dosage of these sessions in accordance with the athlete’s objectives, weaknesses, and phase of training. These sessions, 20 to 40 minutes in duration, can be structured to improve the athlete’s general endurance, general or specific flexibility, and even general or specific strength. One use for supplemental training sessions might be to improve an area of weakness to enhance certain abilities. An athlete who lacks flexibility can undertake a supplemental training session that directly targets flexibility. Any resulting improvement in flexibility could transfer to improve a technical aspect that the athlete is attempting to perfect.
summary: Supplemental training sessions should align with the overall training plan and address individual athlete needs. They can target various aspects like endurance, flexibility, and strength.

content: A supplemental training session lends itself best to a three-part structure. These sessions contain the major components of a three-part training session: warm-up, main body, and cool-down. The goal and format of each session are no different than those of a regular training session. The main body of these sessions should have no more than two objectives; one objective is optimal.
summary: Supplemental training sessions follow a standard three-part structure (warm-up, main body, cool-down) with a focus on 1-2 objectives.

content: The training plan is a tool utilized by the coach to structure and guide the training session. The written version of the training plan should contain all pertinent information and be easy to follow. It is advisable to give the athlete a copy of the training plan well in advance of the training session, allowing the athlete to prepare for the training session mentally and physically.
summary: A well-structured training plan, shared with the athlete in advance, guides training sessions and allows for mental and physical preparation.

content: The goal and format of each session are no different than those of a regular training session. The training plan is a tool utilized by the coach to structure and guide the training session. The written version of the training plan should contain all pertinent information and be easy to follow. It is advisable to give the athlete a copy of the training plan well in advance of the training session, allowing the athlete to prepare for the training session mentally and physically. The coach should briefly introduce the plan during the introduction portion of the session and, if space is available, post the day’s training plan so that the athletes can refer to it during the session.
summary: The training plan should be comprehensive, accessible, and introduced to athletes in advance.

content: There are many formats by which the training plan can be presented, but it should contain some basic elements. One of the most important items to include in a training plan for an individual session is the objective of that session. The session’s objective guides the training session, allowing the athlete to understand what happens during the training session. The plan should include the date and location of the training session as well as the equipment needed. The training plan should specify the exercise, drills, and activities that the athlete will perform.
summary: A training plan should clearly outline the session's objective, date, location, equipment, and activities.

content: The training plan should provide a detailed explanation of the dosage (repetitions, sets, duration) and intensity (percentage maximum strength, heart rate range, time, and power) of the training session. The training plan should include a section detailing the most difficult drills an athlete can perform during the lesson. The training plan should note items that the athlete needs to focus on while performing the drills and exercises.
summary: The training plan should specify the dosage, intensity, and focus points for each exercise and drill.

content: The length of a session plan will depend on the sport and the coach’s experience. Inexperienced coaches need to be as specific as possible and include as much information as possible in the training plan. More experienced coaches may be able to get away with a more generalized training plan.
summary: The level of detail in a training plan should be tailored to the coach's experience.

content: Research by Häkkinen and Kallinen (27) suggests that splitting the day’s training volume into two shorter sessions results in a greater improvement in performance, compared with performing one long training session. Long training sessions decrease the quality of training because they elicit large amounts of fatigue. The increase in fatigue appears to decrease the athlete’s ability to develop biomotor abilities and perfect technical and tactical skills. The daily training volume should be broken into smaller subunits to maximally develop the athlete’s capabilities.
summary: Splitting training volume into shorter sessions is more effective than long sessions due to reduced fatigue.

content: The actual structure of the training day depends on many factors, including the time available for training, the developmental status of the athlete, and the availability of training facilities. If the athlete is attending a training camp, the frequency of training sessions may be substantially greater.
summary: The daily training structure is influenced by factors like time, athlete development, and training facilities.

content: Two training sessions per day are scheduled from 7:30 p.m.–8:00 p.m. for travel home, 8:00 p.m.–8:30 p.m. for dinner, and 8:30 p.m.–10:00 p.m. for free time. Sleep occurs from 10:00 p.m.–5:30 a.m.
summary: Example schedule for two training sessions per day.

content: Three training sessions per day begin with waking up at 6:30 a.m. The first training session takes place from 7:00 a.m.–8:00 a.m. Breakfast is eaten from 8:30 a.m.–9:00 a.m. Rest occurs from 9:00 a.m.–10:00 a.m. The second training session is from 10:00 a.m.–noon. Rest and recovery sessions are scheduled from 12:00 p.m.–1:00 p.m. Lunch is eaten from 1:00 p.m.–2:00 p.m. Rest occurs from 2:00 p.m.–4:00 p.m. The third training session is from 4:00 p.m.–6:00 p.m. Rest and recovery sessions are scheduled from 6:00 p.m.–7:00 p.m. Dinner is eaten from 7:00 p.m.–7:30 p.m. Free time is from 7:30 p.m.–10:00 p.m. Sleep occurs from 10:00 p.m.–6:30 a.m.
summary: Example schedule for three training sessions per day.

content: Four training sessions per day begin with waking up at 6:30 a.m. The first training session is from 7:00 a.m.–8:00 a.m.
summary: Example schedule start for four training sessions per day.

content: Dinner is eaten from 7:00 p.m.–7:30 p.m. Free time is from 7:30 p.m.–10:00 p.m. Sleep occurs from 10:00 p.m.–6:30 a.m.
summary: Daily schedule outlining meal times, free time, and sleep duration.

content: Four training sessions per day begin with waking up at 6:30 a.m. The first training session is from 7:00 a.m.–8:00 a.m. Breakfast is eaten from 8:30 a.m.–9:00 a.m. Rest occurs from 9:00 a.m.–10:00 a.m. The second training session is from 10:00 a.m.–noon. Rest and recovery sessions are scheduled from noon–1:00 p.m. Lunch is eaten from 1:00 p.m.–2:00 p.m. Rest occurs from 2:00 p.m.–4:00 p.m. The third training session is from 4:00 p.m.–5:30 p.m. Rest and recovery sessions are scheduled from 5:30 p.m.–6:30 p.m. The fourth training session is from 6:30 p.m.–7:30 p.m.
summary: Detailed schedule for four daily training sessions, including meal and rest breaks.

content: Recovery techniques are performed from 7:30 p.m.–8:00 p.m. Dinner is eaten from 8:00 p.m.–8:30 p.m. Free time is from 8:30 p.m.–10:00 p.m. Sleep occurs from 10:00 p.m.–6:30 a.m.
summary: Evening routine including recovery techniques, dinner, free time, and sleep.

content: A training model is a simulation of a competition designed to increase training adaptations and translate them to competitive performance. The modeling process creates a training session that mimics the physiological, technical, tactical, and psychological elements encountered during a competition. Training sessions should be designed to coincide with the objectives of a given phase of training while modeling competitive performance.
summary: Definition and purpose of training models, emphasizing their role in simulating competition and aligning with training phases.

content: Variation of the training stimulus is important for inducing physiological and performance gains. The model approach is one method for inserting a new or novel training stimulus into the training plan. The model approach can increase the athlete’s motivation, induce a new physiological challenge, and present him with novel tasks that prepare him for competition.
summary: Importance of training variation and the model approach as a method for introducing novel stimuli and enhancing motivation.

content: A model can be developed to enhance skill acquisition and refinement. New skills are best learned when athletes are rested, capable of concentrating, and fatigue will not impede learning. Cumulative fatigue makes it more difficult to retain skills that have recently been acquired.
summary: Using models for skill development, emphasizing the importance of rest and avoiding fatigue during skill acquisition.

content: Drills that are used to teach and develop skills should take place immediately after the warm-up. A model training session for skill acquisition includes a warm-up (20-30 minutes), technical and tactical drills (45-60 minutes), physical training (30-45 minutes), and a cool-down (10-20 minutes). This model can be used to develop speed, agility, and power.
summary: Recommended structure for a model training session focused on skill acquisition, including warm-up, drills, physical training, and cool-down.

content: A model training for skill refinement under conditions of fatigue may be used to mimic the conditions encountered at the end of a game, match, or race. Although mastery of skills is best addressed when the athlete is fresh, athletes must also practice skills under the influence of fatigue. The objective in using this type of model is to create a situation of fatigue similar to that seen in the later part of a competition.
summary: Using models to refine skills under fatigue, mimicking competition conditions and addressing the need for fatigue adaptation.

content: The coach should create technical and tactical drills that stress the glycolytic and oxidative energy systems in a way similar to that seen in competition. This situation challenges the athlete’s ability to cope with and overcome fatigue, both physically and psychologically. This model can also be adapted to challenge the abilities of the athlete to perform fast, agile, and powerful athletic movements under the condition of fatigue.
summary: Designing drills within a model to simulate competition fatigue and challenge athletes' physical and psychological resilience.

content: Its utilization is suggested when preparing martial artists, racket sport players, athletes in contact sports, or any athlete working to perform technical and tactical skills effectively during the last part of a sporting contest. The goal of this model is to improve performance at the end of the game, match, or competition when fatigue is at its highest, thus allowing the athlete to physiologically and psychologically adapt to this scenario.
summary: Specific applications of fatigue-based models for various sports and the overall goal of improving performance under fatigue.

content: To achieve maximum effectiveness during an afternoon or evening competition, the athlete must be in a state of arousal and psychological alertness. A short morning (e.g., 10:00 a.m.) session may facilitate optimal arousal for the afternoon contest, reduce anxiety, and help the athlete overcome feelings of excitability, nervousness, and restlessness.
summary: The benefits of a short morning training session for optimizing arousal and managing pre-competition anxiety.

content: This model can also be adapted to challenge the abilities of the athlete to perform fast, agile, and powerful athletic movements under the condition of fatigue. Its utilization is suggested when preparing martial artists, racket sport players, athletes in contact sports, or any athlete working to perform technical and tactical skills effectively during the last part of a sporting contest. The goal of this model is to improve performance at the end of the game, match, or competition when fatigue is at its highest, thus allowing the athlete to physiologically and psychologically adapt to this scenario.
summary: This training model focuses on improving performance under fatigue, particularly in the later stages of competition, by simulating game conditions.

content: To achieve maximum effectiveness during an afternoon or evening competition, the athlete must be in a state of arousal and psychological alertness. A short morning (e.g., 10:00 a.m.) session may facilitate optimal arousal for the afternoon contest, reduce anxiety, and help the athlete overcome feelings of excitability, nervousness, and restlessness. This type of session should be used to promote calmness and controlled confidence.
summary: A short morning training session can help athletes prepare mentally and physically for afternoon or evening competitions.

content: The session should be relatively short and contain a brief series of short, explosive movements. These short actions, which should not induce fatigue, can arouse the athlete for the contest and improve later performance by increasing the contractility of the major skeletal muscles used in the sporting movements (17). These activities must be of short duration, include longer rest intervals, and not induce fatigue, since fatigue will decrease performance capacity. An additional strategy is to use long rest intervals between each activity bout to ensure recovery (39).
summary: A short, high-intensity session with long rest intervals can improve arousal and muscle contractility without inducing fatigue.

content: Training effectiveness depends on the coach’s and athlete’s ability to organize and plan training from a single workout to a long-term plan. A workout plan is not difficult to construct, and it is a powerful tool that can maximize performance outcomes. The objectives and goals of the individual training sessions must be clearly outlined for the athlete. The athlete must receive feedback about her progress toward attaining these objectives.
summary: Effective training requires careful planning, clear objectives, and regular feedback to the athlete.

content: The warm-up is an often overlooked but essential component. This important segment prepares the athlete for the training session and should never be compromised or removed from the training plan. The warm-up should contain general dynamic activities and progress to dynamic muscle actions that are specific to the sporting activity.
summary: A proper warm-up is crucial for preparing the athlete for training and should include both general and sport-specific movements.

content: The cool-down is another often-overlooked component that can influence the effectiveness of the training session. This portion of the session allows the body to return to homeostasis and initiate recovery. This is the ideal time to include static stretching and improve flexibility. If implemented correctly, the cool-down can be a very effective part of the training session.
summary: A cool-down period after training helps the body recover and improve flexibility.

content: Training cycles can be structured into long-term plans such as the quadrennial (4-year) plan and the individual annual plan (1 year). The annual plan can be further subdivided into grosse (macrocycle) and kleine (microcycle) plans. Macrocycles can be structured in 2- to 6-week increments, whereas microcycles traditionally last 1 week.
summary: Training plans can be structured into long-term (e.g., 4-year) and annual plans, further divided into macrocycles (2-6 weeks) and microcycles (1 week).

content: It is probably simpler to use four basic microcycle variants: developmental, competition, recovery–regeneration, and peaking–unloading. The term microcycle is rooted in the Greek word micros, which means ‘small,’ and the Latin word ciclus, which refers to a regular sequence of events. In training methodology, a microcycle is a weekly training program within an annual plan. The microcycle is the most important programming tool in the training process. The content of the microcycle determines the quality of the training process.
summary: Microcycles, typically weekly training plans, are the most important tool in training, with four common variants: developmental, competition, recovery-regeneration, and peaking-unloading.

content: The microcycle is structured according to the objectives, volume, intensity, and methods that are the focus of the training phase. The physiological and psychological demands placed on the athlete cannot be steady. The mix of general and sport-specific stimuli must change according to the phase of the annual plan; they must also take into consideration the athlete’s working capacity, the athlete’s need for recovery, and the competition plan.
summary: Microcycles are tailored to specific training phases, adjusting volume, intensity, and methods based on the athlete's needs and competition schedule.

content: The microcycle must be flexible enough that individual training sessions can be modified to address the residual fatigue of the athlete (i.e., internal load, or degree of readiness) in relation to the training objectives of the training session. When the training unit is modified, subsequent training lessons must be modified to maintain the focus of the microcycle and ensure that the training objectives of the phase are achieved.
summary: Microcycles should be adaptable to the athlete's fatigue levels, with adjustments made to individual sessions to ensure training objectives are met.

content: The microcycle has a strong historical precedence and can be found in the works of Philostratus, an ancient Greek scholar. Philostratus proposed a short-term plan that he called the ‘tetra system,’ a 4-day training cycle. Day 1 of the tetra system: Undertake a short and energetic program. Day 2 of the tetra system: Exercise intensely. Day 3 of the tetra system: Relax to revive the activity. Day 4 of the tetra system: Perform moderate exercise.
summary: The concept of the microcycle has historical roots, with Philostratus's 'tetra system' being an early example of a 4-day training cycle.

content: Philostratus proposed a short-term plan that he called the ‘tetra system,’ a 4-day training cycle. Day 1 of the tetra system: Undertake a short and energetic program. Day 2 of the tetra system: Exercise intensely. Day 3 of the tetra system: Relax to revive the activity. Day 4 of the tetra system: Perform moderate exercise. The tetra system structure was to be repeated continually.
summary: The tetra system is a 4-day training cycle with alternating days of high, moderate, and low intensity, designed for continual repetition.

content: The main criteria determining the microcycle structure are the training goal, training factors, and desired improvements in athletic performance. The appropriate microcycle structure will dictate the rate of improvement in the various training factors. The sequencing within the microcycle is of particular importance because the fatigue generated in one session can significantly affect subsequent training sessions. If a session focuses on endurance development or contains a very intense stimulus, and that session precedes a technical training session, the fatigue generated by the first session might impair the development of technique in the next session.
summary: Microcycle structure is determined by training goals and factors, with sequencing crucial to avoid fatigue interference between training sessions.

content: The microcycle should be structured using the same concepts suggested for the training session plan: Technical or tactical training, Development of speed, agility, or power, Development of strength, Development of specific endurance. Repetition of a training stimulus is essential for the athlete to improve a technical element or develop a biomotor ability. Repetitia est mater studiorum means ‘repetition is the mother of study’. To maximize gains, exercises that target specific biomotor abilities must be undertaken with varying frequencies during the microcycle. Depending on the athlete’s ability, targeted training sessions with similar objectives and content may need to be repeated two or three times during a microcycle to maximize the training effect and its adaptations.
summary: Microcycles should incorporate various training types (technical, speed, strength, endurance) with repetition being key for skill and ability development.

content: Of particular importance is the training stimulus used, because the amount of fatigue generated will affect the recovery required before that stimulus can be used again. For example, in strength training a load with a 20-repetition maximum requires significantly more recovery than a set that doesn’t push the athlete to muscle failure. When the athlete and coach are targeting specific endurance with submaximal intensities, three training sessions per week will suffice. However, for specific endurance of maximum intensity during the competitive phase, the athlete should engage in endurance training twice per week and dedicate the remaining days to lower-intensity training.
summary: Training stimulus intensity affects recovery time, influencing the frequency of specific training types (endurance, strength) within the microcycle.

content: The athlete should use one or two training sessions per week to maintain strength, flexibility, and speed. It appears that 2 or 3 days per week are optimal for plyometric, speed, and agility training. Various training loads should be alternated throughout the microcycle. The athlete should use maximal loads no more than twice per week, interspersed with low-intensity training days and active rest days. It is particularly important to schedule active rest and relaxation the day after a competition. Active rest or low-intensity exercise should be interspersed throughout the microcycle, especially after sessions that have high to maximal demand.
summary: Microcycles should balance various training types (strength, flexibility, plyometrics) with load variation and active rest to optimize recovery.

content: When planning the microcycles, the coach can repeat the basic structure across several microcycles, especially during the preparatory phase. Throughout a macrocycle, microcycles of similar nature (i.e., content and methods) can be repeated two or three times; this can result in qualitative improvements based on the athlete’s adaptation. The types of microcycle fluctuations will vary depending on the athlete’s level of development.
summary: Microcycle structures can be repeated within a macrocycle, with variations based on the athlete's progress and training phase.

content: The annual training plan dictates the structure of the macrocycle and microcycle plans. Individual microcycle plans should be developed to meet the objectives of each phase of the annual and macrocycle training plans. This approach must permit flexibility in the training program, allowing the coach to modify the training content in response to the effects of training performed previously and the athlete’s improvement. A training cycle is considered a guideline and daily and weekly programs can be altered to address the athlete’s response to the training stimuli.
summary: Microcycles are designed based on the annual training plan, with flexibility to adjust based on athlete progress and training response.

content: The microcycle should be constructed in accordance with the training objectives and the phase of training. When structuring the microcycles of the training plan, the coach should consider the objective of the microcycle and the dominant training factors. The coach should consider the training demand (e.g., number of sessions, number of hours, volume, intensity, and complexity) targeted during the microcycle. The coach should consider the intensity of the microcycle and the intensity fluctuations contained in the microcycle. The coach should consider the methods that will be used to induce the training stimulus in each training session. The coach should consider the days on which training and competition will occur (if applicable). The coach should consider the need to alter intensity each day (i.e., start the microcycle with a low- or medium-intensity training session and progress intensity in a undulatory fashion). The timing of competitions in the context of the microcycle should be considered (i.e., when the microcycle leads into a competition, the highest intensity or peak training session for that week should occur 3 to 5 days prior to the event).
summary: Microcycle construction requires careful consideration of training objectives, intensity, methods, competition timing, and athlete response.

content: The coach should consider the intensity of the microcycle and the intensity fluctuations contained in the microcycle. The coach should consider the methods that will be used to induce the training stimulus in each training session. The coach should consider the days on which training and competition will occur (if applicable). The coach should consider the need to alter intensity each day (i.e., start the microcycle with a low- or medium-intensity training session and progress intensity in a undulatory fashion). The timing of competitions in the context of the microcycle should be considered (i.e., when the microcycle leads into a competition, the highest intensity or peak training session for that week should occur 3 to 5 days prior to the event).
summary: Microcycle planning involves considering intensity, training methods, competition schedules, and daily intensity fluctuations.

content: The coach must determine whether the athlete should perform one or more sessions per day. If the athlete’s development and work, school, or personal schedule allow for multiple training sessions, the coach should plan the timing of such sessions.
summary: Determining the number of daily training sessions depends on the athlete's schedule and development.

content: It is helpful to begin each microcycle with a meeting during which the coach and athlete discuss the objectives for each training factor contained in the microcycle and how those objectives will be achieved. The coach and athlete should discuss the volume and intensity of training, the number of training sessions contained in each training day, and where the most difficult training sessions will fall. The coach may want to target performance standards for the microcycle. Additional personalized information can be given to athletes at this time. If the microcycle is leading into a competition, the coach should give the athlete details about the upcoming contest and motivate the athlete to attain each competition goal.
summary: Initial microcycle meetings involve setting objectives, discussing training volume and intensity, and providing athlete-specific information.

content: If there is no competition at the end of the microcycle, a short meeting should be held after the last training session of the microcycle to analyze whether the athlete achieved the microcycle training objectives and goals. The coach should use the meeting to critique the athlete’s performance during training, making sure to highlight the positive aspects while targeting others for improvement. The coach can strengthen the evaluation of the microcycle by collecting input from the athlete.
summary: Post-microcycle meetings involve reviewing progress, providing feedback, and gathering athlete input.

content: The meeting following a microcycle is a tool with which coaches and athletes can coordinate their focus on performance outcomes. Several different microcycle structures are presented in this chapter, but specific training circumstances result in an infinite number of structural variations. The dynamics of the microcycle is dictated by many factors including the phase of training, the developmental status of the athlete, and the training factor emphasis (technical, tactical, or physical preparation).
summary: Microcycle meetings facilitate performance goal alignment, and microcycle structures are adaptable based on various factors.

content: One of the most important factors dictating the microcycle structure is the athlete’s level of development and training capacity. A highly trained athlete may be able to tolerate a greater frequency of training sessions performed at higher intensities than a novice or less-developed athlete. Athletes on the same team may have different work capacities and training needs, so individualization of microcycle content may be warranted.
summary: Microcycle structure is influenced by the athlete's development and training capacity, necessitating individualization.

content: To create an individualized training stimulus, the coach must eliminate standardization and rigidity when structuring the microcycle. This flexibility allows the coach to use information gathered from training, assessments, or competition to modify the training program, thereby helping the athlete meet performance and training objectives.
summary: Individualized training requires flexible microcycle planning based on ongoing data and athlete needs.

content: One method for classifying microcycles centers on the number of training sessions per week. The number of training sessions that an athlete can tolerate without overtraining occurring is dictated by the athlete’s level of development and physical preparation. The microcycle structure will change depending on the available time for training and whether the athlete is participating in a training camp or undergoing regular training sessions.
summary: Microcycle classification often considers the number of weekly training sessions, which varies based on athlete development and training context.

content: Common microcycle structures include 3 days per week, 4 days per week, and 5 days per week. Advanced athletes who have a high work tolerance and can meet the time requirements can undergo eight or nine training sessions per week, sometimes up to 12 or 13. Microcycles with additional training sessions may be used during holidays or during training camps, when more time is available for training, or with more advanced athletes.
summary: Common microcycle structures include 3, 4, and 5 days per week, with more intensive options for advanced athletes.

content: There are many ways to increase the number of training sessions. The athlete can use a 3+1 microcycle, training on 3 successive half days, followed by a half day of rest, for a total of 9 training sessions during the microcycle. A 5+1 microcycle involves training on 5 days, followed by a half day of rest. A 5+1+1 microcycle involves training on 5 days, followed by a half day of rest, followed by a half day of work.
summary: Various methods exist for increasing training sessions within a microcycle, including 3+1, 5+1, and 5+1+1 structures.

content: The structure of these more intensive microcycles depends on the amount of time that is available and the type of training stimulus used during each session. The microcycle structure can be further expanded by integrating multiple training sessions throughout the day that target different training factors. A three-component microcycle may be constructed where a sprint–agility or a plyometric session is conducted in the morning and the main training session, which targets tactical or technical development followed by strength training, may be performed in the late afternoon or early evening.
summary: Intensive microcycles can be structured based on available time and training stimuli, potentially incorporating multiple sessions per day targeting different factors.

content: Periodization should vary depending on the characteristics of the training, the type of microcycle used, the environmental conditions (e.g., climate, weather), and the phase of the annual training plan. The intensity of training can alternate between the six intensity zones, ranging from very high (90%-100% of maximum) to a recovery day where no training is undertaken. These alterations are dictated by the objectives of the microcycle.
summary: Periodization and intensity fluctuations within microcycles are adaptable based on training characteristics, environment, and objectives.

content: The microcycle structure can be further expanded by integrating multiple training sessions throughout the day that target different training factors. A three-component microcycle may be constructed where a sprint–agility or a plyometric session is conducted in the morning and the main training session, which targets tactical or technical development followed by strength training, may be performed in the late afternoon or early evening.
summary: Microcycles can be structured with multiple sessions throughout the day, targeting different training factors.

content: Periodization should vary depending on the characteristics of the training, the type of microcycle used, the environmental conditions (e.g., climate, weather), and the phase of the annual training plan.
summary: Periodization should be flexible and adapt to various factors like training type, environment, and the annual training plan.

content: The intensity of training can alternate between the six intensity zones, ranging from very high (90%-100% of maximum) to a recovery day where no training is undertaken. These alterations are dictated by the objectives of the microcycle. For example, the objectives of an intensive microcycle may require 1, 2, or occasionally 3 high-demand to very high-demand training days depending on the objective of the microcycle.
summary: Training intensity varies within a microcycle based on its objectives, ranging from very high to recovery days.

content: When planning the modulations of intensity or training demand within the microcycle, the coach should consider the principles of load progression. The microcycle usually should contain only one peak, which occurs during the middle 3 days of the week. In some instances, a microcycle can contain two peaks that are followed by 1 or 2 days of regeneration sessions. An exception to this rule may occur when model training is being used; in this case, two peaks can occur on adjacent days to simulate a competitive situation.
summary: Microcycle intensity and training demand should follow load progression principles, typically with one peak per week, but potentially two in specific situations.

content: The microcycle structure can be modified if the athlete is training at high altitude or has traveled a long distance and crossed several time zones (i.e., time difference of 5-8 h). In these situations, it may be warranted to add an adaptation microcycle that does not contain a peak. The microcycle structure also should be altered when the athlete is training in a hot and humid climate. In this situation it is recommended that the peak occur at the beginning of the week when the athlete has more vigor.
summary: Microcycle structure should be adjusted based on altitude, travel, and climate conditions to optimize training.

content: The sample microcycles in figures 7.10 through 7.16 represent total training demand rather than the separate variables of volume and intensities. The use of total training demand allows for the microcycle structure to be used in a variety of sporting activities because sports vary in their area of emphasis, with some being dominated by speed-power, maximum strength, or endurance. Additionally, team sports contain a complex interaction of many factors that can best represented by total training demand.
summary: Microcycles can be represented using total training demand, accommodating various sports and their specific demands.

content: A microcycle can be structured many ways; some authors speculate that there are at least 22 possible microcycle structures. The microcycle should be functional and as simple as possible. The plan should specify the date, objectives, and content for each training session. The content should be succinct and easy to understand and should emphasize major items to target in the training session.
summary: Microcycles should be functional, simple, and clearly outline training dates, objectives, and content.

content:
Sport/Event: Javelin Microcyle # 29
Date : 20.07-27-09
Objectives: 1. Perform 67:00 m
Objectives: 2. Perfect the rhythm of the last three strides under higher velocity conditions
Objectives: 3. Develop the ability to concentrate for the morning competition
Objectives: 4. Maintain leg and arm power
summary: Example microcycle plan for a javelin athlete, outlining specific objectives and dates.

content: The structure of the microcycle depends on the training objectives and thus the training phase. There are four general microcycle classifications: developmental, competition, recovery–regeneration, and peaking–unloading.
summary: Microcycle structure varies based on training objectives and phases.

content: Developmental microcycles are specific to the preparatory phase of training. The objective of developmental microcycles is to increase the level of adaptation, improve skills, and develop biomotor abilities. Such cycles could have two or three peaks of medium and high demand. The microcycle uses an alternation of energy systems and loads, creating an undulatory loading dynamic. The actual structure of the microcycle depends on the athlete’s classification, sport characteristics, and the preparation subphase the athlete is in.
summary: Developmental microcycles focus on adaptation, skill development, and biomotor ability during the preparatory phase.

content: The goal of a regeneration microcycle is to dissipate residual fatigue. Another approach to using this type of microcycle, specific to the ... Unloading microcycles are placed at the end of every macrocycle to dissipate fatigue and elevate the athlete’s level of athletic readiness. Two unloading microcycles are used back to back before the most important competitions of the year to allow for peaking. This type of microcycle is created mainly by decreasing weekly training volume (by 50% or more) and by maintaining or decreasing intensity (by 5%-10%). The reduction of training demand will result in physiological responses that allow supercompensation to occur.
summary: Regeneration and unloading microcycles aim to reduce fatigue and promote supercompensation.

content: Unloading microcycles are placed at the end of every macrocycle to dissipate fatigue and elevate the athlete’s level of athletic readiness. Two unloading microcycles are used back to back before the most important competitions of the year to allow for peaking. This type of microcycle is created mainly by decreasing weekly training volume (by 50% or more) and by maintaining or decreasing intensity (by 5%-10%). The reduction of training demand will result in physiological responses that allow supercompensation to occur. The unloading microcycle elevates performance and decreases the potential for overtraining.
summary: Unloading microcycles are strategically placed to reduce fatigue and enhance performance.

content: During the competitive phase, the sequencing of individual microcycles depends on the competitive schedule. Timing of competitions also affects the placement of regeneration and unloading days within the microcycle. The format used when planning a competitive microcycle will be affected by the requirements of the sport. In team sports there may be several competitions in 1 week, whereas in individual sports competitions may occur over several consecutive weeks.
summary: Competitive microcycle planning is influenced by the sport's demands and competition schedule.

content: With one competition per week, 1 or 2 days of rest and recovery should be included each week. The bulk of training, including strength and power training, will be conducted during the middle of the microcycle. In this example, a medium to high training demand is used. After the bulk of training is completed, unloading should then be planned for the 2 days prior to the next competition. This basic competitive microcycle can be modified when the opponent is weaker or the competition is of little importance. Such a competition will not present a high physiological challenge, and the subsequent competition-induced fatigue will be markedly less than usual. It may be warranted in these situations to replace the recovery day that is planned for Monday (in this example) with an additional technical or tactical training session. It is likely that only one unloading day would be needed before a minor competition.
summary: Competitive microcycles typically involve a balance of training, recovery, and adjustments based on competition importance.

content: This schedule results in a net gain of 4 training days, with at least one of those days being of a high demand. Activities for maintaining the level of physical training have to also be planned, although they should be less challenging than during a microcycle with no competitions. Emphasis should be given to the maintenance of the dominant biomotor abilities in the targeted sport.
summary: Maintaining training levels during competition periods requires adjustments and focus on key abilities.

content: When teams have multiple competitions or games in one microcycle, Monday is a short regeneration session that contains a very low training demand. The second session of the microcycle (Tuesday) is a tactical day that is used to elevate performance during the Wednesday competition. On Thursday a regeneration day is planned, and Friday is the only high-demand training session of the microcycle. Strength and power training is conducted on Friday. The number of exercises on Friday should be very low (two to four) and they should strictly address the prime movers. An unloading day is planned for Saturday to facilitate a good performance for the Sunday game.
summary: Microcycle structure for teams with multiple competitions emphasizes recovery and tactical preparation.

content: If the competitive schedule is organized over the 2 days of a weekend, then the microcycle can be organized as depicted in figure 7.21. Two unloading training sessions are used on the 2 days (Thursday and Friday) prior to the weekend competition so that fatigue is dissipated and supercompensation may be achieved at the time of competition. The highest training demand occurs at the beginning of the microcycle (Tuesday), thus progressively decreasing the training demand across the microcycle.
summary: Microcycle planning for weekend competitions involves strategic unloading and progressive training demand reduction.

content: Active recovery performed at very low intensities can facilitate lactate removal, dampen central nervous system activity, increase the tone of the parasympathetic nervous system, and reduce muscle soreness. Active recovery should include very low intensities of exercise that do not significantly affect muscle glycogen stores. Tournament play can significantly affect glycogen, so glycogen stores must be replenished before the next competitive match. The best method for accomplishing glycogen replenishment is to follow a post-exercise supplementation program and to ensure adequate dietary intake of carbohydrate between matches.
summary: Active recovery and glycogen replenishment strategies are crucial for tournament performance.

content: A microcycle for a weeklong tournament is presented in figure 7.22. The morning after every game includes a very low-intensity regeneration session that is designed to speed recovery. A low-intensity tactical training session is planned for the late afternoon on the day prior to the game. A microcycle formatted in this fashion will provide the athlete with the best potential to recover and maximize performance.
summary: Microcycle structure for a weeklong tournament prioritizes recovery and tactical preparation.

content: The vast majority of microcycles in the annual training plan target the development of skills and abilities required by the sport. During the competitive phase, the focus of the training plan shifts to maximizing performance capacity during competition.
summary: Training plan emphasis shifts from skill development to performance maximization during the competitive phase.

content: A microcycle formatted in this fashion will provide the athlete with the best potential to recover and maximize performance. The vast majority of microcycles in the annual training plan target the development of skills and abilities required by the sport. During the competitive phase, the focus of the training plan shifts to maximizing performance capacity during competition.
summary: Microcycle structure prioritizes recovery and skill development, shifting to performance maximization during competition.

content: One strategy is to develop the microcycle based on a model of the competition (model training). This model, especially in individual sports, can be used repeatedly prior to main competition. The model should contain training sessions of various intensities and should alternate between active rest and recovery. The daily cycle should be identical to the day of the competition.
summary: Model training involves replicating competition structure in microcycles, including varied intensities and rest periods.

content: Many sports (e.g., regional-level track and field and swimming, tennis, some team sports, martial arts) have qualifying rounds followed by finals in the same day (e.g., Friday at 10:00 a.m. and 6:00 p.m.). Models designed to address this competitive schedule would place the main training day on Friday, which would contain two training sessions that would occur at the same times as the targeted competition.
summary: Microcycle models can be tailored to specific competition formats, such as single-day events with qualifying and final rounds.

content: Other sports (e.g., some team sports, national- and international-level track and field and swimming, boxing, tennis, wrestling) may contain 2 to 4 days of consecutive competitions. This type of competitive format can also be modeled by modifying the microcycle structure to correspond to the demands of the competition. This model should be repeated several times prior to the contest.
summary: Microcycle models can adapt to multi-day competitions, simulating the demands and schedule.

content: However, the model should only be used every 2 or 3 weeks, with developmental microcycles placed between each microcycle containing this competitive model. Some tournaments such as the Olympic Games, World Championships, or international competitions are organized over 4 to 9 days. It is not feasible to model this competitive format because such a model will create a large amount of physiological stress and significantly affect the time that can be dedicated to training.
summary: Competitive microcycles should be interspersed with developmental ones, and large tournaments are not modeled directly due to potential stress.

content: To prepare for larger tournaments, the athlete should participate in smaller tournaments that last 2 or 3 days and contain four or five competitive efforts. To prepare for these tournaments, the athlete should follow developmental microcycles and daily training structures that contain characteristics of the targeted tournament. It may also be warranted to familiarize the athlete with the competitive schedule by using the competitive model, altering between competition and recovery typically seen in a tournament.
summary: Preparation for large tournaments involves smaller competitions and microcycles mimicking the target event's structure and recovery patterns.

content: It may be recommended that training days that fall on the same day of a tournament involve higher demands, whereas the day after this session should be of lower intensity or contain a recovery session. The athlete should alternate between simulated competitive days and rest and recovery days to maximize her ability to adapt to the competition schedule.
summary: Training intensity should be adjusted to mimic tournament days, with alternating high-demand and recovery sessions for adaptation.

content: Many athletes do not favor free days between competitions because performance during the second day of competition is sometimes not as good as expected. The decrease in performance seems to be based on postcompetition psychological reactions (e.g., overconfidence, conceit) rather than an accumulation of fatigue. To facilitate the athlete’s ability to tolerate the rest days between competitions, the coach can include competition-based microcycles in all macrocycles contained in the competitive phase of the annual training plan.
summary: Rest days between competitions can be addressed by incorporating competition-based microcycles to manage psychological factors affecting performance.

content: If the competitive phase is short, the coach can introduce the competitive model during the last part of the preparatory phase. Although the competitive model can be used to prepare for a major competition, the athlete likely will participate in several additional competitions. Such competitions may occur on a different day of the microcycle than the major competition.
summary: Competitive microcycles can be introduced during the preparatory phase, even if the athlete participates in additional competitions with varying microcycle structures.

content: The main goal of the microcycles preceding the major competition is to allow the athlete to completely recover from the physiological and psychological stress of training so that peak performance occurs. Another strategy for peaking is to manipulate the training load across two microcycles. Peaking can be accomplished in 8 to 14 days with gradual reductions in training load.
summary: Microcycles before major competitions focus on recovery and peaking, often involving gradual training load reductions.

content: Elevations in readiness and performance occur when fatigue is dissipated. If fatigue is managed appropriately, a supercompensation effect will occur, elevating the athlete’s readiness and performance potential.
summary: Managing fatigue through appropriate training strategies leads to supercompensation, enhancing readiness and performance.

content: Recovery and regeneration can be integrated into a microcycle by including rest days, variations in training intensity, and alternative methods of training. A regeneration microcycle should be incorporated at the end of a macrocycle.
summary: Microcycles should incorporate recovery strategies like rest days, varied intensity, and alternative training methods, culminating in a regeneration microcycle at the end of each macrocycle.

content: These microcycles can be structured the same as a training microcycle, but the intensity or frequency of training can be reduced. Another restoration microcycle structure contains actual training sessions that are designed to stimulate recovery. These sessions can contain a slightly longer warm-up and a relatively short training session consisting of either light work applicable to the sport or complementary activities followed by a series of activities designed to facilitate recovery.
summary: Regeneration microcycles can be structured similarly to training microcycles but with reduced intensity, or they can include specific recovery-focused sessions.

content: Regeneration microcycles are integral parts of the annual plan and are particularly important during the competition phase.
summary: Regeneration microcycles are essential components of the annual training plan, especially during the competitive phase.

content: The morning after every game includes a very low-intensity regeneration session that is designed to speed recovery. A low-intensity tactical training session is planned for the late afternoon on the day prior to the game.
summary: Recovery and tactical training are strategically incorporated into the day after and before games.

content: A regeneration microcycle should be incorporated at the end of a macrocycle. These microcycles can be structured the same as a training microcycle, but the intensity or frequency of training can be reduced. Another restoration microcycle structure contains actual training sessions that are designed to stimulate recovery. These sessions can contain a slightly longer warm-up and a relatively short training session consisting of either light work applicable to the sport or complementary activities followed by a series of activities designed to facilitate recovery. Regeneration microcycles are integral parts of the annual plan and are particularly important during the competition phase. During the competition phase of training for many sports, 2 or 3 microcycles can be included that contain a series of competitions. To enable the athlete to tolerate this high amount of physiological and psychological stress, regeneration and recovery microcycle structures should be used. An example of a regeneration microcycle is presented in figure 7.24. This microcycle is designed to remove physiological and psychological fatigue, aid in the replenishment of energy substrates, and supercompensate the athlete at the end of the cycle.
summary: Regeneration microcycles are essential for athlete recovery and should be incorporated throughout the training year, especially during competition phases.

content: The coach and athlete should use objective methods to plan training intensities or loads. In the best-case scenario, the plan alternates heavy training days with easy days throughout the year. In the worst-case scenario, the plan uses a ‘no pain, no gain’ philosophy and the loading or intensity of training is constantly very high, which ultimately leads to high levels of fatigue and even to overtraining.
summary: Training plans should utilize objective methods to determine intensity and volume, ideally alternating between heavy and easy training days.

content: In individual sports, volume is often quantified using mileage, as in the kilometers or miles per microcycle, macrocycle, or year of training. In throwing events, volume may be quantified as the number of throws completed in the individual cycles. Intensity may be quantified as distance jumped or thrown, the percentage of maximum speed, or maximum power output or heart rate. In strength training, the volume of training is quantified as the tonnage (volume load) lifted, weekly repetitions per exercise, or total sets per session. Intensity in strength training is determined by the athlete’s maximum strength or one-repetition maximum (1RM). Training intensity and volume are rarely quantified in team sports.
summary: Training volume and intensity can be quantified using various methods depending on the sport, with individual sports often having more specific metrics.

content: The quantification of training is often a difficult undertaking, which is easier to accomplish when the training program is designed for an athlete with whom the coach is very familiar. The coach should know the athlete’s training background, ability to tolerate physiological and psychological stress, strengths and weaknesses, and training environment. Training programs should not be shared by athletes because these characteristics are different for each athlete. Understanding the athlete’s needs and abilities is an essential component of designing a training plan.
summary: Effective training program design requires a deep understanding of the athlete's individual characteristics and needs.

content: The intensity of training should be planned using established equations, and the volume of training should be quantified. The training intensity throughout the microcycle must be varied to enhance the athlete’s physiological adaptation to the training load and stimulate regeneration after a training session. The coach may identify four to five training intensities based on the physiological demands of the sport. Each intensity must correlate with the activity’s rhythm or tempo, the training type and method, and the athlete’s heart rate response (plus or minus a few beats per minute). The intensity zones should be determined according to the bioenergetic characteristics of the sport or the percentage contribution of the various energy systems.
summary: Training intensity and volume should be carefully planned and varied throughout the microcycle to optimize adaptation and recovery.

content: From the late specific preparation onward, the highest percentage of the training load should target the development of the dominant ability and the bioenergetic characteristics of the sport. In table 7.3, intensity zones 3 and 4 comprise 70% of the total training load for the competitive phase of the annual training plan. If an objective means of quantifying training does not exist, the coach can subjectively divide skills and training into more difficult (pace of game, race, or match) and less difficult stratifications. The pace of the game, race, or match should be simulated with intensity zone 2; this intensity should be used for at least 50% of the training time per week.
summary: Training emphasis should shift towards developing the athlete's dominant abilities and bioenergetic characteristics as competition approaches.

content: A better quantification system contains five intensities, in which zone 5 is a low intensity to be used for compensation between other intensities or to facilitate supercompensation. The intensity higher than the pace of the game, race, or match is dominated by anaerobic energy supply, whereas aerobic energy supply dominates intensities that are below game, race, or match pace. Intensity zone 1 is the most demanding, and intensity zone 5 is the least demanding. The characteristics of intensity zone 1 are Speed endurance. The characteristics of intensity zone 2 are Power endurance. The characteristics of intensity zone 3 are Specific racing endurance. The characteristics of intensity zone 4 are Aerobic endurance of medium distance. The characteristics of intensity zone 5 are Aerobic endurance of long distance. Stroke rate in intensity zone 1 is greater than 40.
summary: Training intensity zones should be tailored to the specific demands of the sport and athlete, with five zones encompassing a range from high-intensity anaerobic to low-intensity aerobic.

content: The characteristics of intensity zone 1 are Power endurance. The type of training in intensity zone 1 is Starts and sprints up to 15 s; rest 1.5 min. Heart rate in intensity zone 1 is greater than 180 beats/min. Bioenergetics in intensity zone 1 is 80% anaerobic. The characteristics of intensity zone 1 are T: complex; TA: lactic acid tolerance training. Duration in intensity zone 1 is 30-60 s. Stroke rate in intensity zone 1 is greater than 40.
summary: Intensity zone 1 focuses on power endurance, involving short bursts of intense activity with long rest periods. It emphasizes anaerobic energy systems and high heart rates.

content: The characteristics of intensity zone 2 are Specific racing endurance. The type of training in intensity zone 2 is Repetitions of 250-1,000 m; rest 3-10 min. Heart rate in intensity zone 2 is 170-180 beats/min. Bioenergetics in intensity zone 2 is 20% anaerobic. The characteristics of intensity zone 2 are T/TA: suicide drills. Stroke rate in intensity zone 2 is 37-40.
summary: Intensity zone 2 targets specific racing endurance, involving repetitions of medium-distance efforts with moderate rest periods. It utilizes a mix of anaerobic and aerobic energy systems.

content: The characteristics of intensity zone 3 are Aerobic endurance of medium distance. The type of training in intensity zone 3 is Races and controlled racing; interval training of 3-4 min; rest 4-5 min. Heart rate in intensity zone 3 is 150-170 beats/min. Bioenergetics in intensity zone 3 is 65% anaerobic. The characteristics of intensity zone 3 are TA: V.O2max T/TA: phosphagen. Stroke rate in intensity zone 3 is 32-36.
summary: Intensity zone 3 focuses on aerobic endurance of medium distances, incorporating races, interval training, and controlled efforts. It primarily relies on aerobic energy systems.

content: The characteristics of intensity zone 4 are Aerobic endurance of long distance. The type of training in intensity zone 4 is Long repetitions; variable rate and power; long-distance rowing with sprints of 30-60 s. Heart rate in intensity zone 4 is 120-150 beats/min. Bioenergetics in intensity zone 4 is 35% anaerobic. The characteristics of intensity zone 4 are T: skills: accuracy in shooting, serving, passing. Stroke rate in intensity zone 4 is 24-32.
summary: Intensity zone 4 emphasizes aerobic endurance of long distances, involving long repetitions, variable-rate training, and long-distance rowing with short sprints. It utilizes a mix of aerobic and anaerobic energy systems.

content: The characteristics of intensity zone 5 are Aerobic endurance of long distance. The type of training in intensity zone 5 is Long-distance (steady-state) technique. Heart rate in intensity zone 5 is less than 120 beats/min. Bioenergetics in intensity zone 5 is 25% anaerobic. Stroke rate in intensity zone 5 is less than 24.
summary: Intensity zone 5 focuses on aerobic endurance of long distances, primarily utilizing steady-state technique at a low heart rate and predominantly relying on aerobic energy systems.

content: The coach should follow the correct sequence when planning the microcycle. The first step in planning the microcycle is to plan the intensity zones for each day of the week. Intensity zones should be chosen for each day of the week to provide variations in intensities, type of work, or energy system targeted. The coach should include several variables of work for each intensity. Each plan should include one to three intensity symbols. It is possible to train at least two types of work that tax the same energy system. This suggestion is mostly valid for sports of high technical and tactical complexity.
summary: Planning a microcycle involves a structured sequence, starting with determining intensity zones for each day.  Variations in intensity, work type, and energy systems should be incorporated, and multiple training variables can target the same energy system, particularly in complex sports.

content: Intensity zone 1 is the most demanding, and intensity zone 5 is the least demanding.
summary: Intensity zones progress from the most demanding (zone 1) to the least demanding (zone 5).

content: The characteristics of intensity zone 1 are T: complex; TA: lactic acid tolerance training. Duration in intensity zone 1 is 30-60 s. Rest interval in intensity zone 1 is 3-5 min. Heart rate in intensity zone 1 is greater than 180. Bioenergetics in intensity zone 1 is 80% anaerobic. Total training volume in intensity zone 1 is 40.
summary: Intensity zone 1 focuses on complex training and lactic acid tolerance, with durations of 30-60 seconds and rest intervals of 3-5 minutes. It features a high heart rate (over 180 bpm) and relies heavily on anaerobic energy (80%). Total training volume in this zone is 40.

content: The characteristics of intensity zone 2 are T/TA: suicide drills. Duration in intensity zone 2 is 20-30 s. Rest interval in intensity zone 2 is 3 min. Heart rate in intensity zone 2 is greater than 180. Bioenergetics in intensity zone 2 is 90% anaerobic. Total training volume in intensity zone 2 is 20.
