summary: Intensity zone 2 utilizes suicide drills and features short bursts (20-30 seconds) with 3-minute rest intervals. It demands a high heart rate (over 180 bpm) and primarily relies on anaerobic energy (90%). Total training volume is 20.

content: The characteristics of intensity zone 3 are TA: V.O2max T/TA: phosphagen. Duration in intensity zone 3 is 3-5 min. Rest interval in intensity zone 3 is 2-3 min. Heart rate in intensity zone 3 is greater than 170. Bioenergetics in intensity zone 3 is 40% anaerobic. Total training volume in intensity zone 3 is 20.
summary: Intensity zone 3 focuses on V.O2max and phosphagen training, lasting 3-5 minutes with 2-3 minute rest intervals. It requires a high heart rate (over 170 bpm) and utilizes a mix of anaerobic (40%) and aerobic energy. Total training volume is 20.

content: The characteristics of intensity zone 4 are T: skills: accuracy in shooting, serving, passing. Duration in intensity zone 4 is 5-15 s. Rest interval in intensity zone 4 is 1-2 min. Heart rate in intensity zone 4 is greater than 170. Bioenergetics in intensity zone 4 is 90% anaerobic. Total training volume in intensity zone 4 is 20.
summary: Intensity zone 4 emphasizes skill development (shooting, serving, passing) with short bursts (5-15 seconds) and 1-2 minute rest intervals. It demands a high heart rate (over 170 bpm) and relies heavily on anaerobic energy (90%). Total training volume is 20.

content: Duration in intensity zone 5 is 10 min (several bouts). Rest interval in intensity zone 5 is 1 min. Heart rate in intensity zone 5 is 120-150. Bioenergetics in intensity zone 5 is 10% anaerobic.
summary: Intensity zone 5 involves longer durations (10 minutes in several bouts) with 1-minute rest intervals. It features a moderate heart rate (120-150 bpm) and primarily utilizes aerobic energy (90%).

content: The higher the intensity or power output of the activity, the greater the reliance on anaerobic energy supply (phosphagen and fast glycolytic). A plan that modulates the intensity of training will target a specific energy system, thus facilitating recovery and regeneration or stimulating adaptation. The structure of this variation will be dictated by the phase of training (preparatory versus competitive) and the need to supercompensate a specific energy system prior to competition. This is best accomplished through creating microcycle variations based on the interaction of exercise physiology and training methodology. A plan that is appropriately varied will greatly increase the athlete’s probability of reaching peak performance at the appropriate time. For most sports, the energy demand of the activity preferentially targets at least two energy systems. Although the primary energy system targeted can be isolated, all of the energy systems are active at the same time and the intensity of the activity (i.e., power output) will dictate which energy systems are preferentially targeted. Therefore, a high intensity will increase the influence of the phosphagen and fast glycolytic systems, whereas a lower intensity will increase the emphasis on the slow glycolytic and oxidative systems. If the competition depletes the athlete’s energy reserves, training intensity during the postcompetitive training days should be reduced.
summary: This chunk explains the relationship between training intensity, energy systems, and periodization. It emphasizes the importance of varying training intensity to target specific energy systems, facilitate recovery, and optimize performance. It also highlights the interconnected nature of energy systems and the need to adjust training intensity based on competition demands.

content: Alternating training intensities during a microcycle is one of the most effective methods to prevent exhaustion, staleness, and overtraining. Wednesday and Saturday are compensation days.
summary: This chunk outlines the benefits of alternating training intensities within a microcycle to prevent overtraining and promote recovery. It also specifies Wednesday and Saturday as compensation days.

content: A plan that is appropriately varied will greatly increase the athlete’s probability of reaching peak performance at the appropriate time.  Alternating the focus on intensity and energy systems can be very difficult with complex sports (e.g., team sports) in which multiple energy systems play a large role in performance and the technical and tactical skills rely on all of those systems. Such activities can require the athlete to maximize power, speed, and high-intensity endurance in order to be successful. Thus, planning involves a conundrum in which many tasks must be trained to meet the demands of the sport without inducing overtraining. The best approach is to vary intensities of training, thereby changing the bioenergetic targets of training, to develop multiple facets of the athlete’s physiology.
summary: Varied training plans are crucial for peak performance, especially in complex sports that demand multiple energy systems.  The challenge lies in balancing training intensity and energy system focus to avoid overtraining while developing all necessary athletic qualities.

content: For most sports, the energy demand of the activity preferentially targets at least two energy systems. Although the primary energy system targeted can be isolated, all of the energy systems are active at the same time and the intensity of the activity (i.e., power output) will dictate which energy systems are preferentially targeted. Therefore, a high intensity will increase the influence of the phosphagen and fast glycolytic systems, whereas a lower intensity will increase the emphasis on the slow glycolytic and oxidative systems.
summary: Most sports utilize multiple energy systems, with intensity dictating which system is primarily emphasized. High intensity favors phosphagen and fast glycolytic systems, while lower intensity relies more on slow glycolytic and oxidative systems.

content: If the competition depletes the athlete’s energy reserves, training intensity during the postcompetitive training days should be reduced. Reducing the intensity of training will dissipate cumulative fatigue, thus creating a microcycle that induces recovery and regeneration and thereby prepares the athlete for subsequent training.
summary: Post-competition training intensity should be reduced to allow for recovery and regeneration, creating a microcycle that promotes adaptation and prepares the athlete for future training.

content: Although it is important to alternate work and regeneration, it is not always necessary for the athlete to be completely recovered for the next bout of training. For example, during the preparatory phase of training, when the major focus is on developing a strong physiological foundation, the athlete will not fully recover and performance will not supercompensate. When the training demand is lowered in later unloading microcycles, the athlete’s level of athletic shape (i.e., readiness) will be elevated and performance will increase. Therefore, during the preparatory phase of training, the plan can include developmental microcycles without allowing the athlete enough time to remove all of the accumulated fatigue. This process will challenge the athlete’s physiological systems and result in greater performance improvements after future unloading microcycles.
summary: While alternating work and regeneration is important, complete recovery is not always necessary, especially during the preparatory phase.  Strategic fatigue management through developmental microcycles can enhance physiological adaptation and performance gains.

content: As a competition approaches, the fatigue generated in the preparatory phase can be reduced by alternating training intensities and lowering training volume; this stimulates physiological adaptations, removes fatigue, and allows physical parameters to supercompensate.
summary: As competition nears, training intensity and volume should be adjusted to reduce fatigue, stimulate physiological adaptations, and allow for peak performance.

content: A two-step process can be used to vary training intensities in an attempt to target specific energy systems. Appropriate nutrition allows the athlete to restore energy sources, facilitating physiological adaptations that will eventually increase performance. During some phases of training, fatigue must be dissipated to stimulate supercompensation, whereas in other phases high levels of fatigue are generated to challenge the athlete’s physiology to adapt. Alternating the training demand will challenge the athlete on some training days, which will induce a high level of fatigue; on other days fatigue will be removed in response to a less challenging training bout.
summary: Training intensity variation can target specific energy systems.  Nutrition plays a crucial role in recovery and adaptation.  Strategic fatigue management through alternating high and low intensity training days is essential for optimal performance.

content: Monday’s session taxes the neuromuscular, phosphagen, and glycolytic energy systems. Activities involving speed, power, and maximum strength training performed for short durations rely on ATP-PCr as fuel. Depending on the volume and intensity of training, the rate of recovery from Monday’s workout should be relatively quick, allowing the athlete to perform Tuesday’s training session without much fatigue. In a traditional plan in which the athlete experiences high levels of physiological stress almost every day, the demanding session occurring on Monday in figure 7.26 could nearly deplete the glycogen stores and produce a high level of accumulated fatigue. Alternating training intensities may help the athlete better manage this fatigue.
summary: Training sessions should be structured to target different energy systems and allow for adequate recovery.  Alternating intensities can help manage fatigue and optimize performance.

content: In figure 7.26 Monday is a training day with a high amount of physiological stress, whereas Tuesday’s training session contains tactical and endurance training performed at a much lower intensity. The remainder of the microcycle alternates training stressors in order to modulate fatigue.
summary: A well-structured microcycle alternates high-intensity and low-intensity training days to manage fatigue and optimize recovery.

content: Training for speed and power occurs on the same day as power endurance training, which is marked by repeating power exercises 10 to 25 times per set. Two high-intensity training days, in which the phosphagen and glycolytic systems are taxed, precede a training day that focuses on tempo training and the development of endurance (compensation).
summary: Training microcycles should incorporate a variety of exercises and intensities to target different energy systems and promote balanced development.

content: Figure 7.28 is a microcycle for a sport that is dominated by aerobic endurance capacity and therefore relies predominantly on oxidative metabolism. The plan simultaneously includes types of strength training that tax the same energy system on that particular day. Consequently, muscular endurance or high-volume (many repetitions) strength training is performed after the bout of endurance training.
summary: Training plans for endurance-focused sports should prioritize aerobic development while incorporating strength training that complements the primary energy system.

content: Higher-intensity activities (maximum strength or power endurance training) occur on the day that specifically taxes the phosphagen and glycolytic systems. Event-specific intensity training is performed on Thursday. This type of targeted training is sometimes termed ergogenesis. Figure 7.29 shows a microcycle structure for an endurance sport in which competition lasts between 4 and 6 min. In this example, high-intensity endurance that stresses both the phosphagen and glycolytic systems is important for a successful performance. Days that target the development of high-intensity endurance (i.e., produce significant glycolytic stress) are followed by low-intensity aerobic work that is used as a compensation activity. The goal is to develop the ability to produce high levels of lactic acid formation, and then buffer this
summary: Training microcycles for endurance sports should include both high-intensity and low-intensity sessions to develop the necessary energy systems and prepare for competition.

content: Figure 7.29 shows a microcycle structure for an endurance sport in which competition lasts between 4 and 6 min. In this example, high-intensity endurance that stresses both the phosphagen and glycolytic systems is important for a successful performance. Days that target the development of high-intensity endurance (i.e., produce significant glycolytic stress) are followed by low-intensity aerobic work that is used as a compensation activity. The goal is to develop the ability to produce high levels of lactic acid formation, and then buffer this.
summary: This chunk describes a microcycle structure for an endurance sport, emphasizing the importance of high-intensity training and recovery.

content: The term macro is derived from the Greek word makros, meaning 'large'. A training macrocycle is a phase that lasts 2 to 6 weeks or microcycles. The microcycle is used to plan for the immediate future, whereas the macrocycle projects the structure of a training program several weeks in advance. Therefore, one could think of the macrocycle as the general structure of training and a projection for the future, whereas the microcycle is the exact plan used to accomplish the targeted goals.
summary: This chunk defines macrocycles and microcycles, explaining their relationship and purpose in training.

content: Although the macrocycle plans used to prepare athletes for various sporting activities may have some similarities, it is likely that each sport will have requirements that must be addressed. These requirements will in most instances dictate the macrocycle structure. The loading structure and the duration of the macrocycles may be different depending on the sport and the phase of training. Therefore, the macrocycle must be crafted carefully to meet the individual athlete’s training objectives.
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of tailoring macrocycle structures to individual sports and athlete needs.

content: During the preparatory phase, the macrocycle is generally longer (4 to 7 weeks) than those in the competition phase to address the objectives of this portion of the annual training plan. In this context the macrocycle should be long enough to develop biomotor abilities, technical skills, or tactical elements. Therefore, one method for determining a macrocycle’s length is to consider the time necessary to perfect an ability or its components. During the competitive phase, macrocycles are shorter in accordance with the competitive schedule, and contain two or three 1-week microcycles.
summary: This chunk describes the typical length and structure of macrocycles during the preparatory and competitive phases of training.

content: Competitions should fall at the end of the unloading microcycle, which coincides with the end of the macrocycle; this will give the coach information about the athlete’s progress toward the target performance level. If the competition phase contains several competitions during the month (possibly as many as eight), as typically seen in team sports, the coach must decide which competitions are the most important. In this type of macrocycle structure, less emphasis is placed on the minor competitions because they are simply used to train through and to provide feedback on the training process. The length of the macrocycle should allow for the last microcycle to lead into the major competition.
summary: This chunk discusses the timing of competitions within macrocycles and how to prioritize them, especially in team sports.

content: The development of the macrocycle structure is based on the objectives, the phase of training, and the competition schedule. The macrocycles of the annual training plan should vary according to the training objectives for each phase of the plan (i.e., preparatory, competitive, and transition phases). The main objective of the preparatory phase is to induce physiological, psychological, and technical adaptations that will serve as the foundation for competitive performances. A disturbing trend in some sports is the use of year-round competitive schedules. In an appropriately constructed annual training plan, the preparatory phase is a crucial part of the plan and is the foundation for competitive success. Developmental macrocycles are well suited for the preparatory phase of training.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of tailoring macrocycles to different training phases and the crucial role of the preparatory phase.

content: The training demand of developmental macrocycles usually follows the step-loading method. In the 4:1 loading pattern, the training load is increased across four microcycles, and unloading or regeneration is planned for the last microcycle. The 4:1 loading pattern works well during the very early part of the preparatory phase when the athlete is attempting to develop a physiological base, correct technical habits, and learn new technical or tactical skills. A 3:1 loading pattern is also well suited for the preparatory phase and is probably the most common loading plan. The 3:1 loading pattern contains three microcycles with increasing workloads, followed by a regeneration or unloading microcycle. If the level of fatigue is very high after the third microcycle, the load used in the fourth microcycle can be decreased even further, or a second regeneration microcycle can be used to create a 3:2 loading pattern.
summary: This chunk describes common loading patterns used in developmental macrocycles during the preparatory phase.

content: The higher the training stress during the macrocycle, the longer the time before the athlete shows improvement in performance or a supercompensation effect. Halson and colleagues reported that after 2 weeks of very high loading demands, 2 weeks of unloading are needed to return performance capacity to preloading levels. The dynamics of the competitive macrocycles are dictated by the competition calendar. A steady loading pattern should be used throughout the competitive season with team sports in which there are one or two competitions per week. Within this structure, microcycles will vary in intensity and volume, especially microcycles that contain competitions. In these microcycles, the contests will be interspersed with regeneration days and training days that fluctuate between various levels of demand (low to high). To address the unique loading demands of team sports, coaches should consider using various microcycle loading patterns. The macrocycle loading pattern can be 4:1, 3:1, 2:1, 1:1, 2:2, or any other combination.
summary: This chunk discusses macrocycle loading patterns during the competitive phase, emphasizing the need for flexibility and adaptation based on the sport and competition schedule.

content: A steady loading pattern should be used throughout the competitive season with team sports in which there are one or two competitions per week. Within this structure, microcycles will vary in intensity and volume, especially microcycles that contain competitions. In these microcycles, the contests will be interspersed with regeneration days and training days that fluctuate between various levels of demand (low to high).
summary: Team sports with frequent competitions benefit from a steady loading pattern, with microcycles varying in intensity and volume to accommodate games and recovery.

content: To address the unique loading demands of team sports, coaches should consider using various microcycle loading patterns. The macrocycle loading pattern can be 4:1, 3:1, 2:1, 1:1, 2:2, or any other combination. Longer macrocycles (4:1, 3:1) are usually employed during general preparation. During specific preparation, 3:1 and 2:1 are the typical macrocycle structures, where the 2:1 is used mainly for power sports in the flat loading format. During the competitive phase, the unloading microcycles (which allow fatigue to dissipate and performance to elevate) are employed more frequently. Thus the most common macrocycle structures are 2:1, 1:1, and 2:2.
summary: Macrocycle loading patterns for team sports can vary based on the training phase, with longer macrocycles used in general preparation and shorter ones during competition.

content: Two consecutive unloading microcycles (to taper, as explained in the next section) can be planned to peak for an important competition or for when a team faces strong opponents in two consecutive weekends. Another important consideration regarding the structure of the macrocycles is the number of peaks contained in the phase. The qualification competition occurs on July 9 and the main competition on August 14. The results of the qualification contest are used as markers of progress toward the major contest and serve as guidelines for modifying before the major contest. If additional contests are planned between these two contests, the focus will be on performing well instead of training. Additionally, if the number of contests is increased during this macrocycle the athlete would experience a significant increase in fatigue, which might impede performance in the major contests at the end of the cycle.
summary: Macrocycle structure should consider the number of peaks and the timing of competitions, with tapering strategies employed before important events.

content: The first microcycle after the qualifying competition is a regeneration microcycle designed to dissipate fatigue and allow the athlete to recover from the stress of the first competition. The next three microcycles are used to fine-tune technical skills, tactical strategies, and physical capacity. These microcycles are designed to build the athlete’s confidence in his abilities and develop the motivational levels necessary to produce a maximal performance at the major event on August 14.
summary: Following a qualifying competition, a regeneration microcycle is followed by three microcycles focused on skill refinement, confidence building, and preparation for the main event.

content: The 8 to 14 days prior to the August 14 competition are used for peaking (refer to figure 9.3 in chapter 9). During this time the training load is decreased to elevate the level of readiness and, as a result to decrease fatigue. If the peaking phase is structured correctly, performance will be significantly elevated.
summary: The final 8-14 days before a major competition involve a tapering phase to reduce fatigue and maximize performance.

content: The goal of unloading and tapering macrocycles is to remove fatigue to stimulate a supercompensation of performance. Optimal taper or unloading durations appear to be between 8 and 14 days and require a decrease in training load of about 40% to 60%.
summary: Tapering involves reducing training load to remove fatigue and promote performance supercompensation, typically lasting 8-14 days with a 40-60% reduction in load.

content: Four strategies for decreasing the load are available: linear, slow decay, fast decay, or step taper. The type and duration of taper are largely determined by the training load encountered in the weeks before the taper period. For example, if the training load is high, the taper or unloading period may require a longer duration and a greater reduction in training load.
summary: Tapering strategies include linear, slow decay, fast decay, and step taper, with the chosen method and duration depending on the preceding training load.

content: The basic tapering strategies appear to be effective in many sports including weightlifting, track and field, and swimming.
summary: Various tapering strategies are effective across multiple sports, including weightlifting, track and field, and swimming.

content: The transition phase is an important part of the annual training plan. A basic macrocycle structure for a transition phase is presented in figure 7.32. More details about the structure of the transition phase are provided in chapter 8.
summary: The transition phase is a crucial part of the annual training plan, with its structure detailed in chapter 8.

content: The microcycle is the most important and functional part of the annual training plan. The macrocycle is useful for dividing the annual training plan into smaller segments. Ultimately, the macrocycle directs the focus of the microcycle to fulfill the goals of the annual training plan.
summary: The microcycle is the core functional unit of the annual training plan, guided by the macrocycle to achieve overall goals.

content: A microcycle must provide variation in the training load (volume and intensity) to facilitate recovery. The technique used to create this variation in training load is based on the scientific principles related to the ability of the body to recover from training stress. The application of these microcycle models is based on many physiological factors that relate to the body’s ability to tolerate, recover from, and adapt to training stress. If the training loads are varied appropriately, the athlete will be able to recover from and tolerate the training demand, which ultimately improves performance.
summary: Microcycles should incorporate varied training loads to facilitate recovery and optimize performance based on physiological principles.

content: Nevertheless, the microcycle program must not be interpreted rigidly; rather, adjustments can be made upon subjective feedback and objective data based on the athlete’s individual response to the training loads. The acceptable levels of residual fatigue vary in accordance with the objectives of the training session and the phase of the annual plan. It is important to vary the loading patterns between the microcycles.
summary: Microcycle programs should be flexible and adjusted based on individual athlete feedback and data, while varying loading patterns between microcycles.

content: The application of these microcycle models is based on many physiological factors that relate to the body’s ability to tolerate, recover from, and adapt to training stress. If the training loads are varied appropriately, the athlete will be able to recover from and tolerate the training demand, which ultimately improves performance. It is important to vary the loading patterns between the microcycles. This allows the training load to be varied across the macrocycle so the athlete can deal with accumulated fatigue and avoid overtraining. The coach must appropriately sequence developmental, competition, recovery–regeneration, and peaking–unloading microcycles. The microcycle and macrocycle are structured to direct training so that the athlete will achieve a physiological and psychological peak at the appropriate time.
summary: Microcycle planning considers physiological factors, varying training loads, and appropriate sequencing to optimize performance and prevent overtraining.

content: The training plan should be based on the concepts of bioenergetic specificity of training, muscle physiology, hormonal physiology, and the body’s response to training stress. To better guide the training program, the coach should consider quantifying the training stress with the numerical intensity and volume system outlined in this chapter.
summary: Training plans should be grounded in physiological principles and utilize quantifiable metrics to guide training stress.

content: Efficiency is one of the most desired qualities of any professional. Coaches must be well organized and use periodization as a tool for planning an athlete’s training. Even coaches who are extremely knowledgeable can compromise their effectiveness if they lack good planning skills. This book focuses on periodization, which can be used by sports professionals to craft a wide variety of plans. A well-organized coach has at his disposal several types of plans, including short-term plans (e.g., daily session plans), microcycles and macrocycles, and annual plans.
summary: Periodization is a crucial tool for coaches to effectively plan and organize athlete training.

content: At the beginning of a new year of training, the coach should create a blueprint, or annual plan. Although this plan has to be as detailed as possible, equally important for the coach is to also apply it in a very flexible manner. Based on the results in testing, training progress and eventual competitions, the original plan might be slightly or profoundly altered. The coach should reflect on the progress or stagnation of the evolution of performance of the athlete, and as a result, make changes in the plan. Planning and programming should not be rigid.
summary: The annual training plan serves as a flexible framework that should be adapted based on athlete progress and performance.

content: The annual plan and its training phases are essential tools used to maximize an athlete’s physiological adaptation, which is a prerequisite for improved performance. Without the progression of training demand from phase to phase, high performance cannot be achieved. During the last month of training (the transition phase), the plan will vary from the rest of the training year to reduce physiological and psychological stress, remove fatigue, induce regeneration, and prepare the athlete for the next year of training. The goal of training is to induce physiological adaptations and maximize performance at specific points in time, usually during the main competitions of the year. To accomplish this goal, the athlete’s physiological potential must increase at the appropriate time, thereby ensuring a greater potential for a high level of performance. The athlete’s level of readiness for competitions requires a complex interaction of skills, biomotor abilities, psychological traits, well-planned nutrition, and the management of fatigue. The best approach for accomplishing these goals is to use periodized training that is logically constructed and appropriately sequenced. The annual plan is the foundation for stimulating physiological and psychological adaptations while managing fatigue.
summary: The annual training plan, structured with phases, aims to optimize physiological adaptations, manage fatigue, and peak performance at key competitions.

content: In the context of this plan, the greatest challenge is peaking the athlete at the appropriate times throughout the training year. When working with inexperienced athletes, the coach will direct the training plan with little input from the athletes. Conversely, with elite athletes the coach should encourage input from the athletes when establishing the annual training plan’s objectives and structure. By involving elite athletes in the planning process, the coach can create a positive environment in which the athlete can use the planning process as a motivational tool. The annual training plan should contain at least three training phases: preparatory, competitive, and transition. The number of times these three phases are performed will depend on the type of plan used (e.g., monocycle, bi-cycle, tri-cycle, multipeak). The objectives and characteristics of training phases remain consistent regardless of how many times they are repeated throughout the annual training plan. Each training phase must be correctly sequenced, completely integrated, and properly structured to optimize the athlete’s development throughout the annual training plan and elevate training potential and readiness for competitions. The preparatory phase is the most important phase of the annual training plan. The preparatory phase establishes the physical, technical, and psychological base from which the competitive phase is developed. Adaptations developed during the preparatory phase allow the athlete to better tolerate the increase in training intensity that occurs in the competitive phase.
summary: Training plans should be tailored to the athlete's experience level, incorporating athlete input for elite athletes. The annual plan typically includes preparatory, competitive, and transition phases, with the preparatory phase laying the foundation for peak performance.

content: The objectives and characteristics of training phases remain consistent regardless of how many times they are repeated throughout the annual training plan. Each training phase must be correctly sequenced, completely integrated, and properly structured to optimize the athlete’s development throughout the annual training plan and elevate training potential and readiness for competitions.
summary: Training phases have consistent objectives and must be sequenced and structured for optimal athlete development and competition readiness.

content: The preparatory phase is the most important phase of the annual training plan. The preparatory phase establishes the physical, technical, and psychological base from which the competitive phase is developed. Adaptations developed during the preparatory phase allow the athlete to better tolerate the increase in training intensity that occurs in the competitive phase. An inadequate preparatory phase compromises the athlete’s ability to tolerate training and maximize performance during the competitive phase.
summary: The preparatory phase is crucial for establishing a physical, technical, and psychological foundation for the competitive phase.

content: The objectives of the preparatory phase include acquiring and improving general physical training capacity, improving the biomotor abilities required by the sport, cultivating psychological traits, developing, improving, or perfecting technique, familiarizing athletes with the basic strategic maneuvers mastered in the following phase, teaching athletes the theory and methodology of training specific to the sport, and developing an individualized and sport-specific nutrition plan.
summary: The preparatory phase aims to develop general physical capacity, sport-specific abilities, psychological traits, technique, and tactical understanding.

content: The preparatory phase lasts 3 to 6 months depending on the climate, sport, and type of annual plan used (e.g., monocycle, bi-cycle, tri-cycle, multipeak). For individual sports, especially aerobic endurance sports (e.g., marathon, triathlon, Nordic skiing), the preparatory phase is approximately twice as long as the competitive phase. For team sports, the preparatory phase may be somewhat shorter, but not less than 2 to 3 months, especially for younger athletes. For international-class players, the preparatory phase can only be 3 to 5 weeks long for organizational reasons, such as their competition schedule.
summary: The duration of the preparatory phase varies based on sport, athlete level, and training plan type.

content: The preparatory phase is specific to each sport and distinctive for each subphase. For any sport, the preparatory phase should be divided into two subphases: general and specific preparation. For international-class athletes that use a multipeak plan (e.g., team or racquet sports), the general subphase is very short.
summary: The preparatory phase is tailored to each sport and divided into general and specific subphases.

content: The general preparation subphase elevates the athlete’s working capacity, increases general physical preparation, improves technical elements, and enhances basic tactical abilities. The primary emphasis of the general preparation subphase is to establish a high level of physical conditioning, which will improve the athlete’s physiological and psychological capacity to tolerate the demands of both training and competition. A sound physical base is an essential component for the athlete. The base is typically established through the use of general and sport-specific exercises, rather than a reliance on sport-specific skill development. A gymnastics coach may dedicate the first two or three microcycles to the development of general and specific strength needed to master a certain technical element in the following cycles. Many times the inability to develop technical skill is the result of an inadequately developed physical base. It may be warranted to determine whether the athlete possesses adequate physical support for the technical performance of an element or skill.
summary: The general preparation subphase focuses on building a strong physical foundation through general and sport-specific exercises.

content: The general subphase of the preparatory phase includes a high training volume consisting of exercises that require both general and specific effort. The goal of the general subphase is to improve working capacity and psychological drive (determination, perseverance, and willpower) necessary for the sport. For sports in which endurance is the dominant ability or a major contributor to performance (e.g., running, swimming, rowing, Nordic skiing, cycling), 70% to 80% of the total training time may be devoted to the development of aerobic endurance. In sports for which strength, power, and speed are important attributes, this subphase will focus on the development of anatomical adaptations and maximum strength. Increasing the weight lifted or the volume load of training is an objective way to increase working capacity and induce adaptations necessary for the sporting activity.
summary: The general subphase emphasizes high training volume and focuses on developing endurance or strength depending on the sport.

content: While developing the physical base, team sport athletes must also spend substantial time developing technical and tactical skills. The plan must not neglect the development of high-intensity exercise, endurance, strength, and speed because these components of the physical base lay the groundwork for future performance accomplishments. In most sports, the type of training used in the preparatory phase, especially the general subphase, plays a major role in determining the athlete’s performance capacity during the competitive phase. Insufficient emphasis on training volume during the general subphase may account for poor performances, lack of consistency, and a decrease in performance capacity during the later portion of the competitive phase. Consequently, 25% to 33% of the training for the preparatory phase should be allocated to this subphase, with the rest of the preparation phase consisting of sport-specific preparatory activities.
summary: Team sport athletes should balance physical development with technical and tactical training during the preparatory phase.

content: The preparation phase will be longer for novice athletes and should be progressively reduced for advanced athletes. A characteristic of the general preparation phase for team sports is the priority given to physical training in the organization of the microcycles. During the general preparation phase, coaches often plan physical training in the morning and leave the afternoon for technical and tactical training. Increasing training volume during the general preparation subphase is the primary emphasis; although training intensity is important, it is a secondary factor in the preparatory phase. Intensive training can be undertaken but should not exceed 40% of the total amount of training in this subphase, particularly for beginners and juniors. The objective of increasing working capacity during the general preparation subphase is important. With increases in training volume, fatigue will increase markedly, and preparedness will decrease greatly, reducing performance capacity. It is inadvisable to compete during the general preparation subphase because the athlete will have a high level of fatigue, which will reduce performance capacity and increase the risk for injury. When the athlete is highly fatigued as a result of training, technical skills will be relatively unstable and the athlete’s ability to perform specific tactical maneuvers will decrease.
summary: The length and emphasis of the preparatory phase vary based on athlete experience, with a focus on increasing training volume and building a solid physical base.

content: The objective of increasing working capacity during the general preparation subphase is important. It is inadvisable to compete during the general preparation subphase because the athlete will have a high level of fatigue, which will reduce performance capacity and increase the risk for injury. When the athlete is highly fatigued as a result of training, technical skills will be relatively unstable and the athlete’s ability to perform specific tactical maneuvers will decrease. Competition during the general preparation subphase also can negatively affect the athlete’s psychological status, and it decreases the amount of time that can be dedicated to developing the physiological base needed to expand the athlete’s capabilities.
summary: The general preparation subphase focuses on increasing working capacity and avoiding competition due to high fatigue levels, which negatively impact performance, technical skills, and psychological well-being.

content: The specific preparation subphase, or the second part of the preparatory phase, represents a transition from an emphasis on physical development to an emphasis on competition-related activities. Like the general preparation subphase, the specific preparation subphase has the objective of increasing the athlete’s working capacity. However, in this subphase training emphasizes sport-specific activities. Although the volume of work is high during this subphase, the primary emphasis (70% to 80% of total work) is on specific exercises related to the skills or technical elements of the sport. Toward the end of this phase the volume begins to progressively decrease, allowing for a gradual increase in the training intensity. For sports in which intensity is important (e.g., sprinting, jumping, and team sports), the training volume may be lowered by as much as 40% in the later portions of this subphase.
summary: The specific preparation subphase shifts focus to sport-specific activities, increasing intensity while gradually decreasing volume, particularly for sports requiring high intensity.

content: A different approach would be taken for sports that rely on technical mastery and coordinated movements (e.g., figure skating, diving, gymnastics). In these sports it is essential that the athlete continue to perfect and develop the technical proficiency necessary for success during the competitive phase. Similarly, in team sports, racket sports, and martial arts, the specific preparation subphase should focus on the development and improvement of sport-specific technical and tactical elements. This is accomplished with specific exercises that target the prime movers, movement patterns, and technical skills required by the sport. These exercises must be performed in such a way as to create a link between the physical attributes developed in the general preparation subphase and the technical and tactical skills necessary for successful competition.
summary: Sports emphasizing technical mastery require continued refinement of skills during the specific preparation subphase, while team and racket sports focus on developing sport-specific technical and tactical elements.

content: Although the major emphasis is on technical and tactical skill development, a secondary emphasis should be on maintaining general physical development. This secondary focus should contain only a few exercises for general development (a maximum of 20%) that contribute to the athlete’s multilateral development. Establishing a high level of physical conditioning is important before beginning specific training tasks.
summary: While prioritizing technical and tactical skill development, the specific preparation subphase also includes a secondary focus on maintaining general physical fitness.

content: As the athlete’s training shifts toward specialized training, there should be progressive improvement in performance-based testing and athletic performance. In the later stages of this subphase, competitions can be used as evaluative tools that provide feedback about the athlete’s preparation for competition, specifically her technical and tactical development. Information garnered from these competitions can be used to modify training plans to address specific deficiencies.
summary: The specific preparation subphase involves progressive performance improvement, utilizing competitions as feedback mechanisms to refine training plans.

content: Among the main tasks of the competitive phase is the perfection of all training factors, which enables the athlete to compete successfully in the main competitions or championships targeted by the annual training plan. Several general objectives are addressed during the competitive phase, regardless of the sport: Continued improvement or maintenance of sport-specific biomotor abilities, Enhancing psychological traits, Perfecting and consolidating technique, Elevating performance to the highest level, Dissipating fatigue and elevating readiness, Perfecting technical and tactical maneuvers, Gaining competitive experience, Maintaining sport-specific fitness, Designing an individualized nutrition plan.
summary: The competitive phase focuses on perfecting all training factors to achieve peak performance in major competitions, addressing objectives like biomotor ability, psychological traits, technique, and competitive experience.

content: As the athlete progresses to the competitive phase, it is important that the level of physical development established during the preparatory phase be maintained. Maintenance of the physical attributes developed in previous phases is important because they support the other training factors that are developed during the competitive phase. This can be accomplished by dedicating 90% of the total physical preparation activities to sport-specific activities, such as skill-based conditioning exercises and tactical drills in racquet and team sports. The remaining 10% of the planned physical preparation activities can come from nonspecific or indirect activities, such as active rest or game-based activities that are not directly related to the sport being trained.
summary: The competitive phase requires maintaining the physical development from the preparatory phase, with 90% of training focused on sport-specific activities and 10% on general activities.

content: The objectives established for the competitive phase are met by the use of sport-specific training activities that can include technical and tactical exercises. Included in this process may be the use of simulated, exhibition, and actual competitive events. It is essential that the training activities are sport specific to stimulate performance improvement, stabilization, and consistency.
summary: The competitive phase utilizes sport-specific training activities, including simulations and competitions, to optimize performance and consistency.

content: As the athlete progresses through the competitive phase, training becomes more intensive while training volume is decreased. For sports dominated by speed, power, and maximum strength (e.g., sprinting, jumping, throwing, weightlifting), training intensity can increase dramatically while the volume of training is progressively decreased. In endurance sports (e.g., distance running, swimming, cross-country skiing, canoeing, rowing), training volume can be maintained or only slightly lower than that seen in the preparatory phase. An exception to this practice occurs during the competitive microcycle, when intensity decreases according to the number of races and the level of competition.
summary: Training intensity increases and volume decreases as the athlete progresses through the competitive phase, with adjustments based on sport type and competition schedule.

content: As the athlete progresses through the competitive phase, the alterations to the training plan should elevate readiness to achieve high performance. The structure of the training plan will play a large role in stimulating these effects; if the plan is structured correctly, the athlete will optimize performance at the appropriate time. If performance begins to decline or becomes stagnant, it is likely that the amount of work was decreased too much, reducing physical capacity, or that the work was maintained at too high a level and fatigue is masking the potential performance gains. The modulation between work and performance appears to be an art based on science, and the integration of athlete monitoring and coaching experience will guide the decisions made during this phase of training.
summary: The competitive phase involves fine-tuning training plans to optimize performance, requiring careful monitoring and adjustments based on athlete feedback and coaching expertise.

content: Intensive training can be undertaken but should not exceed 40% of the total amount of training in this subphase, particularly for beginners and juniors.
summary: Intensive training should be limited to 40% of the total training volume, especially for beginners and juniors.

content: With increases in training volume, fatigue will increase markedly, and preparedness will decrease greatly, reducing performance capacity.
summary: Increased training volume leads to higher fatigue levels and reduced performance capacity.

content: The structure of the training plan will play a large role in stimulating these effects; if the plan is structured correctly, the athlete will optimize performance at the appropriate time. The length of the competitive phase depends on the sport and the type of annual training plan. Long competitive phases (8 to 10 months) are typically seen in team sports as a result of the set league schedules and international championships. Conversely, athletes in individual sports have more freedom in determining their competitive schedule, allowing for more control over the length of the competitive phase and the structure of training leading into the major competition of the year. Regardless of the sport, one of the most important factors in determining the duration and structure of the competitive phase is the phase’s start date.
summary: The competitive phase's length and structure depend on the sport and its start date, with team sports often having longer phases compared to individual sports.

content: When structuring the competitive phase and its start date consider the following parameters: The number of competitions required to reach the highest performance (on average it takes between 7 and 10 competitions to achieve the highest performance results), The amount of time or interval between competitions, The duration of eventual qualifying meets, The time required for special preparation before the main competition of the year, The time needed for recovery and regeneration.
summary: Structuring the competitive phase requires considering factors like the number of competitions, intervals between them, qualifying meet duration, preparation time, and recovery needs.

content: When structuring the competitive phase of the annual training plan, it may be warranted to divide the phase into two subphases: the precompetitive subphase and the main competitive subphase. The precompetitive phase generally contains unofficial competitions or, in the case of team sports, exhibition games. Although this subphase is an integral part of the competitive phase, the objective is not to achieve the highest level of competition. This subphase should serve as a period of preparation and fine-tuning for the main competitive subphase. Exhibition or unofficial contests allow for objective feedback about the athlete’s training level and readiness for future contests. Exhibitions and unofficial competitions should not significantly alter the training program, especially for elite athletes.
summary: The competitive phase can be divided into precompetitive and main competitive subphases, with the former focusing on preparation and fine-tuning before the main competitions.

content: The main competitive subphase is dedicated strictly to maximizing an athlete’s capacity and readiness, thus allowing for superior performances at the main contests. The training content of the main competitive subphase should be centered on sport-specific methods and the maintenance of specific physical development. Although the training volume may still be high for endurance sports, the coach can reduce the training volume to 50% to 75% of the level of the preparatory phase for sports that require technical mastery, speed, strength, or power. While volume is decreasing, the intensity of training gradually increases, with the highest levels occurring 2 or 3 weeks prior to the main competition.
summary: The main competitive subphase prioritizes maximizing performance through sport-specific training, gradually increasing intensity while decreasing volume.

content: A loading microcycle may have 10 to 14 training sessions per week, whereas an unloading microcycle will contain many fewer sessions. The stress curve will be elevated during the competitive phase as a result of the increased intensity of training and the stress of participation in competitions. The stress curve should have an undulating shape, reflecting the fluctuations between stressful activities (e.g., competitions, intense training sessions, social factors) and short periods of regeneration. If possible, the coach should arrange competitions progressively in order of importance, concluding with the main competitions. Another organizational strategy is to introduce main competitions interspersed with lesser competitions that allow the athlete to continue to train without visible changes in the training plan (e.g., lowering volume or intensity) and without longer unloading periods to trigger peak performance. By including six to eight microcycles prior to the main competition, the coach can focus the training program and daily cycle on the specific requirements of the competition. Preparing the athlete for the specific competitive environment and demands will prevent any surprises and enhance the athlete’s performance.
summary: The competitive phase involves undulating stress curves, strategically placed competitions, and microcycles tailored to specific competition demands.

content: In this portion of the competitive phase, 8 to 14 days of unloading will be used to peak the athlete. The unloading or tapering subphase offers the best way to elevate the athlete’s readiness and stimulate a supercompensation of performance that will increase the athlete’s performance potential during the competition. Peaking is accomplished via manipulating both volume and intensity to reduce the accumulated fatigue stimulated from previous training and competition; this will allow the athlete to rest and regenerate before the major competition. The unloading or tapering subphase, especially for individual sports, should last 8 to 14 days and can include various methods of reducing volume and training intensity. Classically, for endurance sports, it is advisable to reduce intensity and maintain volume because endurance athletes are better adapted to tolerate high-volume training than high-intensity training. The unloading process involves reducing the number of daily training sessions and modulating training intensity to begin the recovery process. The coach should eliminate all extraneous activities that can contribute to the athlete’s fatigue and encourage the athlete to use free time to rest and recover for the impending competition.
summary: The unloading or tapering subphase, lasting 8-14 days, involves reducing volume and intensity to allow for peak performance during competition.

content: During the first microcycle of the unloading period, it may be warranted to reduce the volume and frequency (two sessions per week) of strength training. Further reductions in the volume and intensity of training may be planned during the second microcycle of the unloading period. Strength training may be limited to one or two sessions, or removed completely depending on the sport during the second microcycle of the unloading period. The volume and intensity of other training factors should also be reduced. The same unloading approach is used for sports dominated by speed, power, or technical proficiency. In the first microcycle of the unloading period, the training volume is reduced by 40% to 50% depending on the level of training undertaken prior to the taper.
summary: The unloading process involves gradually reducing volume and intensity over two microcycles, with specific adjustments based on the sport and training level.

content: An exception to this practice occurs during the competitive microcycle, when intensity decreases according to the number of races and the level of competition. If performance begins to decline or becomes stagnant, it is likely that the amount of work was decreased too much, reducing physical capacity, or that the work was maintained at too high a level and fatigue is masking the potential performance gains. As the athlete progresses through the competitive phase, the alterations to the training plan should elevate readiness to achieve high performance. The modulation between work and performance appears to be an art based on science, and the integration of athlete monitoring and coaching experience will guide the decisions made during this phase of training.
summary: Training adjustments during the competitive phase involve balancing work and performance based on athlete monitoring and coaching experience.

content: The volume and intensity of other training factors should also be reduced. The same unloading approach is used for sports dominated by speed, power, or technical proficiency. With these types of sports, the volume and frequency of strength training should be reduced while maintaining moderate to high intensities with slightly longer rest intervals. Complete removal of strength training may not be warranted because power and speed are highly dependent on strength levels.
summary: This chunk outlines the unloading approach for sports emphasizing speed, power, or technical skill, involving reduced volume and intensity across training, with adjustments to strength training frequency and intensity.

content: In the first microcycle of the unloading period, the training volume is reduced by 40% to 50% depending on the level of training undertaken prior to the taper. During the second microcycle of the unloading period, further reductions in the volume and intensity of training may be planned.  This period should include several short but high-intensity sessions to maintain the adaptations induced by previous training phases. During the high-intensity training session, all exercises should be dynamic and of short duration and should contain medium- to high-intensity loading. The other sessions in the microcycle should alternate submaximal intensities between low and very low intensities.
summary: This chunk details the unloading period, specifically the first and second microcycles, emphasizing volume reduction, high-intensity maintenance, and alternating submaximal intensities.

content: During the second microcycle of this subphase, in which the main competition occurs, the coach continues to reduce training volume and the intensity of training. Only one peak occurs during the early portion of this microcycle. Across this microcycle the objective is to maximally reduce fatigue and stress while increasing preparedness and maintaining the physiological adaptations that have been established.
summary: This chunk focuses on the second microcycle of the competition subphase, highlighting continued volume and intensity reduction, a single peak, and the goal of minimizing fatigue while maximizing preparedness.

content: The special preparation period can last between 3 and 7 days, depending on the characteristics of the competition. During this phase certain training aspects, especially tactical elements, are altered according to the latest information on opponents or the competitive schedule. The vast majority of the training in this phase follows the model concept, with the purpose of enhancing preparation for the upcoming competition. One aspect that has important implications for the final result is the special psychological preparations that target relaxation, confidence building, and motivation. However, these techniques should be used cautiously because overemphasis on psychological elements can impair performance.
summary: This chunk describes the special preparation period, its duration, tactical adjustments, training focus, and the importance of psychological preparation while cautioning against overemphasis.

content: Each athlete is different and will require specific preparation activities to meet his individual needs. The transition phase links two annual training plans. The transition phase facilitates psychological rest, relaxation, and biological regeneration while maintaining an acceptable level of general physical preparation (40% to 50% of the competitive phase. Training during the transition phase should be low key: All loading factors should be reduced; the main training components should be centered on general training, with minimal, if any, technical or tactical development.
summary: This chunk emphasizes individual needs, the role of the transition phase in facilitating rest and regeneration, and the low-key training approach during this period.

content: The transition phase generally lasts 2 to 4 weeks but could be extended to 6 weeks, especially for younger athletes. Under normal circumstances, the transition phase should not last longer than 6 weeks. One approach to the transition phase encourages complete rest with no physical activity. An abrupt interruption of training and complete inactivity can lead to significant detraining even if undertaken for a short period of time (<4 weeks). Detraining can cause a substantial loss in the physiological adaptations established in the previous months of training.
summary: This chunk details the typical duration of the transition phase, its potential extension for younger athletes, and the risks associated with complete inactivity and detraining.

content: Some authors have suggested that an abrupt cessation of training by highly trained athletes creates a phenomenon known as detraining syndrome, relaxation syndrome (20, 21), exercise abstinence, or exercise dependency syndrome (18). Detraining syndrome can be characterized by insomnia, anxiety, depression, alterations to cardiovascular function, and a loss of appetite. The symptoms of detraining syndrome are usually not pathological and can be reversed if training is resumed within a short time. If the cessation of training is prolonged, the symptoms of detraining syndrome can become more pronounced, indicating that the athlete’s body is unable to adapt to this sudden inactivity. The time frame in which symptoms of detraining syndrome manifest themselves is highly specific to the individual athlete; generally, it can occur within 2 to 3 weeks of inactivity and varies in severity. Simply decreasing the level of training can also stimulate a detraining effect that will decrease physiological and performance capacity.
summary: This chunk explores detraining syndrome, its symptoms, reversibility, and the impact of prolonged inactivity on athletes.

content: Detraining syndrome can be characterized by insomnia, anxiety, depression, alterations to cardiovascular function, and a loss of appetite. The symptoms of detraining syndrome are usually not pathological and can be reversed if training is resumed within a short time. If the cessation of training is prolonged, the symptoms of detraining syndrome can become more pronounced, indicating that the athlete’s body is unable to adapt to this sudden inactivity. The time frame in which symptoms of detraining syndrome manifest themselves is highly specific to the individual athlete; generally, it can occur within 2 to 3 weeks of inactivity and varies in severity.
summary: Detraining syndrome encompasses various symptoms, including psychological and cardiovascular changes, which typically appear within 2-3 weeks of inactivity and are reversible with resumed training.

content: Simply decreasing the level of training can also stimulate a detraining effect that will decrease physiological and performance capacity. The magnitude of the detraining effects will be related to the duration of the detraining period. Short-term detraining, which occurs in less than 4 weeks, can result in some significant decreases in endurance and strength performance (15). In endurance athletes, short-term detraining has been reported to result in a 4% to 25% decrease in time to exhaustion and a substantial reduction in endurance performance (20). It has been postulated that the reductions in endurance performance are largely dictated by the decline in cardiorespiratory fitness noted in response to short-term detraining (14).
summary: Decreasing training intensity or volume can lead to detraining effects, with greater impacts on performance and physiological capacity over longer durations.

content: Maximal aerobic capacity can be reduced by 4% in as little as 4 days of detraining (28); decreased by 7% within 3 weeks of the cessation of training (4); and reduced by 14% in as little as 4 weeks of detraining (20). If the detraining period is extended to 8 weeks, aerobic capacity can continue to decrease up to 20% of pre-detraining values (21). These reductions in aerobic capacity are most likely related to specific alterations to the cardiorespiratory system, including decreases in blood volume, stroke volume, and maximal cardiac output (table 8.2). These detraining-induced physiological alterations appear to occur progressively and proportionally to the training status of the athlete; this suggests that highly trained endurance athletes will experience a greater magnitude of decline in both physiological and performance capacity.
summary: Detraining leads to a decline in maximal aerobic capacity, with the magnitude of reduction increasing with detraining duration and being more pronounced in highly trained athletes.

content: Short-term detraining (<4 weeks) results in a decrease in maximal oxygen uptake, an increase in blood volume, an increase in submaximal heart rate, an increase in recovery heart rate, a decrease in stroke volume during exercise, a decrease in maximal cardiac output, a decrease in ventricular mass and dimension, an increase in mean blood pressure, a decrease in maximal ventilatory volume, an increase in submaximal ventilatory volume, a decrease in oxygen pulse, an increase in ventilatory equivalent, and a decrease in endurance performance. Long-term detraining (>4 weeks) results in a decrease in maximal oxygen uptake, an increase in blood volume, an increase in submaximal heart rate, an increase in recovery heart rate, a decrease in stroke volume during exercise, a decrease in maximal cardiac output, a decrease in ventricular mass and dimension, an increase in mean blood pressure, a decrease in maximal ventilatory volume, an increase in submaximal ventilatory volume, a decrease in oxygen pulse, an increase in ventilatory equivalent, and a decrease in endurance performance.
summary: Short-term and long-term detraining both lead to reductions in aerobic capacity, cardiovascular function, and endurance performance, with some physiological adaptations initially increasing before declining.

content: 4 weeks of detraining in which strength training is completely removed from the training plan results in a 6% to 10% reduction in maximum muscular strength and a 14% to 17% decrease in maximum power generation capacity. The reduction in the ability to express muscular strength and power characteristics depends on the magnitude of the reduction of muscle cross-sectional area and electromyographic activity. The largest decrease in the expression of muscular strength occurs during the first 4 weeks (10% decrease), extending the detraining period to 8 weeks will result in a continued performance reduction (11% to 12% decrease). These reductions in performance appear to occur at a greater rate and magnitude in highly trained individuals compared with recreational athletes and untrained people; the latter appear able to maintain both strength and power performance in response to 2 to 3 weeks of detraining.
summary: Detraining from strength training leads to significant reductions in maximal strength and power, with the magnitude of decline being greater in highly trained individuals.

content: Short-term detraining (<4 weeks) results in a decrease in capillary density, no data available on arterial-venous oxygen difference, altered fiber type distribution, a decrease in fiber cross-sectional area, no data available on type II:I area ratio, no data available on muscle mass, a decrease in EMG activity, a decrease in strength-power performance, a decrease in oxidative enzyme capacity, no data available on glycogen synthase activity, a decrease in mitochondrial ATP production, no data available on metabolic characteristics, and a decrease in muscle glycogen level. Long-term detraining (>4 weeks) results in a decrease in capillary density, a decrease in arterial-venous oxygen difference, altered fiber type distribution, a decrease in fiber cross-sectional area, a decrease in type II:I area ratio, a decrease in muscle mass, a decrease in EMG activity, a decrease in strength-power performance, a decrease in oxidative enzyme capacity, no data available on glycogen synthase activity, a decrease in mitochondrial ATP production, no data available on metabolic characteristics, and a decrease in muscle glycogen level.
summary: Detraining leads to reductions in muscle fiber characteristics, oxidative capacity, and glycogen stores, with both short-term and long-term detraining exhibiting similar trends.

content: The transition phase begins immediately after the completion of the main competition and can last between 2 and 4 weeks. Active rest refers to participating in a compatible sport or using a period of low-volume and low-intensity training within the athlete’s sport. By using this approach, the athlete will be able to minimize the loss of physiological adaptation and maintain some level of general fitness. Passive rest can be used during the transition phase. Passive rest may be necessary if an athlete has injuries. If active rest is used during the transition phase, the volume and intensity of training are substantially reduced and may target movement patterns or activities that are not used in training. During the second to fourth microcycle of the transition phase (in a 4-week transition), the volume and intensity of training can remain low or increase slightly. The activity used for active rest must match the bioenergetic characteristics of the sport being trained for. A cyclist may use cross-country skiing or running as a transition activity. A volleyball player may use basketball as a transition activity. The transition phase is a period during which the athlete can recover physically and psychologically while minimizing the loss of fitness. During the transition phase, the coach and athlete should analyze the training program, performance results, and testing outcomes.
summary: The transition phase, lasting 2-4 weeks after the main competition, allows for recovery and analysis while minimizing fitness loss through active or passive rest strategies.

content: Figure 8.4 illustrates a monocycle model of an annual training plan. The monocycle model is appropriate for seasonal sports with one major competitive phase. The model is based on the specifics of training for speed and power sports such as sprinting, jumping, and throwing events in track and field. The volume and intensity curves in Figure 8.4 may not be appropriate for sports that are dominated by endurance. Annual training plans differ according to the requirements of the sport. Seasonal sports such as skiing, canoeing, cycling, triathlon, soccer, and sports with one major competition during the year usually require one competitive phase.
summary: The monocycle model, suitable for seasonal sports with a single competitive phase, outlines training volume and intensity fluctuations throughout the year, with adjustments needed for endurance-dominant sports.

content: The monocycle model is appropriate for seasonal sports with one major competitive phase. Annual training plans differ according to the requirements of the sport. Seasonal sports such as skiing, canoeing, cycling, triathlon, soccer, and sports with one major competition during the year usually require one competitive phase. Annual training plans for seasonal sports can be classified as monocycles because they contain only one competitive phase and one major peak.
summary: Monocycle training plans are designed for seasonal sports with a single peak competition.

content: Monocycle plans are divided into three major phases: preparatory, competitive, and transition phases. The preparatory phase in monocycle plans includes both general and specific phases of preparation. As the general preparation phase decreases, the specific preparation phase increases substantially. In some instances, such as in soccer, the general preparation phase can be very short or can be eliminated altogether, especially for national and international-class players.
summary: Monocycle plans consist of preparatory, competitive, and transition phases, with the preparatory phase focusing on both general and specific training.

content: The competitive phase in monocycle plans is divided into several smaller subphases. The precompetitive subphase, which usually includes exhibition competitions only, comes before the main competition subphase in which all official competitions are scheduled. Before the most important competition of the year, two shorter subphases should be planned. The first subphase is an unloading phase or taper, which is generally marked by lower volumes and intensities of training. The unloading phase allows for the removal of fatigue and an elevation in physical and psychological potential, which creates a performance supercompensation effect. After the unloading phase, a special preparation phase follows, during which technical and tactical changes can be made. The special preparation phase can occur in conjunction with the unloading phase or can be a separate subphase.
summary: The competitive phase of a monocycle plan includes subphases like pre-competition, main competition, unloading, and special preparation.

content: During the preparatory phase and early competitive phase, training volume is emphasized with lower intensities according to the specifics of the sport. During the preparatory phase, the quantity of work is very high and the intensity of work is correspondingly lower. As the competitive phase approaches, the training volume decreases to allow the athletes to use most of their energy for high-intensity training for speed- and power-dominant sports.
summary: Training volume is prioritized in the preparatory phase, gradually decreasing as the competitive phase approaches.

content: The model is based on the specifics of training for speed and power sports such as sprinting, jumping, and throwing events in track and field. The volume and intensity curves in Figure 8.4 may not be appropriate for sports that are dominated by endurance.
summary: The monocycle model is tailored for speed and power sports, with potential modifications needed for endurance-focused disciplines.

content: Figure 8.5 is an example of an annual training plan for endurance sports, where the bioenergetic contribution is 50%:50% (anaerobic:aerobic), or an even higher proportion for aerobic endurance. The dynamics of the volume of training in Figure 8.5 demonstrate that aerobic metabolism is clearly dominant. Therefore, the training volume curve must be high throughout the competitive phase.
summary: Endurance sports require a different training approach, emphasizing high training volume throughout the competitive phase.

content: An annual training plan that contains two peaks, or a bi-cycle, is used because there are two distinct competitive phases. The first preparatory phase lasts approximately 3 months and is broken into general and specific subphases. The first competitive phase lasts about 2 1/2 months and brings the athlete to a peak performance. The first transition phase lasts approximately 1 to 2 weeks and is marked by a period of unloading to recover the athlete. The second preparatory phase is shorter, but slightly more specific, than the first preparatory phase, lasting approximately 2 months. The second competitive phase is slightly longer, about 3 1/2 months, and brings the athlete to a peak performance. The second transition phase is approximately 1 to 1 1/2 months long and is used to unload and recover the athlete.
summary: Bi-cycle training plans feature two distinct competitive peaks separated by transition phases.

content: A bi-cycle plan contains two short monocycles that are linked by a very short unloading and transition phase. The training volume in preparatory phase 1 is much greater than that in preparatory phase 2. The level of readiness will be lower during competitive phase 1. The outdoor championships are considered to be more important than the indoor competitions. The second competitive phase of the annual plan should target the major competition.
summary: Bi-cycle plans consist of two linked monocycles with varying training volumes and readiness levels.

content: Other sports such as boxing, wrestling, martial arts, and gymnastics may have three major competitions during the annual plan. Assuming each competition is 3 or 4 months apart, the athlete would have three competitive phases, which would create a tri-cycle annual training plan structure. Preparatory phase 1 is the longest preparatory phase of the annual training plan, lasting around 2 months. Competitive phase 1 is the shortest of the three competitive phases in the annual training plan, lasting around 1 1/2 months. The first transition phase is very short and links the first competitive phase with the second preparatory phase.
summary: Tri-cycle training plans accommodate three major competitions throughout the year.

content: Other sports such as boxing, wrestling, martial arts, and gymnastics may have three major competitions during the annual plan. Assuming each competition is 3 or 4 months apart, the athlete would have three competitive phases, which would create a tri-cycle annual training plan structure. This type of plan is typically used with advanced athletes only, and the first preparatory phase contains the general preparation subphase. In a tri-cycle plan, the most important competition should occur during the last cycle of the year. The first of the three preparatory phases should be the longest, during which the athlete builds the technical, tactical, and physical foundation from which the next two cycles are built. In this type of plan, the volume curve is the highest in the first preparatory phase.
