summary: Early strength gains are primarily neurological, while long-term gains are limited by morphological factors. The time frame for neurological adaptations varies depending on exercise complexity.

content: The experience with intermediate and advanced strength athletes (powerlifters, weightlifters), increasing their strength and power levels year after year despite not gaining in body mass, indicates that neurological factors, especially intermuscular coordination, may have a predominant role in force production over the long term. With the initiation of a strength training program, the primary adaptations that affect performance relate to motor learning and coordination.
summary: Long-term strength gains in experienced athletes are likely driven by neurological factors, particularly intermuscular coordination.

content: Chilibeck and colleagues (29) suggested that complex exercises that involve more than one joint (e.g., squat, power clean, pulls) may require more time for neural adaptations to be manifested. Those investigators noted that the neurological adaptations in the upper arm occurred very rapidly, because hypertrophy was noted after 10 weeks of performing the biceps curl. Conversely, significant hypertrophy was not noted in the legs until after 20 weeks of leg press training.
summary: Complex exercises may take longer for neural adaptations compared to isolated exercises, as demonstrated by the faster upper arm hypertrophy compared to leg hypertrophy.

content: With the initiation of a strength training program, the primary adaptations that affect performance relate to motor learning and coordination. These adaptations appear to be very specific to the movement pattern and the sequence of muscle contraction. Strength training has the potential to alter the motor unit recruitment patterns, motor unit rate coding, and degree of motor unit synchronization.
summary: Initial strength training adaptations focus on motor learning and coordination, influencing motor unit recruitment, rate coding, and synchronization.

content: Explosive strength training with high loads (e.g., jump squats) has been shown to lower the motor unit recruitment thresholds and increase the motor unit rate coding. A high loads strength training program has the potential to increase motor unit synchronization and reduce neural inhibition. These neurological adaptations appear to alter force-generating capacity and the rate of force generation, which both can affect sports performance.
summary: Explosive strength training improves force production by lowering motor unit recruitment thresholds, increasing rate coding, and enhancing synchronization.

content: The occurrence of muscle hypertrophy in response to a strength training program results in changes to the muscle architecture. These architectural alterations can increase contractile material, which in turn can increase force production capacity. The most significant morphological change noted in most strength training studies is an increase in muscle hypertrophy. Most short-term strength training studies demonstrate significant hypertrophy of type II muscle fibers, whereas long-term studies demonstrate hypertrophy of both type II and type I fibers.
summary: Muscle hypertrophy, primarily of type II fibers initially, increases contractile material and force production capacity.

content: Muscle hypertrophy is marked by significant increases in the cross-sectional area of the skeletal muscle fibers, which can result in an increase in contractile material and an increase in the angle of pennation of the muscle. These two morphological adaptations significantly enhance muscular strength adaptations in response to a strength training program. It appears that explosive strength training has the potential to significantly increase type II fiber size, significantly altering the ratio of type II to type I muscle fiber cross-sectional area, which favors maximum strength and power-generating capacity.
summary: Muscle hypertrophy, including increased cross-sectional area and pennation angle, enhances strength. Explosive training favors type II fiber growth, maximizing strength and power.

content: Bodybuilders' data suggest that the type of strength training used can dictate the types of morphological changes expressed by skeletal muscle. Another potential positive morphological adaptation to a strength training program is an alteration in the muscle fiber type. The most consistent fiber type adaptation seen in response to a training program is a reduction in type IIx fiber distribution with a concomitant increase in type IIa distribution, especially if the training is anaerobic lactic.
summary: Training type influences muscle morphology, including fiber type shifts towards more fatigue-resistant type IIa fibers with anaerobic training.

content: Newer analytical techniques suggest that muscles exhibit an even greater plasticity and thus may express greater alterations in response to training or detraining. Contemporary research has examined myosin heavy chain (MHC) content when identifying muscle composition. MHC composition is closely associated with the classic fiber typing methods. Current literature reveals that in addition to the major categories of MHC (type I, type IIa, and type IIx), there exists a pool of hybrid fibers (type I/IIa, type I/IIa/IIx, and type IIa/IIx). This pool of hybrids can be altered by strength training, endurance training, and bed rest. As this pool is modified, the percentage of type IIx, IIa, or I fibers can be altered, which partially explains the fiber type compositions of different classifications of strength athletes.
summary: Muscle plasticity allows for significant adaptations in response to training, including changes in MHC composition and hybrid fiber distribution, contributing to the diverse fiber types seen in athletes.

content: General strength refers to the strength of the whole muscular system. This strength is the foundation for the strength program and must be developed for ultimate performance to be achieved. Coaches can focus on general strength during the preparatory phase of training or during the first few years of training in novice athletes. If general strength is not adequately developed, the athlete’s progress may be impeded.
summary: General strength, developed early in training, is crucial for overall performance and progress.

content: Specific strength relates to the motor patterns of muscle groups that are essential to a sport activity, as well as their ergogenesis. Athletes usually work on specific strength at the end of the preparation phase.
summary: Specific strength, targeting sport-relevant muscle groups, is developed later in training.

content: Power is the ability to develop force rapidly and at high velocities. This is essential in most sports, especially in athletics, team, and racquet sports. Periodization of strength is best developed during the specific preparation phase and within the competition phase of training.
summary: Power, crucial for many sports, is developed through periodization during specific preparation and competition phases.

content: Maximum strength refers to the highest force the neuromuscular system can generate during a maximum voluntary contraction. Maximum strength is demonstrated by the highest load that an athlete can lift one time. Maximum strength has been related to factors such as muscle endurance, weightlifting performance, and speed.
summary: Maximum strength, measured by one-rep max, is linked to factors like muscle endurance, weightlifting performance, and speed.

content: Muscle endurance is the ability of the neuromuscular system to produce force in a repetitive fashion over extended periods of time. The total number of repetitions that can be lifted with a specific load is a marker of muscle endurance.
summary: Muscle endurance, measured by repetitions with a specific load, reflects the ability to sustain force production over time.

content: Maximum strength refers to the highest force the neuromuscular system can generate during a maximum voluntary contraction. Maximum strength is demonstrated by the highest load that an athlete can lift one time. An athlete’s absolute maximal strength capacity can be measured with one repetition maximum testing (one-repetition maximum). Knowledge of the athlete’s maximum capacity is necessary to calculate the training loads needed within a periodized training system.
summary: Maximum strength, measured by one-repetition maximum, is the highest force the neuromuscular system can generate and is crucial for training load calculations.

content: Absolute strength refers to the amount of force that can be generated regardless of body weight. Relative strength is the ratio between an athlete’s maximum strength and his body weight. The ratio for evaluating relative strength is calculated by the athlete’s absolute strength divided by his body weight.
summary: Absolute strength is the force generated regardless of body weight, while relative strength is the ratio of absolute strength to body weight.

content: Muscle endurance is the ability of the neuromuscular system to produce force in a repetitive fashion over extended periods of time. The total number of repetitions that can be lifted with a specific load is a marker of muscle endurance.
summary: Muscle endurance is the ability to repeatedly produce force over time, measured by the number of repetitions with a given load.

content: Strength training entails using various loads and methods to develop muscular strength and power. The preferred method of strength training combines the use of free weights with other methods of developing strength and power such as plyometrics, medicine ball work, and agility training.
summary: Strength training involves various methods, including free weights, plyometrics, and medicine ball work, to develop strength and power.

content: Using multijoint, large muscle mass exercises (e.g., cleans, arm pulls, squats) provides greater transfer to the athlete’s sporting events compared with single-joint, small muscle mass exercises.
summary: Multijoint exercises like cleans, squats, and arm pulls are more effective for transferring strength to athletic performance than single-joint exercises.

content: When designing a strength training program, the coach should consider the concept of progressive overload. Progressive overload can be accomplished through the manipulation of many of the training variables, such as changing the loads used, altering the number of repetitions or sets in the training program, varying the frequency of training, altering the rest interval between sets or repetitions, and changing the exercises in the training program.
summary: Progressive overload, achieved by manipulating training variables like load, reps, sets, frequency, and rest intervals, is crucial for strength training program design.

content: An effective strength training program systematically manipulates many training factors in a periodized fashion. Coaches can optimize the training plan by methodically managing the volume and the intensity of training. Early in the training plan, during the preparatory phase, volume will be higher, intensity will be lower, and sport-specific training will be minimized. As the athlete moves closer to the competitive phase, there is a general downward shift in training volume and an increase in training intensity and sport-specific training.
summary: Effective strength training programs are periodized, systematically adjusting volume and intensity throughout the training cycle, with higher volume and lower intensity in the preparatory phase and a shift towards higher intensity and sport-specific training as competition approaches.

content: Although the manipulation of volume and intensity of training is extremely important, it is also important to manipulate other variables associated with the training plan, such as the frequency of training, the order of exercises, the interset rest intervals, and the exercises selected.
summary: Beyond volume and intensity, other training variables like frequency, exercise order, rest intervals, and exercise selection should be manipulated for optimal training.

content: Body weight resistance can be used to increase strength because of the actions of gravity on the body. Body weight exercises include push-ups, pull-ups, chin-ups, dips, and stair climbing.
summary: Body weight exercises utilize gravity to provide resistance and can be effective for strength development.

content: Elastic bands can create resistive forces when stretched. As forces exerted against an elastic band increase, the resistance becomes greater and the movement slower. Elastic bands apply less force at the initiation of a jump and greater force before the athlete has left the ground.
summary: Elastic bands provide variable resistance, increasing as force applied increases, and can be used for specific training applications like jumps.

content: Using a weighted vest or jumping with free weights applies force consistently throughout the movement. The only applications for elastic bands in sport training are in water sports, as they mimic the increase of resistance encountered as the arm of the swimmer or the oar of the rower get deeper in the water, or as a tool for accommodating resistance in strength exercises used for power training if the ballistic method is not utilized.
summary: Weighted vests and free weights provide consistent resistance, while elastic bands are primarily used in water sports or for accommodating resistance in power training.

content: Weighted objects can include medicine balls, power balls, kettlebells, and bags of sand. Resistive force is created mostly by the force of gravity on weighted objects.
summary: Weighted objects like medicine balls, kettlebells, and sandbags utilize gravity to create resistance for training.

content: With weight stack machines, resistance is provided by the action of gravity on the resistance. The direction of the force in weight stack machines is controlled by the use of pulleys, cables, cams, and gears.
summary: Weight stack machines use gravity and mechanical systems to provide resistance and control movement direction.

content: It has been suggested that weight stack machines do not match human strength curve patterns.
summary: Weight stack machines may not fully replicate the natural strength curve of human movement.

content: Fluid resistance machines create resistive forces by moving the body or apparatus through a fluid, moving fluid past an object, moving fluid around an object, or moving fluid through an orifice. The fluid used in fluid resistance machines can be either a liquid or a gas.
summary: Fluid resistance machines utilize the properties of liquids or gases to create resistance during movement.

content: Fluid-based resistive devices do not provide for eccentric muscle actions, nor for acceleration.
summary: Fluid resistance machines lack the ability to fully replicate eccentric muscle actions and acceleration phases of movement.

content: Free weights, such as dumbbells and barbells, are considered the gold standard of strength training. Free weights most closely match the human strength curve. Free weights use gravity to apply the resistive forces.
summary: Free weights are considered the most effective for strength training due to their ability to closely mimic natural movement patterns and utilize gravity for resistance.

content: Isometric methods apply resistive forces in which the contractile forces equal the resistive forces. An example of an isometric muscle action is pushing with maximal forces against an immovable object.
summary: Isometric exercises involve generating force against a fixed resistance, such as pushing against an immovable object.

content: Free weights, such as dumbbells and barbells, are considered the gold standard of strength training. Free weights most closely match the human strength curve. Free weights use gravity to apply the resistive forces.
summary: Free weights are the preferred tool for strength training due to their close match to the human strength curve and use of gravity for resistance.

content: Isometric methods apply resistive forces in which the contractile forces equal the resistive forces. An example of an isometric muscle action is pushing with maximal forces against an immovable object.
summary: Isometric training involves applying maximal force against a stationary object, where muscle contraction and resistance are equal.

content: Volume of training can be quantified as the amount of work performed and can incorporate total training hours, number of kilograms lifted, metric ton or short tons lifted per training session, phase of training or per year, and the number of sets and repetitions completed. In the literature, volume is traditionally represented as a metric ton. A metric ton is equivalent to 2,204.6 lb (1,000 kg), whereas a short ton is equal to 2,430.2 lb (1,102.3 kg). The volume of a training session is calculated by multiplying the weight lifted by the number of sets and the number of repetitions, which yields a volume load value.
summary: Training volume is a measure of total work performed, often expressed in metric tons, and calculated by multiplying weight, sets, and repetitions.

content: The total workload in an annual training plan can approach 3,726 metric tons for elite weightlifters, with 2,789 metric tons in the preparatory phase and 937 metric tons in the competition phase. The number of metric tons encountered in a training session depends on the sport being trained for, the developmental status of the athlete, and the phase of training. As the athlete becomes more developed, he can tolerate higher training session or microcycle volume loads. It is common for weightlifters to train with 10 to 60 metric tons in a microcycle. The microcycle volume can vary drastically depending on the sport and the phase of training.
summary: Training volume varies greatly depending on the sport, athlete development, and training phase, with elite weightlifters potentially handling thousands of metric tons annually.

content: The training intensity, or load, relates to the amount of weight or resistance being used. The intensity of a training session can be calculated by dividing the volume load by the total number of repetitions completed. The load used in strength training is best expressed as a percentage of one-repetition maximum (1RM).
summary: Training intensity refers to the weight used, often expressed as a percentage of 1RM, and can be calculated by dividing volume load by repetitions.

content: Some strength and conditioning professionals recommend using repetitions to failure with repetition maximum zones (e.g., 1-3RM, 8-12RM, or 13-15RM) as a method for determining training intensity. However, using training to failure in the development of maximum strength has been consistently questioned and is not the optimal method for loading during strength training. This contention is supported by research from Izquierdo and colleagues who suggested that training to failure brings about fewer improvements in muscular strength compared with not training to failure. Additional support for this argument was offered by Peterson and colleagues who clearly showed in their meta-analysis that training to failure does not maximize strength gains. Thus, it appears that loading is best determined as a percentage of 1RM.
summary: While repetition maximum zones and training to failure are sometimes used, research suggests that loading as a percentage of 1RM is more effective for maximizing strength gains.

content: Examining different percentages of 1RM can help in the creation of a loading structure. In this structure, loading intensity zones can be quantified in relationship to their emphasis on muscle endurance, power generation, and maximum strength. Maximum strength is most likely emphasized with loads 70% of 1RM or greater, whereas muscle endurance is emphasized with loads between 20% and 50% of 1RM. Muscular power appears to be maximized somewhere between 30% and 80% of 1RM depending on the exercise.
summary: Different percentages of 1RM can be used to structure training, targeting muscle endurance (20-50% 1RM), power (30-80% 1RM), and maximum strength (70% 1RM or greater).

content: Intensities between 100% and 125% of 1RM are classified as supermaximum loads; athletes can use these loads when using eccentric overload or forced repetition techniques. Although eccentric overload or forced repetition training appears to have a place in strength training, it may not result in significant increases in muscular strength compared with regular training. Supermaximum loads should only be attempted by highly strength trained athletes and should be used infrequently. Training loads that range from 90% to 100% of 1RM are classified as maximum loads. Heavy loads range in intensity from 80% and 90% of 1RM. Loads of 70% to 80% of 1RM can be classified as medium-heavy intensities. Loads of 50% to 70% are classified as medium intensity. Loads from 20% to 50% are classified as low intensity.
summary: Supermaximum loads (100-125% 1RM) and maximum loads (90-100% 1RM) are advanced techniques, while medium-heavy (70-80% 1RM), medium (50-70% 1RM), and low (20-50% 1RM) intensities are more common.

content: The vast majority of the loading should come from medium and medium-heavy intensities (50% to 80% of 1RM). Support for this can be seen in the training loads undertaken by weightlifters, where the following intensity breakdown can be seen: low = 8%, medium = 59%, heavy = 26%, and maximum = 7%. The load encountered in training will vary depending on the training phase and the loading structure used. The number of repetitions that can be performed is usually a function of the load used. The higher the load, the lower the number of repetitions that can be performed. It is difficult to make definitive connections between a percentage of 1RM and the number of repetitions that can be performed because it appears that training status, muscle mass engaged, gender, and type of exercise can change the number of repetitions that can be performed at a given load.
summary: Training should primarily focus on medium and medium-heavy intensities (50-80% 1RM), with the number of repetitions decreasing as load increases and influenced by factors like training status and exercise type.

content: The load encountered in training will vary depending on the training phase and the loading structure used. It is difficult to make definitive connections between a percentage of 1RM and the number of repetitions that can be performed because it appears that training status, muscle mass engaged, gender, and type of exercise can change the number of repetitions that can be performed at a given load.
summary: Training load varies based on phase and structure, and rep ranges are influenced by factors beyond 1RM percentage.

content: At 60% of 1RM, untrained individuals can perform 35.9 ± 13.4 repetitions in the back squat, 21.6 ± 4.2 repetitions in the bench press, and 17.2 ± 3.7 repetitions in the arm curl. Trained individuals were reported to have performed 29.9 ± 7.4 repetitions in the back squat, 21.7 ± 3.8 repetitions in the bench press, and 19.0 ± 2.9 repetitions in the arm curl at training loads that corresponded to 60% of 1RM. Exercise type, training status, and gender resulted in differences in the number of repetitions that could be performed at various percentages of 1RM.
summary: Untrained and trained individuals exhibit different rep ranges at 60% 1RM, influenced by exercise, training status, and gender.

content: The higher the load, the lower the number of repetitions that can be performed. The number of repetitions that can be performed is usually a function of the load used.
summary: Load and rep range are inversely related; heavier loads allow for fewer repetitions.

content: Low-repetition schemes (1-6 repetitions) are better for the development of maximum muscular strength. Higher numbers of repetitions (>15 repetitions) appear to be more suited for stimulating muscle endurance. High-intensity endurance (short duration) appears to be enhanced with repetition schemes of 15 to 30. Low-intensity endurance (long duration) is improved with more than 50 repetitions.
summary: Rep schemes influence training adaptations: low reps for strength, high reps for endurance.

content: Power-based adaptations are best realized with low-repetition schemes (1-10 repetitions) depending on the phase of training. The repetition scheme should be selected based on the goal of the phase of training and the loading scheme being used. In a periodized training plan, the repetition scheme is manipulated to facilitate specific adaptations.
summary: Power development benefits from low reps, and rep schemes are tailored to training goals and phases.

content: Buffer is the difference between the percentage of 1RM necessary to go to failure with the number of repetitions performed in a set, and the percentage of 1RM actually used for that number of repetitions. Performing a set of 3 repetitions with 80% of 1RM translates into a buffer of 10%, as 3 repetitions to failure necessitate a percentage of 90% of 1RM.
summary: Buffer describes the difference between training load used and the load needed to reach failure.

content: A high buffer allows the athlete to perform more technically correct repetitions (because the load is less challenging) and more explosive concentrics; it also elicits a lower residual fatigue. Sets with a high buffer are used especially for intermuscular coordination work (technique), for power development, and during unloading microcycles.
summary: High buffer training enhances technique, power, and recovery.

content: Throughout a macrocycle, the coach can decrease the buffer by increasing intensity, while maintaining the same number of repetitions. Doing so makes each workout progressively harder, while maintaining the same number of sets and repetitions (a preferred method for maximum strength development). The coach can also keep the buffer the same while increasing or decreasing one of the other parameters. Usually, the coach would not increase the buffer throughout a macrocycle.
summary: Buffer manipulation throughout training cycles progressively increases intensity for strength development.

content: During unloading or peaking microcycles, the buffer is increased via a reduction of repetitions per set, while maintaining or only slightly reducing intensity. Buffer 0 means going to concentric failure, a preferred modality for hypertrophy (bodybuilding) training. Doing multiple sets of 1 to 3 repetitions to failure or close to failure (5% buffer) elicits gains in relative strength that represents an increase of strength without an accompanying increase of body weight.
summary: Buffer adjustments during unloading and peaking phases prioritize recovery and hypertrophy.

content: Going to failure or getting close to failure with a slightly higher time under tension per set and performing 3 to 6 repetitions will elicit gains in absolute strength that are gains in both strength and muscle size. Performing sets of 1 to 3 reps with a buffer of 10% to 20% will increase both maximum strength and power with high loads (e.g., strength-speed). Performing 3 to 6 reps with a buffer of 25% to 40% will increase both maximum strength, via an improvement of the intermuscular coordination, and power with low loads.
summary: Different buffer ranges and rep schemes optimize for strength, power, and hypertrophy.

content: Power is the main ingredient for all sports that require a high rate of force, speed, and agility. Sports that are speed and power dominant include sprinting, jumping, and throwing events in track and field; team sports; racket sports; gymnastics; diving; and martial arts. If an athlete desires to increase his level of performance, he has to improve power. Power is the main ingredient necessary to produce a fast, quick, and agile athlete.
summary: Power is essential for athletic performance in various sports, and improving it enhances speed, agility, and overall performance.

content: Any increase of power must be the result of improvements in either strength or speed, or a combination of the two. An athlete can be very strong, yet unable to display power because of an inability to contract already strong muscles in a very short time. To overcome this deficiency, the athlete must undergo power training to improve her rate of force development.
summary: Power development requires improvements in both strength and speed, addressing the ability to rapidly generate force.

content: Support for this can be seen in the training loads undertaken by weightlifters, where the following intensity breakdown can be seen: low = 8%, medium = 59%, heavy = 26%, and maximum = 7%.
summary: Weightlifter training loads typically emphasize medium intensity, with lower and higher intensities used less frequently.

content: If an athlete desires to increase his level of performance, he has to improve power. Power is the main ingredient necessary to produce a fast, quick, and agile athlete. Any increase of power must be the result of improvements in either strength or speed, or a combination of the two. An athlete can be very strong, yet unable to display power because of an inability to contract already strong muscles in a very short time. To overcome this deficiency, the athlete must undergo power training to improve her rate of force development.
summary: Power is essential for athletic performance, requiring improvements in strength and/or speed. Athletes may need power training to enhance their rate of force development.

content: In the same way, in a sport that requires power endurance or muscle endurance, performance correlates with the ability to maintain the highest power output for the duration of the event. Today it is possible to conveniently measure the power output of each repetition of a strength training session, thanks to affordable monitoring devices that utilize the technology of an accelerometer with a gyroscope. Such devices measure the peak and mean velocity as well as the peak and mean power output of each repetition of a set. This has been a great advancement in the monitoring of strength training.
summary: Power endurance is crucial for sustained performance. Modern monitoring devices using accelerometers and gyroscopes allow precise measurement of power output during training.

content: Table 10.6 provides the mean velocity suggested for strength lifts (squat, bench press, deadlift, chin-ups), according to the desired training effect. For Olympic lifts, please refer to table 10.7. The accelerometer can be used as a power training monitoring device, allowing the athlete to cut repetitions within sets, and sets of an exercise within a session, whenever a marked drop-off in power output becomes evident. It can be used as a testing tool, too, both for absolute power (peak velocity for ballistic exercises and Olympic lifts, and mean velocity for strength lifts) as well as power output stability for power endurance and
summary: Mean velocity guidelines for strength lifts are provided in tables 10.6 and 10.7. Accelerometers can monitor power output during training, allowing adjustments and serving as a testing tool for absolute power and power endurance.

content: The accelerometer can be utilized through jump testing before a training session or monitoring the power output during the strength training warm-up sets, and then comparing the data with the best performance of the season.
summary: Accelerometers can be used for pre-training jump tests and warm-up power monitoring, comparing data to peak performance.

content: Traditionally, a set consists of a series of repetitions performed continuously, followed by a rest interval. A single-set training program is insufficient for identifying any visible strength improvements. A multiple-set protocol is necessary to significantly improve adaptation and, as a result, improve strength in both athletes and nonathletes. Peterson and colleagues (144) reported dose–response data that suggested that single-set protocols offer minimal stimulus for strength gains and that between 4 and 8 sets are needed to optimize training-induced strength gains. Rhea and colleagues (151) also reported that a minimum of three sets are needed to maximize strength gains in both trained and untrained individuals.
summary: Multiple sets are essential for strength gains, with 3-8 sets generally recommended for optimal adaptation.

content: The literature indicates that untrained individuals receive the most benefit from three or four sets, whereas trained individuals will gain the most adaptations from four to eight sets. When constructing a training program, coaches must consider the training status of the individual; highly trained athletes are able to tolerate and may benefit from higher numbers of sets. The more sets an athlete can tolerate, the greater the stimulus for adaptation and the greater the strength gains.
summary: Training set recommendations vary based on experience level, with untrained individuals benefiting from 3-4 sets and trained individuals from 4-8 sets.

content: The phase of training will dictate the makeup of the training set. During the preparation phase of training, when greater numbers of repetitions and exercises are performed, fewer sets may be performed. As the competitive phase approaches, the number of exercises will decrease, whereas the number of sets usually increases. During the competitive phase, everything will decrease, including the number of repetitions and number of sets, so that the athlete has recovered and can focus on technical and tactical training.
summary: Training set structure adapts to different training phases, with higher repetitions and fewer sets during preparation, increasing sets as competition nears, and decreasing both during the competitive phase.

content: For multiplanar (i.e., acting on multiple planes of movement) sports, such as team sports, contact sports, and martial arts, a higher number of exercises and a lower number of sets per exercise has to be employed.
summary: Training for multiplanar sports requires a higher volume of exercises with lower sets per exercise.

content: The rest interval between sets is a function of the load being used, the goal of the training plan, the type of strength being developed, and the degree of explosiveness of the training exercise. The rest interval must be long enough to allow for the restoration of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and phosphocreatine (PCr), the clearance of fatigue-inducing substrates, and the restoration of force-generating capacity. After 30 s of rest, 70% of ATP has been restored, although complete ATP resynthesis does not occur until 3 to 5 min into rest. Two minutes of rest is needed to restore approximately 84% of PCr, 4 min of rest is needed to restore approximately 89% of the PCr stores, and 8 min of rest is needed for complete restoration of PCr.
summary: Rest intervals depend on training goals, exercise type, and load. Sufficient rest is crucial for ATP and PCr replenishment and force recovery.

content: During high-volume training, rest intervals of less than 1 min may not be long enough to allow for restoration of substrates and may result in inadequate recovery. Force- and power-generating capacity have been shown to be almost completely restored after 2 to 5 min of recovery between sets. Conversely, when less than 1 min of recovery is allowed between sets, force- and power-generating capacity can be decreased by 12% to 44%. These data indicate that short recovery periods (<1 min) should be avoided in favor of longer recovery periods (2 to 5 min) when the athlete is attempting to maximize force- and power-generating capacity.
summary: Short rest intervals (<1 min) during high-volume training can hinder recovery and reduce force/power output. Longer rest periods (2-5 min) are recommended for maximizing force and power.

content: Short rest intervals may be advantageous when the strength training program is designed to emphasize muscle endurance. Short rest intervals coupled with high volumes of training may lead to physiological adaptations that facilitate endurance performance. Possible physiological adaptations from short rest intervals include increases in capillary density, mitochondrial density, and buffer capacity. Short rest interval programs have been suggested to result in significant hormonal responses that provide a greater stimulus for the improvement of body composition.
summary: Short rest intervals can be beneficial for muscle endurance training, potentially leading to adaptations like increased capillary density and hormonal responses that promote body composition improvements.

content: These data indicate that short recovery periods (<1 min) should be avoided in favor of longer recovery periods (2 to 5 min) when the athlete is attempting to maximize force- and power-generating capacity. Short rest intervals may be advantageous when the strength training program is designed to emphasize muscle endurance. Short rest intervals coupled with high volumes of training may lead to physiological adaptations that facilitate endurance performance. Possible physiological adaptations from short rest intervals include increases in capillary density, mitochondrial density, and buffer capacity. Short rest interval programs have been suggested to result in significant hormonal responses that provide a greater stimulus for the improvement of body composition. Greater levels of growth hormone are released when short rest intervals are included in a strength training program that uses higher repetitions (~10 repetitions) with moderate loads.
summary: Short rest intervals are beneficial for muscle endurance and may lead to physiological adaptations, but longer rest periods are better for maximizing force and power.

content: The work accomplished (i.e., tonnage or volume load) is the primary stimulus for muscular hypertrophy.  Large mass, multijoint exercises should be placed early in the training session because these exercises are fundamental to strength development and need to be trained when the athlete has a minimal amount of fatigue. After completing large mass, multijoint exercises, the athlete can then progress to smaller mass, single-joint exercises. Alternating between upper body and lower body exercises may facilitate recovery.
summary: Training volume and prioritizing large, multi-joint exercises early in the session are key for muscle growth.

content: Performing either an explosive exercise or a heavy load, multijoint, large mass exercise before performing an explosive exercise such as a jump or a sprint may be advantageous for maximizing strength and power development. Postactivation complexes have been shown to significantly increase the rate of force development (RFD), jumping height, sprinting performance, and sprint cycle performance. The postactivation complex appears to be effective only when used with highly trained individuals.
summary: Preceding explosive exercises with heavy lifts or other explosive movements can enhance power output.

content: Back squats 5 × 1 at 90% of 1RM, 18 1/2 min rest, jump squats, power output ↑ with jumps at 30% of 1RM. Back squat 1 × 3 at 90% of 1RM, 4 min rest, 40 m sprint, 0.87% ↓ in sprint time. Back squats 10 × 1 at 90% of 1RM, 5 min rest, 10 s sprint cycle test, 4.8% ↑ in average power output. Back squat 3 × at 70% of 1RM, 4 min rest, 40 m sprint, 2.3% ↓ in sprint time. Back squat 1 × 5RM, 4 min rest, loaded counter-movement jump, 2.8% ↑ in jump height. A heavy-load activity (≥80% of 1RM) such as a back squat for minimal repetitions (one to three repetitions) performed 4 to 5 minutes prior to an explosive activity, such as jumping or sprinting, is recommended.
summary: Examples of postactivation complexes using back squats and their effects on jump height, sprint time, and power output.

content: Training frequency is usually measured as the number of times per week a certain muscle group is trained or how often the athlete trains the whole body. For novice and intermediate weight trainers who are training the whole body during each training session, training 2 or 3 days per week appears to be optimal. University-level American football players who participated in four or five training sessions per week experienced significantly greater gains in muscular strength than those who participated in fewer training sessions (93). Higher frequencies of training are necessary for maximum muscular strength adaptations (8). Breaking the training volume into short sessions, followed by periods of recovery in which dietary nutrients and supplements are supplied, will increase training quality. Häkkinen and Kallinen (72) demonstrated that increasing the frequency of training to two sessions per day, even when the volume was maintained, results in significantly greater increases in muscle hypertrophy and neuromuscular adaptations, compared with a frequency of one training session per day.
summary: Training frequency depends on experience level and goals, with higher frequencies generally leading to greater strength gains.

content: The optimal frequency of training is determined by many factors: training status, the type of strength needed, the phase of the periodized training plan, and the athlete’s goal influence the frequency of strength training. Most athletes undertake strength training to improve their performance in other activities. Therefore, these athletes may perform two to four strength training sessions per week in conjunction with their other training activities. During the preparation phase, the frequency of training might be substantially higher than in the later portion of the competition phase. The number of training sessions often is reduced in order to taper training and dissipate fatigue.
summary: Training frequency should be individualized based on factors like training status, goals, and the training phase.

content: The flat pyramid is the most effective loading pattern to stimulate maximal strength gains. In the flat pyramid, the athlete begins with several warm-up sets and works toward a prescribed load at which all of the training sets are performed. The flat portion of the pyramid generally uses an intensity between 70% and 90% of 1RM when the athlete is specifically targeting maximum strength. Performance is maximized when the majority of training is undertaken with intensities of about 70% to 85% of 1RM (63, 207). Zatsiorsky (207) reported that 35% of the training volume of Soviet weightlifters was between 70% and 80% of 1RM, 26% of the training volume was between 80% and 90% of 1RM, and only 7% of the training volume was between 90% and 100% of 1RM throughout the training year. Häkkinen and colleagues (73) reported that when training volume is increased from 80% to 90% of 1RM and 90% to 100% of 1RM, maximal strength increases significantly. When too much of the training volume is in the 90% to 100% range, performance may not be optimized and overtraining may occur (56).
summary: The flat pyramid loading pattern, using 70-90% of 1RM, is effective for maximizing strength gains.

content: The pyramid loading pattern (20, 169), or an ascending pyramid loading scheme, is a second loading pattern. In this loading scheme, with each set the percentage of 1RM increases and the number of repetitions decreases. A pyramid loading pattern for strength development has been suggested to range from 10% to 15% of 1RM.
summary: The pyramid loading pattern involves increasing weight and decreasing reps per set.

content: Häkkinen and colleagues (73) reported that when training volume is increased from 80% to 90% of 1RM and 90% to 100% of 1RM, maximal strength increases significantly. When too much of the training volume is in the 90% to 100% range, performance may not be optimized and overtraining may occur (56).
summary: Increasing training volume from 80% to 100% of 1RM can increase maximal strength, but excessive volume in the 90-100% range may lead to overtraining.

content: The pyramid loading pattern (20, 169), or an ascending pyramid loading scheme, is a second loading pattern. In this loading scheme, with each set the percentage of 1RM increases and the number of repetitions decreases. A pyramid loading pattern for strength development has been suggested to range from 10% to 15% of 1RM. Load ranges greater than 15% of 1RM are not recommended because the cumulative fatigue may impair strength development.
summary: The pyramid loading pattern involves increasing 1RM percentage and decreasing reps per set, typically ranging from 10-15% of 1RM for strength development.

content: A modification of the ascending pyramid loading pattern is the double pyramid. The traditional loading pattern for the double pyramid is an increase of resistance up to a maximal attempt followed by a progressive decrease. A better approach for the double pyramid is to leave the volume constant, typically at one repetition, and then use an ascending intensity pattern followed by a descending pattern. This pattern elicits a potentiation effect on the descending intensity portion of the pyramid. The number of sets in a double pyramid pattern must be limited to five to six. The top intensity in a double pyramid pattern must not go beyond 90% of the 1RM.
summary: The double pyramid loading pattern involves an ascending then descending intensity pattern, with volume kept constant, typically capped at 5-6 sets and 90% 1RM.

content: Another modification to the pyramid loading pattern is the skewed pyramid. In the skewed pyramid, the intensity is increased for a series of sets, and on the last set a down set is performed. Lowering the load in the last set (e.g., the back-off set or down set) and taking it to failure has been proven to retain muscle hypertrophy.
summary: The skewed pyramid loading pattern involves increasing intensity for most sets, followed by a lighter 'down set' to failure, promoting hypertrophy.

content: The wave-loading model is sometimes referred to as segment work. In the wave-loading model, the training load increases in an undulating or wavelike fashion.
summary: The wave-loading model involves an undulating pattern of increasing training load.

content: In traditional set configurations, with each repetition there is a decrease in repetition velocity, power, and quality. The addition of a 10 to 30 second interrepetition rest interval can allow for partial recovery between each repetition, thus allowing for higher repetition quality.
summary: Traditional sets experience decreasing velocity and quality with each repetition, while cluster sets with 10-30 second rest intervals allow for better quality.

content: Traditional sets may be better for the development of muscular hypertrophy or maximum muscular strength. Contemporary literature suggests that the most effective use of the cluster set is with ballistic or explosive exercise.
summary: Traditional sets are better for hypertrophy and strength, while cluster sets are more effective for ballistic exercises.

content: Four distinct repetition schemes can be constructed for cluster sets: a straight cluster set, an ascending cluster, an undulating cluster, and a waving cluster.
summary: Cluster sets can be structured in four ways: straight, ascending, undulating, and waving.

content: Continual monitoring of the training process is an often overlooked but essential part of implementing a periodized strength training plan. Monitoring the athlete’s progress allows the coach to determine whether the goals of the training plan are being achieved.
summary: Continual monitoring of training progress is essential for ensuring the training plan's goals are met.

content: A strength training program has preparatory, competitive, and transition phases of training. Each phase of training is essential to maximize training outcomes. The concept of phase potentiation suggests that activities of the general preparatory phase facilitate the athlete’s development in both the specific preparatory phase and the competition phase.
summary: Strength training programs have preparatory, competitive, and transition phases, with each phase contributing to overall training outcomes.

content: The most important step in establishing a periodized strength training program is to set separate training objectives for the annual plan as well as for each training phase. This refers not only to performance objectives, but also to the goals for each dominant physical abilities. Performance objectives may be impeded if the goals of the dominant abilities are not achieved.
summary: Periodized strength training programs require setting separate objectives for the annual plan and each phase, including both performance and dominant physical abilities.

content: During the last phase of the annual training program (transition phase), the coach has to analyze whether the objectives for the year have been achieved and, based on this analysis, set realistic objectives for the next annual plan. This is important both for performance, but also for the objectives for each dominant ability. The coach must also analyze the physiological demands needed to achieve superior performance and what the athlete needs in order to achieve it.
summary: The transition phase involves analyzing yearly objectives and setting new ones, considering both performance and dominant abilities, while analyzing physiological demands for future success.

content: The weakest link of a chain always breaks first. The goals for strength training must be integrated with all of the other components important to the selected sport, including technical, tactical, and physical components. Therefore, the coach must consider the strength training program and how it may affect other attributes, such as conditioning agility and technical and tactical training activities (i.e., all of the components that contribute to performance).
summary: Strength training goals must align with all aspects of the sport, considering technical, tactical, and physical components, and how strength training may impact other training areas.

content: The training exercises selected must be related to the requirements of the sport. When examining a sport during the needs analysis, the coach can determine the muscles that act as prime movers and match exercises to these activities.
summary: Strength training exercises should be chosen based on the sport's requirements, matching exercises to the prime movers involved.

content: The weakest link of a chain always breaks first. The goals for strength training must be integrated with all of the other components important to the selected sport, including technical, tactical, and physical components. Therefore, the coach must consider the strength training program and how it may affect other attributes, such as conditioning agility and technical and tactical training activities (i.e., all of the components that contribute to performance).
summary: Strength training goals should align with the overall sport demands, considering technical, tactical, and physical aspects.

content: When examining a sport during the needs analysis, the coach can determine the muscles that act as prime movers and match exercises to these activities. For example, analyzing a 100 m sprinter’s technique may shows that lower body strength is impeding optimal performance outcomes. Thus, the coach may select exercises such as back squats and kettlebell swings in an attempt to target the prime movers employed during the propulsion phase in sprinting. Support for this idea can be seen in several studies, which suggest that maximum strength capacity in the squat and power clean is significantly related to running performance.
summary: Strength training exercises should be tailored to the specific muscle groups and movements required by the sport.

content: An additional consideration when selecting exercises is the phase of training. Certain exercises are best used at specific times during the periodized plan. Early in the general preparation phase of training it may be warranted to use general strength exercises. As the athlete moves toward the specific preparation and competitive phases of training, more specific exercises can be used. Kettlebell swings work the stretch short-ening cycle in a more specific way.
summary: Exercise selection should be adjusted based on the training phase, progressing from general to sport-specific exercises.

content: Knowledge of an athlete’s 1RM capabilities in at least the primary or dominant exercises will allow the coach to establish the training loads. The 1RM will continually change as the athlete adapts to the physiological stressors of the training program. The athlete must be tested at the end of each macrocycle to individualize the loading parameters.
summary: Training loads should be individualized based on an athlete's 1RM, which should be periodically reassessed.

content: Many coaches wrongly believe that performing a 1RM test is dangerous and will increase the athlete’s risk of injury. 1RM testing is very safe for most populations and is considered the gold standard in the assessment of muscular strength. Some authors suggest that testing strength with multiple repetition tests is a better way to establish the training intensity. It has been consistently shown that many of the prediction equations misrepresent the 1RM. Using an estimate of 1RM capabilities that is too high may increase injury risk during training or induce high-intensity overtraining from consistently training at high intensities. Training with an intensity that is underpredicted will result in inadequate strength development because the athlete will train using suboptimal loads.
summary: 1RM testing is a safe and effective method for assessing muscular strength, and training loads should be based on accurate 1RM estimations.

content: Under normal conditions an exercise has to be selected based on the needs to target the prime movers (the muscles that contract to perform the technical skills). The muscle contraction has to be performed along the line of pull (median direction of contraction of the main muscles involved in the technical skill). The exercise has to be very specific to the needs of the selected sport.
summary: Exercise selection should prioritize targeting the prime movers involved in the specific sport's technical skills.

content: The OL athletes are strong not because of the exercises they do, but rather because of the heavy loads they use (especially in the back squat). The OL exercises have far fewer applications than popularly thought because they are not force-vector specific and do not properly target the prime movers employed in many sports. Coaches who work in sports that require fast force applied vertically are better off using low or high pulls. Exercises such as plyometrics and kettlebell swings work the stretch shortening cycle in a more specific way and at a greater extent than OL, making them preferable for the sprinting athlete (in track and field and team sports). Do not allow the athletes to be robots and do just OL moves—it might do a disservice to them.
summary: Olympic lifts, while effective for building strength, may not be the most appropriate exercises for all sports.  Coaches should prioritize exercises that mimic the specific movements and muscle activations required by the athlete's sport.

content: Most sport activities require a fast application of force, in less than 300 ms, once an athlete reaches triple extension (ankle, knee, hip), it does not matter what he does with his arms, if he catches in a clean or snatch position.
summary: The primary focus for athletes in most sports should be on developing fast force production, regardless of the specific catching position.

content: After establishing the athlete’s physical performance capabilities, the coach can establish the number of exercises, sets, repetitions, and loading (percentage of 1RM) that will be used during the microcycles within the macrocycle. Throughout the training plan the coach will need to alter volume, intensity, and the exercises selected to allow for continued physiological adaptations that will lead to the maximization of specific strength. As muscular strength increases across the annual plan, the coach will need to periodically retest the 1RM to optimize the training load. It is essential for the coach to test the 1RM before each new macrocycle. A detailed training program will be written down only for one macrocycle at a time. The coach must record the exercises, number of sets, number of repetitions, and training load (table 10.16).
summary: Strength training programs should be individualized and periodised, with adjustments made based on athlete progress and ongoing assessment.

content: The main objective of strength training is not to achieve an immediate overload, but to elicit a progressive adaptation of the athlete’s anatomy. The anatomical adaptation phase lays the foundation for the other phases of training. The main physiological objectives of the anatomical adaptation phase are to (1) strengthen the tendons, ligaments, and joints, which is doable through a higher volume of training than in the remainder of the year; and (2) increase bone mineral content and the proliferation of the connective tissue. Regardless of the sport, this phase improves cardiovascular fitness, adequately challenges muscular strength, and tests and assists the athlete with practicing and improving neuromuscular coordination for strength movement patterns. Although this phase does not focus on increasing the cross-sectional area of muscle, some gains in the hypertrophy of some muscles may be visible, especially for novice athletes.
summary: The anatomical adaptation phase prioritizes strengthening tendons, ligaments, joints, and increasing bone density, laying the groundwork for subsequent training phases.

content: Periodization of strength offers a seven-phase approach that follows the physiological rhythm of the neuromuscular system’s response to strength training. The seven phases of periodization of strength are anatomical adaptation, hypertrophy, maximum strength, conversion, maintenance, cessation, and compensation. Depending on the physiological demands of the sport, the periodization of strength involves combining, in sequence, at least four of the phases: anatomical adaptation, maximum strength, conversion to specific strength (power or muscle endurance), and maintenance. All models for periodization of strength begin with an anatomical adaptation phase.
summary: Periodization of strength utilizes a seven-phase model, with coaches typically combining four phases tailored to the athlete's sport.

content: Tendons are strengthened by implementing a time under tension per set that falls between 30 and 70 s. The hydrogen ions released by lactic acid have been proven to stimulate the release of growth hormone and therefore collagen synthesis, which is also stimulated by eccentric load. For this reason, the majority of the time under tension is spent in the eccentric phase of the exercise (3 to 5 s per repetition).
summary: Eccentric loading and time under tension are crucial for tendon strengthening, stimulating collagen synthesis and growth hormone release.

content: Hypertrophy, or the enlargement of muscle size, is one of the most visible signs of adaptation to strength training. The two main physiological objectives of the hypertrophy phase are (1) to increase the muscle cross-sectional area by increasing muscle protein content and (2) to increase storage capacity for high-energy substrates and enzymes. Many principles used in hypertrophy training are similar to those used in bodybuilding, but there are also some differences.
summary: The hypertrophy phase focuses on increasing muscle size and storage capacity for energy substrates, employing principles similar to bodybuilding.

content: Throughout the training plan the coach will need to alter volume, intensity, and the exercises selected to allow for continued physiological adaptations that will lead to the maximization of specific strength. As muscular strength increases across the annual plan, the coach will need to periodically retest the 1RM to optimize the training load. It is essential for the coach to test the 1RM before each new macrocycle. A detailed training program will be written down only for one macrocycle at a time.
summary: Coaches should adjust training volume, intensity, and exercises throughout the year, periodically retesting 1RM to optimize training load and create detailed macrocycle plans.

content: The load, number of repetitions, and sets usually are noted as follows: % of 1RM / number of repetitions 3 sets. Noting the load as a percentage of 1RM is useful when working with many athletes because this allows the coach to calculate the load for every athlete. By using a percentage notation, the coach can individualize the program for each athlete, and athletes can use their own 1RM to establish their training loads.
summary: Training loads are often expressed as a percentage of 1RM, allowing for individualization and easy calculation across multiple athletes.

content: One of the most important steps is to record what was done in the training sessions. If the coach and athlete keep detailed records, they will be able to evaluate the athlete’s progress and chart her performance. Things to record in the training log include the exercise, the number of repetitions completed, the number of sets performed, the load lifted in lb or kg, and the duration of the training session (figure 10.11). This record will allow the coach to calculate the volume load, tons, and training intensity (volume load divided by total repetitions performed).
summary: Detailed training logs are essential for tracking progress, recording exercises, repetitions, sets, load, and duration, allowing coaches to calculate volume and intensity.

content: By using a spreadsheet software program such as Microsoft Excel, the coach can easily calculate the volume load, tons, and training intensity and create figures depicting the volume and intensity of training. Those strength and conditioning coaches who have an accelerometer can record the peak velocity, average velocity, peak power, and average power of the best repetition of each set (figure 10.12). Average velocity during the first sets of a session can be utilized as an indicator of daily internal load, and the training session load or direction can be adjusted accordingly.
summary: Coaches can leverage spreadsheet software and accelerometers to analyze training volume, intensity, velocity, and power, adjusting sessions based on internal load.

content: Testing maximum strength should not constitute a safety concern. Testing for 1RM must follow a thorough, progressive warm-up. A projected 1RM at 150 kg warm-up includes eight sets with increasing weight and rest intervals. If the training log is accurate and inclusive, it provides an excellent tool for monitoring training.
summary: 1RM testing should be conducted safely with a thorough warm-up, and accurate training logs are crucial for monitoring progress.

content: Some coaches believe that testing for 1RM is dangerous and that lifting 100% can result in injury. It is not dangerous for trained athletes to lift 100% once every 3 or 4 weeks. Most injuries occur during training and competition, not during testing. Sometimes the body of an athlete is subjected to forces up to five times body weight during the sport activity.
summary: While some coaches perceive 1RM testing as risky, it can be safe for trained athletes when conducted appropriately, and injuries are more common during training and competition.

content: Hypertrophy, or the enlargement of muscle size, is one of the most visible signs of adaptation to strength training. The two main physiological objectives of the hypertrophy phase are (1) to increase the muscle cross-sectional area by increasing muscle protein content and (2) to increase storage capacity for high-energy substrates and enzymes. Many principles used in hypertrophy training are similar to those used in bodybuilding, but there are also some differences. In comparison with bodybuilding programs, athletic hypertrophy programs use a lower average number of repetitions per set, a higher average load, and a longer average rest interval between sets.  Although hypertrophic changes occur in both fast-twitch and slow-twitch muscle fibers, more changes take place in the fast-twitch fibers. This form of hypertrophy is desired for athletes who use strength training to improve their athletic performance. Hypertrophy training is indicated mostly for athletes who desire an overall increase in body weight (e.g., throwers in track and field) or for tall athletes, specifically those who play in scrums in rugby and American football or who fall in the heavyweight category in combat sports or martial arts. For most other athletes, this strength training phase is not necessary unless the coach is training beginners.
summary: This chunk describes hypertrophy training, its physiological benefits, and its application in athletic development.

content: Muscular balance is achieved both by using an equal training volume between agonist and antagonist muscles around a joint and by making greater use of unilateral exercises, rather than bilateral ones.
summary: This chunk explains the concept of muscular balance and how to achieve it through training.

content: In most sports, the development of maximum strength is probably the single most important variable. Maximum strength depends on the diameter of the cross-sectional area of the muscles, the capacity to recruit fast-twitch muscle fibers, the frequency of activation, and the ability to simultaneously call into action all the primary muscles involved in a given movement. The popularity of maximum strength training is rooted in the positive increase in relative strength. Many sports (e.g., volleyball, gymnastics, combat sports) require greater force generation without a concomitant increase in body weight. An increase in maximum strength without an associated increase in body weight characterizes the maximum strength phase as central nervous system training. An athlete could benefit from traditional maximum strength training methods, such as performing high loads with maximal rest (3 to 5 min) between sets.
summary: This chunk focuses on the importance of maximum strength in sports and the training methods used to develop it.

content: Strength and Power Development is a function of training with moderate weights lifted explosively, as well as heavy loads (up to 90% of 1RM or more). Adaptive responses can also be elicited by eccentric training with loads greater than 100% of 1RM, although its practical application is limited to very few situations. To increase the weight lifted in an exercise over the long term, the key is intermuscular coordination training (technique training). With time, as the nervous system learns the gesture, fewer motor units are activated by the same weight, thereby leaving more motor units available for activation by higher weights. The concentric action should be explosive in order to activate the fast-twitch muscle fibers (responsible for the highest and fastest force generation) and to achieve the highest specific hypertrophy. The most commonly employed methods entail the use of moderately heavy (MxS-I) and heavy (MxS-II) loads, applied in this sequence (see figure 10.13). Improving intermuscular coordination (i.e., coordination of muscle groups) depends strictly on learning (technique), which requires many sets of repetitions of the same exercise using a moderate load (40% to 80% of 1RM) and performed explosively with perfect technique (MxS-I). Intramuscular coordination, or the capacity to recruit fast-twitch fibers, depends on training content in which high loads (80% to 90% of 1RM) are moved explosively (MxS-II). Both of these types of strength training, MxS-I and MxS-II, activate the powerful fast-twitch motor units.
summary: This chunk details the training methods and physiological adaptations involved in strength and power development.

content: Once the mind–muscle connection is made, the physical requirements of the sport determine the next phase. Depending on the sport, a maximum strength phase of training can be followed by one of three fundamental options: conversion to power, power endurance, or muscle endurance. Conversion to power or power endurance is accomplished by using relatively moderate to heavy loads (40% to 80% of 1RM) with the intention of moving the weight as quickly as possible, the difference being the duration of the sets. By engaging the nervous system, methods such as ballistic training and upper or lower body plyometric training improve an athlete’s high-velocity strength or ability to recruit and engage the high-powered, fast-twitch motor units. A strong foundation of maximum strength is a must for maximizing the rate of force production. Even maximum strength training with high loads moved at low velocity Periodization has been shown to transfer to gains in power if the athlete attempts to move the weight as quickly as possible.
summary: This chunk outlines the progression of training phases after a maximum strength foundation, focusing on power, power endurance, and muscle endurance.

content: Depending on the demands of the sport, muscle endurance can be trained for short, medium, or long duration. Short muscle endurance as the main energy system is the anaerobic lactic, whereas medium and long muscle endurance are predominantly aerobic. Conversion to muscle endurance requires more than performing 15 to 20 repetitions per set; it can require as many as 400 repetitions per set, implemented concomitantly with metabolic training. Metabolic training and muscle endurance training pursue similar physiological training objectives.
summary: This chunk describes the different types of muscle endurance training and their physiological adaptations.

content: For this reason, the majority of the time under tension is spent in the eccentric phase of the exercise (3 to 5 s per repetition).  In addition, athletes should always try to move the weight as fast as possible during the concentric phase of the lift.
summary: This chunk focuses on the importance of eccentric training and the speed of concentric contractions.

content: Periodization has been shown to transfer to gains in power if the athlete attempts to move the weight as quickly as possible.  As the main competition of the year approaches, most of the athlete’s energy must be directed to the main sport-specific biomotor ability or mix of biomotor abilities. The purpose of the cessation phase is to conserve the athlete’s energy for competition and peak his or sport-specific biomotor abilities. The strength training program should end at least 3 to 14 days before the main competition. Female athletes, who retain strength gains less easily than males do, should usually maintain strength training until 3 days before competition. A longer cessation phase, 1 to 2 weeks long, may result in improved alactic speed performance due to overshooting of the fast-twitch type IIx muscle fibers. For long endurance sports for which strength is not as important as for anaerobic sports, strength training can be ended 2 weeks before the main competition. Heavier athletes tend to retain both adaptations and residual fatigue longer and therefore should end strength training earlier than lighter athletes.
summary: This chunk outlines the periodization of strength training, particularly focusing on the cessation phase leading up to competition.

content: Stimulating the body for optimal performance takes time, planning, and persistence. Physiology is helpful in planning the program, but performance improvement is achieved through practical application of the many principles and methods of training inherent in the periodization of strength.  Once the neuromuscular system has been adapted for maximum performance, it is time to put the gains to the test. Maintaining the strong and stable base formed during precompetitive phases requires the athlete to continue training during the competitive season. Failure to plan at least one weekly session dedicated to strength training results in decreased performance or early onset of fatigue as the season wears on.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of consistent training, planning, and adaptation throughout the competitive season.

content: The last phase of the annual plan is called the transition phase. The main goal of the transition phase is to remove fatigue acquired during the training year and replenish the exhausted energy stores by decreasing both volume (through a decrease in frequency) and intensity of training. During the months of training and competition, most athletes are exposed to numerous psychological and social stressors that drain their mental energy. During the transition phase, athletes can relax psychologically by getting involved in various physical and social activities they enjoy. The transition phase should last no longer than 4 weeks for serious athletes. A longer transition phase results in detraining effects, such as the loss of most training gains, especially strength gains. The detraining that results from neglecting strength training in the off-season can be detrimental to athletes’ rate of performance improvement in the following year. Athletes and coaches should remember that strength is hard to gain and easy to lose. Athletes who perform no strength training at all during the transition phase may experience decreased muscle size and considerable power loss. Because power and speed are interdependent, athletes also lose speed when they do not perform strength training during the transition phase.
summary: This chunk describes the transition phase, emphasizing its importance for recovery and preventing detraining.

content: Strength is one of the most important biomotor abilities for most sports. Strength is the foundation for both generating maximum power and maintaining repetitive muscular contractions (i.e., muscle endurance).  To maximize performance in muscle endurance sports, maximum strength training must be followed by a combination of specific metabolic training and specific strength training to prepare the body for the demands of the sport.  Training for conversion to muscle endurance requires heightened adaptation of the aerobic and the anaerobic lactic systems.  Conversion to muscle endurance requires more than performing 15 to 20 repetitions per set; it can require as many as 400 repetitions per set, implemented concomitantly with metabolic training. Metabolic training and muscle endurance training pursue similar physiological training objectives.
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of strength as a foundation for both power and muscle endurance, and outlines the training principles for developing muscle endurance.

content: The body replenishes energy for muscular contractions through the combined efforts of three energy systems: anaerobic alactic, anaerobic lactic, and aerobic. Depending on the demands of the sport, muscle endurance can be trained for short, medium, or long duration. Short muscle endurance as the main energy system is the anaerobic lactic, whereas medium and long muscle endurance are predominantly aerobic. Adapting the neuromuscular and cardiovascular systems physiologically, biochemically, and metabolically provides invaluable benefit to athletes in many endurance sports. The main objectives of aerobic training include improvement in physiological parameters, such as heart efficiency; biochemical parameters, such as increased mitochondria and capillary density, which result in greater diffusion and use of oxygen; and metabolic parameters, which result in greater use of fat as energy and an increased rate of removal and reuse of lactic acid.
summary: This chunk explains the three energy systems involved in muscular contractions and the different training approaches for muscle endurance based on duration.

content: Strength is one of the most important biomotor abilities for most sports. Strength is the foundation for both generating maximum power and maintaining repetitive muscular contractions (i.e., muscle endurance). A periodized strength training program can be an excellent tool for maximizing performance outcomes. The physiological adaptations to the neuromuscular system that strength training can bring about are very specific to the training program that the athlete uses. Athletic performance can be maximized only if the training program provides appropriate variation. Many methods for inducing training variation are available, and many of them can result in very specific physiological adaptations. To truly maximize the effectiveness of a strength training program, it must be integrated into the comprehensive periodized training plan developed for the athlete. Simply adding strength training to an athlete’s training plan without considering the other training activities will not maximize performance outcomes.
summary: Strength training is crucial for athletic performance, serving as a foundation for power and endurance. Periodized programs with appropriate variation are essential for maximizing strength adaptations and integrating them effectively into the overall training plan.

content: During the transition phase, although physical activity volume is reduced by 50% to 60%, athletes should find time to work on the maintenance of strength training. It can be beneficial to work on the antagonists, stabilizers, and other muscles that may not necessarily be involved in the performance of the sport-specific skills during the transition phase. Compensation exercises should be planned for sports in which an imbalance may develop between parts or sides of the body (e.g., pitching, throwing events, archery, soccer, cycling) during the transition phase.
summary: During the transition phase, athletes should maintain strength training and focus on antagonists, stabilizers, and compensation exercises to address potential imbalances.

content: Endurance can be classified in several ways. Aerobic endurance, sometimes called low-intensity exercise endurance, allows a person to perform activities continuously for a long duration. Anaerobic endurance, or high-intensity exercise endurance, provides the ability to repetitively perform bouts of high-intensity exercise. Most sports rely on some form of endurance. The type of endurance developed (high or low intensity) can significantly affect performance outcomes. The coach and athlete must consider the type of endurance that the athlete needs for the sport and how the appropriate endurance will be targeted within the training plan. The coach and athlete must also consider the athlete’s physiological responses to the methods for developing endurance. Once the type of endurance and the physiological responses are understood, the coach can develop a training plan to enhance sport-specific endurance.
summary: Endurance encompasses both aerobic and anaerobic capabilities, and the specific type needed for an athlete's sport significantly influences training.
