In the later stages of the preparatory phase and the early part of the competition phase of the annual training plan, the athlete will begin to incorporate elements from the competitive trial method of developing HIEE, which has also been termed special endurance.
summary: Interval training intensity and volume should progress throughout the training year, starting with lower intensities and increasing as the athlete approaches competition.

content: As the competitive phase of the season progresses, the athlete uses specific strategies, including various interval training and competitive trial methods, to maintain HIEE capacity across the season.
summary: Throughout the competitive season, athletes should maintain their high-intensity endurance capacity through targeted training strategies.

content: The interval training plan should include both extensive and intensive methods. Extensive interval training involves lower intensities (60%-80% of competitive speed) and longer durations (20-100 s or 100-400 m) with more repetitions (5-12 repetitions). Intensive interval training involves higher intensities (80%-90% of competitive speed) and shorter durations (14-95 s or 100-400 m) with fewer repetitions (4-8 repetitions).
summary: Interval training plans should incorporate both extensive and intensive methods, which differ in intensity, duration, and repetitions.

content: The target recovery heart rate for both extensive and intensive interval training is 110-120 beats/min for novice athletes and 125-130 beats/min for advanced athletes. The recovery duration for extensive interval training is 60-120 s for novice athletes and 45-90 s for advanced athletes. The recovery duration for intensive interval training is 120-240 s for novice athletes and 90-180 s for advanced athletes.
summary: Recovery heart rate and duration should be adjusted based on the athlete's experience level and the type of interval training performed.

content: The competitive trial methods of developing HIEE include supramaximal training, maximal training, and submaximal training. Supramaximal training involves competition-intensity intervals (100-200 m for a 400 m sprinter). Maximal training involves competition-intensity intervals (200-400 m for a 400 m sprinter). Submaximal training involves intervals longer than competition (400-600 m for a 400 m sprinter).
summary: Competitive trial methods, such as supramaximal, maximal, and submaximal training, can be used to develop high-intensity endurance capacity.

content: Over the course of the competitive phase, the athlete can fall into a state of deconditioning if specific attention is not paid to integrating conditioning activities into his competitive training schedule. When the volume of anaerobic interval training (e.g., sprint and agility training) is significantly reduced or when this training is eliminated altogether during the competitive season, there is a concomitant reduction in markers of fitness and muscle mass.
summary: Maintaining conditioning activities, including anaerobic interval training, is crucial during the competitive season to prevent deconditioning and preserve fitness.

content: Specific HIEE conditioning strategies must be implemented in the in-season training plan. If acute overtraining is induced during the 6 to 8 weeks before the initiation of the season, a general catabolic state will predominate during the season and performance capacity will decline. Even if the preseason training plan is implemented appropriately, performance can decline during the season if the HIEE is not appropriately incorporated into the in-season training plan.
summary: In-season training plans should include specific high-intensity endurance conditioning strategies to maintain performance and prevent overtraining.

content: The best approach is to include a combination of sport-specific practices, strength training, and HIEE-based conditioning. When the frequency of competition is more than once a week, though, those players that are going to play three games within 7 days would be better off training in LIEE modality, to avoid overtraining and foster recovery.
summary: Optimal training plans combine sport-specific practices, strength training, and high-intensity endurance conditioning, while adjusting for competition frequency to prevent overtraining.

content: Specific HIEE conditioning strategies must be implemented in the in-season training plan. Even if the preseason training plan is implemented appropriately, performance can decline during the season if the HIEE is not appropriately incorporated into the in-season training plan. The best approach is to include a combination of sport-specific practices, strength training, and HIEE-based conditioning.
summary: In-season training requires specific HIEE strategies to maintain performance, alongside sport-specific practices and strength training.

content: When the frequency of competition is more than once a week, though, those players that are going to play three games within 7 days would be better off training in LIEE modality, to avoid overtraining and foster recovery.
summary: Players with frequent competition (3+ games in a week) benefit from LIEE training to prevent overtraining and promote recovery.

content: HIEE can be trained in conjunction with sport-specific skills to maximize training time. Using a three-sided training game on a large pitch can increase the exercise intensity to a level similar to that seen in actual game play. The training stimulus can be modified by manipulating the field dimensions, number of players, duration of plays, and skills used during a small-sided soccer game.
summary: HIEE training can be integrated with sport-specific skills, using modified small-sided games to simulate match intensity.

content: Although skill-based conditioning drills are important, it is equally important to include strength training in conjunction with HIEE and sprint agility training to avoid the  overtraining would only compound the typical degradation in performance seen across the season. Different training factors (e.g., HIEE, drills, and strength training) should be integrated to avoid overtraining because
summary: A balanced training plan incorporating HIEE, strength training, and skill drills is crucial to avoid overtraining and performance decline throughout the season.

content: The coach must allow for recovery between training sessions and competitive events. The coach must perform a balancing act by providing an adequate training stimulus to maintain fitness and performance while avoiding overtraining.
summary: Coaches must balance training load with recovery to prevent overtraining while maintaining fitness and performance.

content: In the early 2000s, motion video analysis was used to monitor the performance of players during training and, especially, games. Although it was an advance in sport science, the speed bracket categorization was insufficient in its depiction of a player’s performance during a game, because intermittent sports are characterized by numerous accelerations and decelerations which do not necessarily lead to high speed, yet constitute high-intensity efforts.
summary: Early motion analysis, while valuable, was limited in capturing the full spectrum of high-intensity efforts in intermittent sports.

content: In 2005, Di Prampero and colleagues conducted a research study in which they demonstrated that ‘sprint running on flat terrain, during the acceleration phase is biomechanically equivalent to uphill running at constant speed, the slope being dictated by the forward acceleration and that, conversely, during the deceleration phase, it is biomechanically equivalent to running downhill (40c).’ This allowed the creation of an algorithm to measure the metabolic power of each action during an intermittent activity, typical of team sports (109b).
summary: Research by Di Prampero et al. established the biomechanical equivalence of acceleration/deceleration to uphill/downhill running, leading to the development of algorithms for measuring metabolic power in intermittent sports.

content: Accelerometer devices with GPS and gyroscope became more practical and cheaper than video analysis tools, accessed in order to represent the performance of team sports’ players. The performance could then be categorized in power output brackets: minimum power (0 to 10 W.Kg-1), low power (10 to 20 W.Kg-1), intermediate power (20 to 35 W.Kg-1), high power (35 to 55 W.Kg-1), and maximum power (55 W.Kg-1 and higher).
summary: Advancements in wearable technology allowed for more accurate and accessible performance monitoring in team sports, categorizing player exertion into power output brackets.

content: These devices allow for a better depiction of the physical demands of match play (4b, 56b); they also allow strength and conditioning coaches to quantify the work performed by each player during the ever-increasing utilization of game-based training activities (i.e., small-sided games), as a means of improving the skill and physical fitness levels of team sport athletes (56b, 56c). As of today, scientific literature indicating training standards according to such performance monitoring devices is quite scarce; thus, strength and conditioning coaches using this technology are building their own database and performing data analysis to estimate the workload profile of specific drills and the individual players’ readiness fluctuation.
summary: Wearable technology allows coaches to quantify player workload during game-based training, but standardized training protocols based on this data are still developing.

content: This process is, in fact, paramount when substituting general training means (e.g., runs) with specific drills (e.g., small-sided games) and increasing the load over time. Furthermore, a good technical level of the team is required for the specific drills to reach a high enough intensity, which, in certain cases, is easier to reach with general means.
summary: Transitioning from general training to specific drills requires careful workload progression and a high technical level within the team to achieve desired intensity.

content: Investigators have repeatedly shown that strength training improves HIEE (66, 67, 99, 125, 147) and that gains in HIEE performance are likely related to increases in muscular strength, morphological adaptations, or metabolic adaptations that increase buffering capacity (102, 125). HIEE performance is improved when the training plan includes resistance training with 12 or more repetitions per set (125). This improvement in performance is most noted as the intensity of exercise is increased (66). 294 sets appear to improve HIEE more than do lower-repetition protocols or single-set protocols (51, 99, 146).
summary: Strength training, particularly with higher repetitions and volume, significantly improves HIEE performance by enhancing muscular strength, morphology, and metabolic buffering capacity.

content: HIEE performance seems to be improved to a greater extent with high volumes (volume loads) of resistance training. Several authors have suggested that short rest intervals between sets of resistance training can increase HIEE capacity (51, 146). Hill-Haas and colleagues (67) demonstrated that a resistance training program that includes short rest intervals (20 s) results in significantly greater HIEE performance gains (+12.5%) compared with a resistance training program that uses longer rest intervals (80 s). It is likely that the program that includes short rest intervals increases the lactate-buffering capacity in response to resistance training due to repeated exposure to high lactate levels that allows for a greater HIEE performance.
summary: High-volume resistance training and short rest intervals between sets can enhance HIEE performance by increasing lactate buffering capacity.

content: Robinson and colleagues (125) reported that short rest intervals (30 s) do not result in greater improvements in HIEE performance compared with longer rest intervals (180 s). Their findings suggest that simply increasing the volume of resistance training is more effective at improving HIEE without compromising other training-induced adaptations. Both studies found muted strength gains in response to short rest intervals (67, 125). Because sprinting speed is highly correlated with muscular strength (6, 37), the validity of decreasing the rest interval between sets may be questionable because increasing volume and intensity of training appears to improve HIEE (99, 125). Therefore, the coach and the athlete should be cautious when manipulating rest interval lengths because drastic decreases in interset rest intervals appear to impair the development of strength.
summary: While short rest intervals can improve HIEE, simply increasing training volume may be more effective, and drastic reductions in rest intervals may hinder strength development.

content: Endurance training is still prescribed to develop aerobic endurance. During a game, an American football linebacker performs 40 to 60 short accelerations of 3 to 6 seconds each with rest intervals of 1 to 3 minutes.
summary: Endurance training remains important for aerobic development, and intermittent sports like American football involve repeated short bursts of high-intensity effort with recovery periods.

content: The first macrocycle of the general preparation for power sports can consist of tempo training (400-600 m) in sets of repetitions of the same distance or descending ladders (varying intensity according to the distance of each repetition). In both cases, there is a weekly variation of distances and intensities, from general to more specific training parameters. Distances can be broken down to perform more sport-specific shuttle runs.
summary: Initial training for power sports often involves tempo runs and descending ladders, gradually progressing to more sport-specific shuttle runs.

content: Endurance training is still prescribed to develop aerobic endurance. The progression from aerobic-dominant types of training (intensity zones 5 to 3) to anaerobic lactic system training (zone 2) should follow the natural progression of the annual plan, beginning with the preparatory phase and moving into the competitive phase.
summary: Endurance training is used to develop aerobic endurance, and training intensity should progress from aerobic to anaerobic as the season progresses.

content: Figure 11.12 shows heart rate variations during an aerobic power training session in ladder fashion, consisting of three sets of 600-500-400-300-200 m repetitions, at 95%-95%-100%-100%-100% of maximum aerobic speed, with a 1:1 rest interval between reps. The coach must alter the intensity of training throughout a microcycle to enhance the athletes’ physiological adaptation to training and regeneration after a demanding training session.
summary: Aerobic power training involves ladder-style repetitions with varying distances and intensities, and coaches should adjust training intensity throughout microcycles for optimal adaptation and recovery.

content: As most sports use fuel produced by all of the energy systems, training has to be more complex, exposing athletes to all energy systems, especially during the last part of the preparatory and throughout the competitive phases. Six intensity values are proposed in table 11.17 to assist coaches in achieving scientific and well-planned training that considers the physiological profile and the energy requirements of a sport.
summary: Training should encompass all energy systems, with a focus on anaerobic systems during the preparatory and competitive phases, and coaches should use intensity zones to plan training effectively.

content: The first macrocycle of the general preparation for power sports can consist of tempo training (400-600 m) in sets of repetitions of the same distance or descending ladders (varying intensity according to the distance of each repetition). In both cases, there is a weekly variation of distances and intensities, from general to more specific training parameters. Distances can be broken down to perform more sport-specific shuttle runs.
summary: Initial training for power sports involves tempo and ladder training with varying distances and intensities, progressing towards sport-specific shuttle runs.

content: Figure 11.13 shows a hypothetical periodization of endurance annual plan for a team sport with a 20-week preparation phase.
summary: A hypothetical periodization plan for a team sport's endurance training over a 20-week preparation phase is illustrated.

content: Endurance training employs short work periods of 2 to 8 seconds at a speed exceeding 95% of maximum. This training program utilizes the phosphate energy system. The outcome of this training program is an increase in the quantity of creatine phosphate (CP) stored in the muscle and increased activity of the enzymes that release energy through the ATP-CP system.
summary: Endurance training involves short bursts of high-intensity effort, utilizing the phosphate energy system to increase creatine phosphate stores and enzyme activity.

content: Long recovery intervals between work periods with a ratio of 1:50 to 1:100 are necessary to completely refill the muscles’ CP supply. If the rest interval is short, the restoration of CP will be incomplete. As a result, anaerobic glycolysis will become the major source of energy, rather than the phosphate reaction. This will produce lactic acid that will reduce speed, and the athlete will not realize the desired training effect.
summary: Long recovery intervals are crucial for replenishing creatine phosphate stores, as short intervals lead to anaerobic glycolysis and lactic acid buildup, hindering training effectiveness.

content: AST or sprint training should not cause muscle acidosis because this is a sign of anaerobic glycolysis.
summary: Sprint training should avoid muscle acidosis, which indicates reliance on anaerobic glycolysis instead of the desired phosphate energy system.

content: The intent of Alactic System Training (AST) is to increase an athlete’s ability to be faster with less effort. AST should improve the start and acceleration.
summary: Alactic System Training (AST) aims to enhance an athlete's speed and acceleration by improving the alactic energy system.

content: Zone 2: Lactic Acid Training (LAT) increases an athlete’s ability to perform during lactic efforts and tolerate lactic acid buildup. LAT is useful for fast repetitions of 15 to 90 seconds.
summary: Lactic Acid Training (LAT) improves an athlete's ability to perform during lactic efforts and tolerate lactic acid buildup, suitable for repetitions between 15 and 90 seconds.

content: Very high levels of lactic acid buildup can result from high-intensity reps of 40 to 50 seconds. The fastest rate of lactic acid accumulation happens with maximum effort between 12 and 16 seconds.
summary: High-intensity repetitions of 40 to 50 seconds can lead to significant lactic acid buildup, with the fastest accumulation occurring between 12 and 16 seconds of maximum effort.

content: Power output during lactic efforts is improved via the increase of the lactic energy system’s metabolic enzymes, as well as adaptations of the nervous system. Performance in lactic power events (10 to 20 seconds in duration) seems to be subject to a major limitation involving the nervous system’s ability to maintain the frequency of discharge to the muscles rather than any metabolic reasons.
summary: Lactic power output is enhanced through increased metabolic enzymes and nervous system adaptations. However, performance in lactic power events (10-20 seconds) may be limited by the nervous system's ability to maintain muscle activation frequency.

content: Lactic acid tolerance increases as a result of skeletal muscles repeatedly removing lactic acid from the bloodstream. Studies have demonstrated that lactate transporters increase in number as a function of high-intensity training. The ability to clear lactic acid from the bloodstream and transport it to slow-twitch muscle fibers for energy usage is an adaptive response that delays fatigue and inevitably improves performance in sports that require lactic acid tolerance.
summary: Repeated high-intensity training increases lactate transporter numbers, enhancing the ability to clear lactic acid from the bloodstream and utilize it for energy, delaying fatigue and improving performance in lactic-dependent sports.

content: An athlete can perform better for longer if his nervous system is trained to maintain the frequency of discharge for the duration of a lactic effort or if he can tolerate the pain of acidosis (high lactic acid concentrations in the blood). Therefore, the purposes of training in intensity zone 2 are to adapt to the nervous strain of longer maximum-intensity efforts, to resist the acidic effect of lactic acid buildup, to buffer the effects of lactic acid, to increase lactic acid removal from the working muscles, and to increase the athlete’s physiological and psychological tolerance of the pain of training and of challenging competitions.
summary: Training in intensity zone 2 aims to improve the nervous system's ability to maintain muscle activation frequency during lactic efforts, enhance lactic acid tolerance, and buffer its effects, ultimately improving performance and tolerance to pain during training and competition.

content: Training for intensity zone 2 comes in the following variations: 1. Lactic power short: Organize a series of shorter, near-maximum and maximum-intensity repetitions or drills (3 to 10 seconds) with shorter rest intervals (15 seconds to 4 minutes, depending on duration of effort, number of repetitions, and relative intensity) that result in only partial removal of lactic acid from the system. The physiological consequence of this type of training is that the athlete tolerates increased amounts of lactic acid while producing high levels of anaerobic power under the condition of extreme acidosis. This method is often used as the competitive season approaches and the athlete’s system is challenged to the maximum capacity. 2. Lactic power long: Organize near-maximum and maximum-intensity repetitions of longer duration (10 to 20 seconds) that make the lactic acid energy system work at its maximum rate of energy production. This method is one of the highest possible stressors for the neuromuscular system. Therefore, to repeat the same quality of work, the athlete needs very long rest intervals (12 to 30 minutes, depending on the athlete’s performance level and the number of repetitions) to facilitate an almost complete removal of lactic acid and the recovery of the central nervous system.
summary: Training in intensity zone 2 can be implemented through two variations: 'Lactic power short' involves shorter, high-intensity repetitions with shorter rest intervals, enhancing lactic acid tolerance and anaerobic power, while 'Lactic power long' utilizes longer repetitions with longer rest intervals to maximize the lactic acid energy system's output and challenge the neuromuscular system.

content: During a game, an American football linebacker performs 40 to 60 short accelerations of 3 to 6 seconds each with rest intervals of 1 to 3 minutes.
summary: American football linebackers perform numerous short accelerations during a game, with rest intervals between each burst.

content: The physiological consequence of this type of training is that the athlete tolerates increased amounts of lactic acid while producing high levels of anaerobic power under the condition of extreme acidosis. This method is often used as the competitive season approaches and the athlete’s system is challenged to the maximum capacity. This method is one of the highest possible stressors for the neuromuscular system. Therefore, to repeat the same quality of work, the athlete needs very long rest intervals (12 to 30 minutes, depending on the athlete’s performance level and the number of repetitions) to facilitate an almost complete removal of lactic acid and the recovery of the central nervous system. If the rest interval is not long enough, recovery is incomplete and injury risk is high.
summary: This training method pushes athletes to their limits, increasing lactic acid tolerance and anaerobic power, requiring long rest intervals for recovery.

content: Lactic power long: Organize near-maximum and maximum-intensity repetitions of longer duration (10 to 20 seconds) that make the lactic acid energy system work at its maximum rate of energy production.  The purpose of lactic tolerance training is to push athletes beyond their pain threshold. Lactic tolerance training should not be used more than two times per week. Overdoing lactic tolerance training may increase the risk of injury, overreaching, and overtraining.
summary: Lactic power long training involves near-maximum repetitions lasting 10-20 seconds, while lactic tolerance training pushes athletes beyond their pain threshold, requiring careful frequency to avoid overtraining.

content: Lactic capacity training involves high-intensity repetitions lasting 20 to 60 seconds that result in over 12 mmol of lactic acid. To repeat the same quality of work in lactic capacity training, athletes need moderate rest intervals of 4 to 8 minutes, depending on the duration of effort, number of repetitions, and relative intensity. If rest intervals in lactic capacity training are not long enough, lactic acid removal is incomplete, leading to acidosis and a slower repetition speed.
summary: Lactic capacity training involves high-intensity repetitions lasting 20-60 seconds, requiring moderate rest intervals to ensure complete lactic acid removal.

content: Both the central (heart) and peripheral (capillaries at the level of the working muscle) parts of the oxygen transport system are heavily taxed during training and competition. Athletes with a larger V.O2 capacity have demonstrated better performances in endurance events. Increased V.O2max results from improved oxygen transportation by the circulatory system and increased extraction and use of O2 by the muscular system.
summary: Training and competition heavily tax the oxygen transport system, with a larger V.O2 capacity linked to better endurance performance.

content: Training periods for V.O2max development last 1 to 6 minutes at 90% to 100% of maximum oxygen consumption. The number of repetitions in a V.O2max training session depends on the duration of the sporting event. Athletes can improve V.O2max through shorter work periods of 30 seconds to 2 minutes with short rest intervals of 10-30 seconds at an intensity above V.O2max. Training effects from V.O2max training result from the accumulative effect of several repetitions (4 to 12) that reach V.O2max.
summary: V.O2max training involves high-intensity intervals lasting 1-6 minutes, with the number of repetitions depending on the sport, and shorter intervals with short rest periods can also be effective.

content: Anaerobic threshold training (AnTT) refers to the intensity of an exercise where the rate of lactic acid diffusion in the bloodstream equals its removal rate (AnTT = 4-6 mmol). Lactic acid produced in muscles diffuses into adjacent resting muscles, lowering its concentration level. Lactic acid is metabolized in the working muscle and removed from the blood by the heart, liver, and muscles at the rate it is accumulated.
summary: Anaerobic threshold training (AnTT) focuses on the intensity where lactic acid production equals removal, highlighting the body's lactate metabolism.

content: AnTT can use shorter repetitions of 1 to 6 minutes at 85% to 90% of V.O2max or 92% to 96% of maximum heart rate with slightly longer rests between bouts (work-to-rest ratio between 1:0.5 and 1:1). AnTT can also use longer repetitions of 5 to 7 repetitions of 8 to 15 minutes at 80% to 85% of V.O2max or 87% to 92% of maximum heart rate with a work-to-rest ratio between 1:0.3 and 1:0.5. During AnTT, the athlete should feel mild distress with a speed slightly faster than comfortable.
summary: AnTT can be performed with shorter or longer repetitions at intensities based on V.O2max or heart rate, aiming for a mild distress level.

content: High aerobic capacity is a decisive factor for all events of medium and long duration. High aerobic capacity is a determinant for all sports in which the oxygen supply represents a limiting factor. Using Aerobic Threshold Training (ATT) is beneficial for most sports because it enhances quick recovery following training and competition. ATT develops the functional efficiency of the cardiorespiratory and nervous systems. ATT enhances the economical functioning of the metabolic system. ATT increases the capacity to tolerate stress for long periods.
summary: High aerobic capacity is crucial for endurance events and sports limited by oxygen supply. Aerobic Threshold Training (ATT) improves cardiorespiratory and metabolic function, aiding recovery and stress tolerance.

content: ATT is performed mostly through a high volume of work without interruption (uniform pace), or interval training using repetitions longer than 10 minutes. The duration of an ATT session could be between 1 and 2 hours. The athlete achieves the intended training effect only when the lactic acid concentration is between 2 and 3 mmol, with a heart rate of 130 to 150 beats/min. During the competition phase, you can plan ATT one or two times per week as a method of maintaining aerobic capacity and as a recovery session to reduce intensity but maintain the general fitness level.
summary: ATT involves high-volume work, either continuous or interval-based, lasting 1-2 hours, aiming for a specific lactic acid and heart rate range. It's used for maintaining aerobic capacity and recovery during competition.

content: Aerobic Compensation Training (CoT) facilitates athletes’ recovery following competitions and the high-intensity training sessions characteristic of intensity zones 2 and 3. To eliminate metabolites from the system and speed recovery and regeneration, workouts must be planned using very light intensity (45% to 60% of V.O2max).
summary: Aerobic Compensation Training (CoT) aids recovery after competitions and high-intensity training using light-intensity workouts to eliminate metabolites.

content: High-intensity endurance training is a necessary component of adaptation and performance enhancement. Strenuous exercise often negatively affects the body before it can recover and strengthen. Recovery and regeneration can be aided by active recovery methods such as cycling or running for 5 to 20 minutes at about 50% of maximum capacity.
summary: High-intensity endurance training is essential for adaptation and performance, but strenuous exercise requires adequate recovery, which can be aided by active recovery methods.

content: Aerobic Compensation Training (CoT) facilitates athletes’ recovery following competitions and the high-intensity training sessions characteristic of intensity zones 2 and 3. To eliminate metabolites from the system and speed recovery and regeneration, workouts must be planned using very light intensity (45% to 60% of V.O2max). Active recovery (along with proper postworkout nutrition) has been shown to counteract the increase in cortisol and adrenaline, override the drop in white blood cell count, and eliminate the drop in neutrophil and monocyte count. Active recovery reignites immune system function following strenuous training, which in turn allows the body to regenerate faster.
summary: Active recovery methods, including light-intensity exercise and proper nutrition, aid in post-workout recovery and regeneration by reducing stress hormones and boosting immune function.

content: High-intensity endurance training is a necessary component of adaptation and performance enhancement. Strenuous exercise often negatively affects the body before it can recover and strengthen. Following strenuous endurance-type training with static rest (such as lying or sitting down) can delay regeneration of the body’s systems and removal of the by-products of training. Recovery and regeneration can be aided by active recovery methods such as cycling or running for 5 to 20 minutes at about 50% of maximum capacity. By the end of the training session, the difficult part of the workout is complete, but athletes who are willing to live with the sacrifice needed for improvement and adaptation should devote another 15 to 20 minutes to foster healing and regeneration. Choosing not to do so slows the recovery process and may negatively affect the next training session; it also leads to overtraining and injury.
summary: High-intensity training is crucial for performance, but recovery is equally important. Active recovery and avoiding prolonged static rest are essential for optimal regeneration and preventing overtraining.

content: Recovery and regeneration are slowed by elevated levels of plasma cortisol and adrenaline, and by decreased levels of white blood cells and low levels of immune system catalysts (e.g., neutrophils and monocytes).
summary: Elevated stress hormones and suppressed immune function hinder recovery and regeneration.

content: The critical element is training the energy systems, which represent the foundation of good performance. The coach must do this in conjunction with the technical and tactical elements, based on knowledge of the physiological profile prevailing in an event.
summary: Training should prioritize energy system development while integrating technical and tactical elements specific to the athlete's sport.

content: Periodization involves distributing energy system emphasis in training sessions. The distribution of six intensities per microcycle depends on the training phase, athlete's needs, and planned competitions. Higher intensity training generates more fatigue. Alternating high and low intensity training sessions facilitates supercompensation. Purely aerobic sports, like marathon running and cross-country skiing, primarily involve alternating intensities. Combining intensities one and three or two and six allows for recovery after anaerobic training. Intensity combinations like 1 + 3 + 2 model a race with a phosphate system start, lactic and oxygen system body, and lactic acid tolerance finish.
summary: Periodization involves strategically distributing training intensities based on the training phase, athlete needs, and sport-specific demands. Alternating high and low intensities promotes recovery and mimics race scenarios.

content: To avoid overtraining, consider the sequence and frequency of intensity symbols while adhering to supercompensation.
summary: Careful planning of training intensity and frequency is crucial to prevent overtraining and allow for adequate recovery.

content: All sports require some level of endurance. Endurance is classified as low-intensity exercise endurance (LIEE) and high-intensity exercise endurance (HIEE). LIEE is needed in aerobic sports requiring continuous work for long durations. HIEE requires repetitive high-intensity activities interspersed with recovery periods. HIEE training methods improve LIEE performance, but LIEE training methods can reduce HIEE performance.
summary: Endurance is essential in all sports and encompasses both low-intensity (LIEE) and high-intensity (HIEE) forms. Training adaptations in one type of endurance can influence the other.

content: Team sport coaches should tailor endurance programs to player positions, dominant energy systems, match running volume, and maximum aerobic speed. Individualized training programs maximize sport-specific endurance development.
summary: Endurance training programs should be individualized based on player position, energy system demands, and sport-specific requirements.

content: Speed, agility, and speed endurance are crucial abilities that can affect performance in a variety of sports. These abilities are related and depend in large part on the athlete’s muscular strength. Speed is the ability to cover a distance quickly. The ability to move quickly in a straight line or in different directions (changes of direction) is an integral component of successful performance in a wide variety of sports.
summary: Speed, agility, and speed endurance are interconnected performance attributes that rely heavily on muscular strength and are essential for success in various sports.

content: Straight-line sprinting can be broken down into three phases: acceleration, attainment of maximum speed, and maintenance of maximum speed. Acceleration is the ability to increase maximum velocity in a minimum amount of time.
summary: Sprinting involves distinct phases: acceleration, reaching maximum speed, and maintaining that speed.

content: Traditionally, a coach designs a training program by assigning certain physical, technical, or tactical objectives to certain days of a microcycle. When planning a microcycle, the coach does not need to write down the training content, but the mathematical values of the intensities needed in the cycle.
summary: Training programs are structured around microcycles, with coaches focusing on assigning specific training intensities rather than detailed content.

content: During very demanding weeks of training, intensity zone 6 may be used one to three times, sometimes in combination with other intensities (in that case, at the end of a workout).
summary: High-intensity training (intensity zone 6) can be incorporated strategically during demanding training weeks.

content: Speed is the ability to cover a distance quickly. The ability to move quickly in a straight line or in different directions (changes of direction) is an integral component of successful performance in a wide variety of sports. Straight-line sprinting can be broken down into three phases: acceleration, attainment of maximum speed, and maintenance of maximum speed.
summary: This chunk defines speed and its importance in sports, outlining the three phases of straight-line sprinting.

content: Acceleration is the ability to increase maximum velocity in a minimum amount of time. Acceleration determines sprint performance abilities over short distances (e.g., 5 m and 20 m) and is usually measured in m/s or simply as a unit of time (e.g., s). The ability to accelerate differentiates between athletes for a variety of sports. During a 100 m race, untrained sprinters achieve maximum speed within 20 to 30 m, whereas highly trained sprinters do not attain maximum speed until around 50 to 60 m.
summary: This chunk focuses on acceleration as a key component of sprinting, defining it and highlighting its importance in differentiating athletes.

content: It is likely that maximum strength levels for the knee extensors, hip extensors, and plantar flexors (calf muscles) explains the acceleratory abilities of various athletes because strength is strongly related to sprinting ability. Faster sprinters are significantly stronger and are able to accelerate at faster rates than their slower counterparts. In many sports, such as soccer, the ability to accelerate underlies successful game play.
summary: This chunk connects sprinting ability to muscular strength, emphasizing the role of strength in acceleration.

content: During soccer game play, the average sprint length is around 17 m and ranges from 5 to 50 or 60 m. Often, these sprints are initiated while the athlete is moving at slower speeds or when the athlete is making a breakaway or initiating a tackle. Therefore, the ability to accelerate rapidly in the first few steps is essential to effective game play.
summary: This chunk focuses on the application of acceleration in soccer, highlighting its importance in game play.

content: A sprint training program that targets the acceleration phase should develop specific strength (maximum strength and power) characteristics and mechanical skills.
summary: This chunk provides a training recommendation for developing acceleration.

content: After completing the acceleration phase of a sprint, the athlete achieves maximum running velocity. Athletes may have great acceleratory capacity but lack the ability to achieve high velocities in this phase of a sprint, which suggests that acceleration and the maximum speed of running are very specific sprinting qualities.
summary: This chunk describes the transition from acceleration to maximum velocity, noting the specificity of these sprinting qualities.

content: Differences in the kinematics of the acceleratory and maximum velocity portions of a sprint support this observation and suggest that running mechanics and specific strength qualities play a role in developing maximum running speed.
summary: This chunk further emphasizes the role of mechanics and strength in achieving maximum running speed.

content: The final phase of a straight-line sprint requires the athlete to maintain maximum speed, described among sprinters as speed endurance. Although the athlete is moving at maximum speed, the development of fatigue begins to affect the athlete’s ability to maintain force output, effective running mechanics, and thus speed.
summary: This chunk introduces the concept of speed endurance as the final phase of sprinting.

content: During a bout of maximum sprinting, insufficient rest intervals can lead to depletion of phosphocreatine and accumulation of lactic acid, which is formed in response to the rapid glycolytic rate. As phosphocreatine levels decrease, there is a higher reliance on the anaerobic lactic system; as lactic acid increases, there is an accumulation of hydrogen ions (H+), which can reduce the athlete’s capacity to exert force and lead to a breakdown in running mechanics and mechanical efficiency.
summary: This chunk explains the physiological factors contributing to fatigue during sprinting.

content: Both short-repetitions sprinting programs with incomplete rest intervals and long-repetitions sprinting programs with complete rest intervals appear effective in improving muscle buffering capacity and reducing fatigue. Maximum speed, on the other hand, should be trained with short repetitions and complete rest intervals.
summary: This chunk outlines training methods for improving speed endurance and maximum speed.

content: Speed is the expression of a set of skills and abilities that allow for high movement velocities. Although it is often suggested that skills and abilities are unrelated, they are highly related and thus can be developed with specific training practices.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the interconnectedness of skills and abilities in developing speed.

content: The application of appropriate sprint training methods in conjunction with a periodized training plan can improve sprint performance (e.g., acceleration, achievement of maximum velocity, and maintenance of high velocities) and thereby improve competitive performance.
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of a structured training plan for improving sprint performance.

content: To develop speed, the coach and athlete must understand the factors that affect an athlete’s ability to generate high movement speeds. Speed training isn’t just for track athletes; it should be incorporated into training plans for athletes in all sports.
summary: This chunk stresses the importance of understanding the factors influencing speed and its relevance across various sports.

content: Sprinting involves a rapid release of energy that allows for a high rate of cross-bridge cycling within the muscle and a rapid and repetitive production of muscular force.
summary: This chunk describes the physiological mechanisms underlying sprinting.

content: The body meets the energy demands of muscle under sprinting conditions by (1) altering the enzymatic activity of specific energy-producing pathways, (2) increasing the amount of energy stored within the muscle, and (3) increasing the muscles’ ability to overcome the accumulation of fatigue-inducing metabolites.
summary: This chunk details the body's adaptations to meet the energy demands of sprinting.

content: All three of the body’s energy systems (phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative) contribute to energy supply. The phosphagen and glycolytic systems predominate during most sprinting activities. The degree of contribution of the oxidative energy system depends on the duration, length, and number of sprints performed as well as the rest interval between repetitions. If the sprinting activity is long (≥30 s in duration) and repeated several times with short rest intervals between bouts, the contribution of the oxidative energy system will progressively increase.
summary: This chunk explains the role of different energy systems in sprinting.

content: The enzymatic adaptations will be very specific to the sprinting tasks performed in training.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the specificity of enzymatic adaptations to training.

content: The response of the phosphagen system (ATP-PC) to sprinting activities shows that muscular stores of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and phosphocreatine (PCr) can be significantly reduced in response to bouts of sprint training. The rate of PCr breakdown is significantly higher in the fastest sprinters. This may occur as a result of an increased rate of creatine phosphokinase (CPK) activity in response to sprint training.
summary: This chunk describes the adaptations of the phosphagen system to sprint training.

content: To meet the increased demand for ATP during sprint training, an increased myokinase (MK) enzyme activity is stimulated, which may increase the rate of ATP resynthesis.
summary: This chunk explains the role of myokinase in ATP resynthesis during sprint training.

content: The enzymatic adaptations will be very specific to the sprinting tasks performed in training. The response of the phosphagen system (ATP-PC) to sprinting activities shows that muscular stores of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and phosphocreatine (PCr) can be significantly reduced in response to bouts of sprint training. The rate of PCr breakdown is significantly higher in the fastest sprinters. This may occur as a result of an increased rate of creatine phosphokinase (CPK) activity in response to sprint training. To meet the increased demand for ATP during sprint training, an increased myokinase (MK) enzyme activity is stimulated, which may increase the rate of ATP resynthesis. This increase in MK activity has been reported to occur in response to training with both short- and long-duration sprints.
summary: Sprint training induces specific enzymatic adaptations, particularly affecting the phosphagen system, with increased PCr breakdown, CPK activity, and MK activity to meet ATP demands.

content: Several key enzymes associated with the glycolytic system also are affected by various forms of sprint training. Glycogen phosphorylase (PHOS), the enzyme responsible for stimulating muscle glycogen breakdown, is increased in response to both short (<10 s) and long (>10 s) bouts of sprinting. Phosphofructokinase (PFK) activity (the enzyme that regulates the rate of the glycolytic system) appears to increase in response to short-duration, long-duration, or combination sprinting activities. Changes in PFK activity may be of particular importance because the rate of PFK activity has been related to performance in high-intensity exercise such as sprinting. Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) activity has been shown to increase in response to both short- and long-duration sprinting.
summary: Sprint training, regardless of duration, enhances glycolytic enzyme activity, including PHOS, PFK, and LDH, crucial for energy production during high-intensity exercise.

content: The contribution of the oxidative system to an acute 10 s bout of sprinting is considered to be minimal (~13%). During multiple sprints of a longer duration (≥30 s) there is a significant decrease in the glycolytic energetic supply and a concomitant decrease in maximum power output and speed. This may occur in response to elevated H+ concentrations slowing the glycolytic rate and allowing for a decreased production of lactate. To meet the energy demands of the exercising muscle, the contribution of oxidative metabolism increases. However, the contribution of oxidative metabolism to the energy supply is affected largely by the duration of the sprint and the rest interval between bouts. Longer sprints performed multiple times with short rest intervals will increase the contribution of the oxidative systems to energy supply. With this increased energy supply from oxidative metabolism, it is not surprising that there are increases in succinate dehydrogenase and citrate synthase activity (key enzymes of the oxidative system) in response to sprinting.
summary: Sprint duration influences energy system contribution. Short sprints rely primarily on phosphagen and glycolysis, while longer, repeated sprints increase oxidative metabolism, reflected in enzyme adaptations.

content: Short and long sprint interval training bouts can significantly increase the athlete’s aerobic power (V.O2max). High-intensity interval training is an important tool for the development of sport-specific fitness for sports dominated by both anaerobic (e.g., soccer, American football, basketball) and aerobic (e.g., long-distance running, cycling, skiing) energy supplies. High-intensity interval training resulted in significantly greater increases in V.O2max than traditional aerobic training. The interval-based training increase in V.O2max was related to significant increases in running economy, distances covered, number of involvements with the ball, and average work intensity during a soccer game.
summary: High-intensity interval training (HIIT) enhances both anaerobic and aerobic fitness, leading to greater V.O2max improvements compared to traditional aerobic training and translating to better performance in team sports.

content: Adaptation to multiple bouts of high-intensity sprint intervals seems to produce a superior training stimulus, which appears to translate to team sports play better than does traditional endurance training. High-intensity interval training should be the preferred endurance training modality for team sports, especially during the specific preparation phase.
summary: HIIT is superior to traditional endurance training for team sports, particularly during the specific preparation phase, as it better translates to on-field performance.

content: An increased availability of metabolic substrates (PCr, ATP, and glycogen) prior to the initiation of an exercise bout may enhance the athlete’s ability to perform or maintain high-intensity exercise. A short sprint protocol elevated resting PCr and glycogen levels, whereas a long protocol elevated only resting glycogen levels. The sprint training program may alter the energy substrates stored in the muscle. These changes to energy substrate storage may have contributed to the increases in sprint performance noted in the investigation.
summary: Sprint training protocols can alter energy substrate availability, potentially enhancing performance by increasing PCr, ATP, and glycogen levels.

content: Accumulation of fatigue-inducing metabolites, such as lactic acid, appears to contribute to impaired sprinting performance. With an increase in lactic acid accumulation, there is an increase in H+ ion concentration (which can inhibit PFK activity), a decrease in the Ca2+ transport rate, and a decrease in the rate of cross bridge cycling within the skeletal muscle. If the H+ ions are not buffered, the ability to sprint, and more importantly to repetitively sprint, will be impaired.
summary: Lactic acid accumulation during sprinting negatively impacts performance by inhibiting key metabolic processes and reducing muscle function.

content: The use of high-intensity interval training has been demonstrated to result in an increased buffering capacity. With this increased buffering capacity, there is an increased ability to maintain energetic flux and thus to maintain high power output performance, such as sprinting. When developing a physiological base for sprint and agility performance, it is important to include high-intensity interval training in the overall training plan as it has the ability to increase buffering capacity, which allows the body to deal with the accumulation of metabolic fatigue inducing factors (e.g., lactic acid or H+).
summary: HIIT improves buffering capacity, allowing athletes to better manage metabolic byproducts and maintain high-intensity performance during repeated sprints.

content: Performance in sprinting activities depends largely on muscle fiber characteristics as well as neural activation patterns. A higher percentage of type IIb or IIx myosin heavy chain (MHC) isoforms (fast-twitch) is advantageous for activities that require the expression of high power outputs or forces, such as those seen in sprinting. A continuum of muscle fiber MHC isoforms ranging from type I (slow-twitch) to type IIa, IIb, or IIx can be delineated, along with specific hybrids that exist as transitional states (e.g., I/IIa, I/IIa/IIx, IIa/IIx) between the major subtypes. Within this continuum of individual MHC isoforms, a spectrum of force- and power-generating capacity can be created.
summary: Sprinting performance relies on a combination of muscle fiber type (predominantly fast-twitch) and efficient neural activation patterns.

content: The morphological characteristics of the muscle as well as adaptations to neural activation patterns can play a significant role in the expression of high-velocity movements. Performance in sprinting activities depends largely on muscle fiber characteristics as well as neural activation patterns. Sprint performance capacity may be partially explained by the athlete’s fiber type. Sprint performance is significantly correlated with the athlete’s percentage of type II fibers. Sprinters have been shown to have a high percentage of type II fibers. Sprint-trained athletes appear to exhibit greater rates of force development and force output than either untrained or endurance-trained individuals.
summary: Sprinting performance is influenced by both muscle fiber type and neural activation patterns, with sprint-trained athletes exhibiting superior force production.

content: A continuum of muscle fiber MHC isoforms ranging from type I (slow-twitch) to type IIa, IIb, or IIx can be delineated, along with specific hybrids that exist as transitional states (e.g., I/IIa, I/IIa/IIx, IIa/IIx) between the major subtypes. Within this continuum of individual MHC isoforms, a spectrum of force- and power-generating capacity can be created. Type I fibers exhibit the lowest force- and power-generating capacity, whereas type IIb or IIx MHC isoforms are associated with the highest. A higher percentage of type IIb or IIx myosin heavy chain (MHC) isoforms (fast-twitch) is advantageous for activities that require the expression of high power outputs or forces, such as those seen in sprinting.
summary: Muscle fibers exist on a continuum from slow-twitch to fast-twitch, with fast-twitch fibers being more advantageous for sprinting due to their higher force and power output.

content: Sprint training can increase type II fiber content. A sprint training program that entails a frequent exposure to lactic bouts induces a bidirectional shift toward type IIa fibers. Prolonged endurance training usually induces a shift from type II to a type I fiber composition. Endurance training included in the training plan can mute the favorable adaptation to the muscle fiber composition. Providing insufficient rest between repetitions or sets of sprinting efforts results in a fiber type transition similar to that seen with endurance training. Including only long-duration sprints results in a fiber type transition similar to that seen with endurance training. Traditional endurance training, such as long slow distance (LSD) work, should be avoided by athletes who must express high levels of sprinting speed.
summary: Sprint training promotes type II fiber development, while endurance training can hinder this adaptation. Insufficient rest or long-duration sprints can also lead to a shift towards endurance-type fibers.

content: High-velocity training, such as maximum intensity sprints, requires a high level of neural activation. Several neural factors influence sprinting ability, include the sequencing of muscle activation, the stretch reflex, and the development of neural fatigue. A multitude of different muscles are activated at specific times and intensities to optimize movement speed. Training results in a refinement of neural innervations’ patterns and a more developed and efficient motor program. The ability to fully or selectively recruit muscle fibers is important for sprinting. Type II muscle fibers may be important when optimizing sprint performance capacity. Engaging in training practices that use ballistic or explosive activities (e.g., sprinting, weightlifting, plyometrics) may alter the motor unit recruitment pattern so that type II fibers are recruited sooner.
summary: Sprinting relies heavily on neural activation, including muscle sequencing, stretch reflex, and motor unit recruitment. Training enhances these neural adaptations, optimizing muscle fiber recruitment for sprinting.

content: Stretch reflex short latency stretch reflexes appear to influence sprint running performance. The stretch reflex appears to enhance force production when the athlete is sprinting. During the nonsupport phase of sprinting, numerous muscles involved in the development of force are activated, and there is an increase in muscle spindle sensitivity. Training-induced adaptations to muscle spindle sensitivity may occur in response to sprint training and can improve muscle stiffness upon ground contact. Increases in muscle stiffness appear to be related to both maximum running velocity and speed maintenance. Increases in stiffness also appear to decrease contact time during the support phase of sprinting by increasing the rate of force development and the peak forces generated during this time.
summary: The stretch reflex and muscle spindle sensitivity play a crucial role in sprint performance, contributing to force production and increased muscle stiffness for faster running.

content: As fatigue manifests itself during a maximum 100 m sprint, there is a slight decline in speed, especially in the later stages of the race, which corresponds to a decrease in stride rate. Ross and colleagues suggested that this reduction in stride rate is the result of neural fatigue, whereby motor unit recruitment patterns are altered and change the motor-unit firing rate. During sprinting, such as the 100 m dash, there is a preferential recruitment of type II (fast-twitch) muscle fibers, which are particularly susceptible to acute neural fatigue as a result of their short contraction times and high axial conduction velocities. As the 100 m dash progresses, there is a progressive reduction in recruitment that probably occurs as a result of a less-than-optimal output from the motor cortex.
summary: Neural fatigue, particularly affecting fast-twitch muscle fibers, contributes to the decline in speed observed during a 100m sprint, leading to a reduction in stride rate.

content: As the athlete progresses through the specific preparatory phase and into the competitive phase, the use of shorter sprints with longer rest intervals will help the athlete produce higher speeds of movement. Short rest intervals and longer sprinting bouts should be reserved for the general preparatory phase of the annual training plan.
summary: Training periodization for sprinting involves adjusting sprint duration and rest intervals based on the training phase, with shorter sprints and longer rest periods being beneficial for competitive performance.

content: Neural fatigue can affect sprint performance by reducing voluntary force-generating capacity. As fatigue manifests itself during a maximum 100 m sprint, there is a slight decline in speed, especially in the later stages of the race, which corresponds to a decrease in stride rate. Ross and colleagues suggested that this reduction in stride rate is the result of neural fatigue, whereby motor unit recruitment patterns are altered and change the motor-unit firing rate. During sprinting, such as the 100 m dash, there is a preferential recruitment of type II (fast-twitch) muscle fibers, which are particularly susceptible to acute neural fatigue as a result of their short contraction times and high axial conduction velocities. As the 100 m dash progresses, there is a progressive reduction in recruitment that probably occurs as a result of a less-than-optimal output from the motor cortex. A 4.9% to 8.7% reduction in muscle activation has been noted once maximum velocity has been achieved during a 100 m dash. This reduction in recruitment may occur as a result of fatigue of the neuromuscular junction, a decreased firing rate, or a reduction in the recruitment of higher threshold motor units (type IIb or IIx). Ross and colleagues postulated that acute neural fatigue may decrease reflex sensitivity. It is possible that large volumes of traumatic stretch shortening can reduce reflex sensitivity, which could reduce force output during running. This reduction in force output could impair sprint performance.
summary: Neural fatigue during sprinting, particularly in the later stages, leads to decreased stride rate and force output, ultimately impairing sprint performance.

content: Sprinting is a ballistic activity in which a series of running strides launch the body forward with maximum velocity over some distance. Sprinting contains two major phases: a nonsupport (or flight) phase and a support phase. The nonsupport phase contains recovery and ground preparations, whereas the support phase includes both landing or eccentric breaking and concentric propulsion subphases. As an athlete sprints, she alternates between the nonsupport and support phases. As the athlete enters the support phase, a force-absorbing eccentric action precedes an explosive concentric contraction (propulsion phase). With increasing running speed, the time spent in the nonsupport phase generally increases and the time spent in the support phase decreases. As the time spent in the support phase decreases, it becomes particularly important that the athlete demonstrate a high rate of force development (RFD) to maintain or continue to elevate running speed. The decrease in duration of the supporting phase is essential for success in any high-velocity activities and directly depends on the improvement of the athlete’s maximum strength and power capabilities. Maximum strength and power will decrease the duration of the contact (supporting) phase. To achieve that, you have to improve maximum strength and power of the quadriceps, gastrocnemius, and soleus muscles.
summary: Sprinting involves alternating nonsupport and support phases, with decreasing support phase duration requiring high rate of force development for increased speed. This is achieved through improved strength and power in key leg muscles.

content: The speed at which an athlete runs or sprints depends largely on an interaction between stride rate and stride length. As the athlete accelerates and approaches maximum velocity, stride rate increases to a greater extent than stride length. Stride length is related to both body height and the force exerted during the propulsion phase. Elite sprinters tend to achieve a greater stride rate and stride length in a shorter amount of time, which suggests that both stride rate and length can be optimized with appropriate training interventions.
summary: Sprint speed is determined by both stride rate and length, with stride rate increasing more during acceleration. Elite sprinters demonstrate high stride rates and lengths, indicating trainability of both factors.

content: The phases of a sprint are the start, acceleration, and maximum velocity. The optimal starting position is a medium heel-to-toe stance, regardless of whether the athlete is in a two-point (standing) or three- or four-point (crouching) stance. Initiating a sprint from the starting stance is then accomplished by overcoming inertia through the explosive application of force against the ground with both legs. The front leg extends while the rear leg swings forward with what is called a low heel recovery, which will be maintained for at least two steps before gradually raising. As the rear leg comes forward, the hip is flexed to at least 90° and the ankle is dorsiflexed, preparing the foot to apply force down to the ground. At the same time, the arm opposite the leg initiates starting actions by swinging forward and up, with the elbow flexed at approximately 90°, and the hand moves past the head. As the front leg moves through the support phase and extends, the opposite arm should swing backward with the elbow fully extended. When the start is performed correctly, the body is at an angle of 45° or less from horizontal, the head is aligned with the back, and the push-off is performed with perfect alignment from the tip of the foot to the shoulder. Both stride rate and length will increase during the first 15 to 20 m or 9 to 12 strides during the initial acceleration period from a static start. Analysis of elite sprinters show that stride length increases more than stride frequency during the first 20 m of a 100 m dash. It appears that stride frequency is the performance discriminant among fast and slow team sports athletes. During the preliminary portion of the acceleration period, the body will have a forward lean (≤45°), which will progressively move to a more upright position as the athlete approaches maximum velocity. The forward lean position allows the athlete to have the vertical projection of the center of gravity ahead of the supporting legs, a technical position that is favorable to higher acceleration. During the whole acceleration phase, the leg is fully extended and in line with the body’s longitudinal axis at push-off. The recovery of the leg is performed by passing the foot progressively higher at each step, always ending with the thigh perpendicular to the trunk. As the leg enters the support phase, it extends downward and back; at the same time, the elbow angle of the arm simultaneously swinging backward will close a little more with each step, finally staying at 90° for the whole cycle when the athlete is in the upright position typical of the maximum speed phase.
summary: Sprint phases include start, acceleration, and maximum velocity, each with specific biomechanical characteristics. Stride rate and length increase during acceleration, with stride frequency being a key differentiator for athletes.

content: During the preliminary portion of the acceleration period, the body will have a forward lean (≤45°), which will progressively move to a more upright position as the athlete approaches maximum velocity. The forward lean position allows the athlete to have the vertical projection of the center of gravity ahead of the supporting legs, a technical position that is favorable to higher acceleration.
summary: The acceleration phase begins with a forward lean that gradually corrects as velocity increases, optimizing the center of gravity for maximum acceleration.

content: During the whole acceleration phase, the leg is fully extended and in line with the body’s longitudinal axis at push-off. The recovery of the leg is performed by passing the foot progressively higher at each step, always ending with the thigh perpendicular to the trunk.
summary: During acceleration, the leg remains extended and aligned with the body, with a progressively higher recovery motion for each step.

content: As the leg enters the support phase, it extends downward and back; at the same time, the elbow angle of the arm simultaneously swinging backward will close a little more with each step, finally staying at 90° for the whole cycle when the athlete is in the upright position typical of the maximum speed phase. The arm motion should always originate from the shoulder and move backward and forward. These arm motions offset the axial momentum generated by the contralateral leg and hip.
summary: The support phase involves leg extension and backward arm swing, which counterbalance momentum and prepare for the next stride.

content: During the support phase, the athlete will transition between an eccentric and concentric subphase with the use of a stretch shortening cycle (SSC) action. As the athlete accelerates, greater ground reaction forces must be developed to continue to accelerate. These data seem to suggest that strength training, especially activities that improve the rate of force development, is an essential component of a training program designed to enhance sprinting performance.
summary: The support phase utilizes a stretch-shortening cycle (SSC) to generate force, and increasing ground reaction forces are crucial for accelerating.

content: Horizontal ground reaction forces, in particular, seem to be important for sprinting performance. More emphasis should be granted to training means with anteroposterior force vector (kettlebell swing versus Olympic lifts, hip thrust versus squat) or requiring more horizontal force application (horizontal jumps versus vertical jumps) than traditionally used.
summary: Horizontal ground reaction forces are key for sprinting, suggesting a need for training that emphasizes horizontal force production.

content: When maximum velocity is achieved (within 30 to 60 m, depending on the athlete), the trunk position will be more upright. During the maximum velocity phase of a sprint, the time spent in the nonsupport phase will be maximized by the application of vertical ground reaction forces during the initial support phase, allowing sufficient time for the swinging leg to be repositioned in preparation for the transition into the next support phase.
summary: At maximum velocity, the trunk becomes more upright, and the support phase is optimized for maximizing the time spent in the nonsupport phase.

content: Athletes who are able to apply higher horizontal ground reaction forces during the support phase of the maximum velocity portion of a sprint are able to reach and maintain higher velocities of movement.
summary: Higher horizontal ground reaction forces during the maximum velocity phase contribute to achieving and maintaining higher sprint speeds.

content: As the athlete enters the support phase, he will contact the ground directly beneath his center of gravity. While moving into the support phase, the athlete will transition from an eccentric braking action into a concentric action with the use of an SSC action.
summary: The support phase begins with ground contact directly beneath the center of gravity, transitioning from eccentric braking to concentric force generation via an SSC.

content: During the concentric phase, the athlete will perform a ‘triple extension’ of the hip, knee, and ankle to properly apply forces against the ground. After the triple extension is performed, the athlete will then initiate a triple flexion of the ankle, knee, and hip, which will place the heel close to the buttocks. At this point, the heel of the recovery leg will pass above the knee. This triple flexion motion allows the athlete to rapidly move the knee to a position in front of the hip, effectively positioning the leg on the front side of the body. This movement prepares the foot for a rapid descent and allows the athlete to maximize ground reaction forces as the foot moves down and back during ground strike.
summary: The concentric phase involves a triple extension to generate force, followed by a triple flexion to prepare for the next stride and maximize ground reaction forces.

content: During sprint training, the athlete should be conscious of fatigue because the development of fatigue can reduce sprint performance capacity. As fatigue is manifested, stride rate can be decreased, while stride length increases and the duration of the ground support phase can increase. These events effectively decrease the effectiveness of the SSC that is applied between the eccentric and concentric subphases of the support phase. High levels of fatigue may also reduce ranges of leg extension. These breakdowns in running mechanics may be partially explained by the occurrence of metabolic fatigue.
summary: Fatigue negatively impacts sprint performance by reducing stride rate, increasing stride length and support phase duration, and diminishing the effectiveness of the stretch-shortening cycle.

content: Fatigue can reduce sprinting capacity, especially when a series of maximum sprints are performed. This type of fatigue may occur as a result of supraspinal failure, segmental afferent inhibition, depression of motor neuron excitability, loss of excitation branch points, and a decreased ability of the neuromuscular junction to fully activate the muscle. Neuromuscular fatigue can play a large role in reducing sprinting speed.
summary: Fatigue, particularly from repeated maximal sprints, can stem from various neuromuscular factors, ultimately reducing sprinting speed.

content: Speed and speed endurance can be developed by manipulating a multitude of training factors. The acceleration phase can be developed by targeting the ATP-PC system and performing short sprints (10 to 40 m) at 95% to 100% of maximum speed with longer recovery periods between repetitions and sets. Conversely, extensive tempo work in which longer distances (>200 m) are covered and lower intensities (<70% of maximum) are interspersed with short rest intervals (<60 s) will develop the athlete’s aerobic capacity. Table 12.1 gives examples of manipulations for the development of several different aspects of speed and speed endurance.
summary: Speed and speed endurance can be trained through various methods, including short, high-intensity sprints for acceleration development and longer, lower-intensity runs for aerobic capacity.

content: Straight-line sprinting ability is important for track and field athletes and athletes who participate in other field-based sports (e.g., soccer, American football, baseball); but the ability to rapidly accelerate, decelerate, and change direction is quite determinant for court-based and team sports. Often these athletes have to quickly decelerate, stop, and reaccelerate in other directions in response to external cues. These abilities are usually considered an expression of agility.
summary: While straight-line sprinting is crucial for some sports, agility, encompassing acceleration, deceleration, and change of direction, is essential for court-based and team sports.

content: Some literature uses the term quickness synonymously with agility or change-of-direction speed (76, 94). Sheppard and Young (94) suggested that the definition of quickness does not consider deceleration or a change of direction and that quickness in and of itself contributes to agility. The term cutting has been used to describe change-of-direction capabilities (94) and is sometimes falsely used to describe agility.
summary: Quickness, agility, and change-of-direction speed are related concepts, with quickness often contributing to agility, and 'cutting' sometimes being mistakenly used as a synonym for agility.

content: Agility is a complex set of interdependent skills that converge for the athlete to respond to an external stimulus with a rapid deceleration, change of direction, and reacceleration. Agility is affected by perceptual and decision-making ability, the ability to quickly change direction, visual focus, anticipation, pattern recognition, tactical knowledge, technique, body position, leg action, arm action, braking mechanics, change-of-direction speed, sprinting speed, muscle characteristics, power, maximum strength, reactive strength, rate of force development, and stretch-shortening cycle characteristics. During competition, the athlete must be able to perceive a situation, make a decision, and change his direction of movement and speed in response to some external stimulus. The ability to engage in this process involves a complex interaction of visual interpretation, anticipation, recognition of patterns, and knowledge of tactical situations. The ability to visually scan or focus while performing multidirectional tasks appears to positively influence performance. The ability to visually recognize a specific action, process the ramifications of that action, and respond with the appropriate change of direction or movement pattern differentiates between athletes. Knowledge of the tactical situation and the ability to anticipate the potential movements of the opponent also affect the athlete’s ability to change direction appropriately.
summary: Agility is a multi-faceted skill encompassing perceptual, cognitive, and physical components, enabling athletes to respond effectively to external stimuli with rapid deceleration, change of direction, and reacceleration.

content: Often these athletes have to quickly decelerate, stop, and reaccelerate in other directions in response to external cues. These abilities are usually considered an expression of agility. Some literature uses the term quickness synonymously with agility or change-of-direction speed. Sheppard and Young (94) suggested that the definition of quickness does not consider deceleration or a change of direction and that quickness in and of itself contributes to agility. The term cutting has been used to describe change-of-direction capabilities and is sometimes falsely used to describe agility. This term only considers the change of direction initiated by the foot’s ground contact overlooking the determinant role played by the extensors' eccentric strength.
summary: Agility and quickness are related concepts, with agility encompassing a broader range of skills including deceleration and change of direction.

content: Three key factors affect the athlete’s ability to perform change-of-direction tasks: technique, sprinting speed, and muscle characteristics. Leg action, arm action, and braking mechanics can all affect an athlete’s ability to express agility in movements. When the athlete is accelerating or decelerating, the body lean must increase to allow the base of support to move away from the athlete’s center of gravity while the center of gravity is lowered. These actions allow the athlete to maintain dynamic stability and change direction rapidly. When initiating the deceleration action prior to the change of direction, the athlete will decrease her stride length. Upon reacceleration, the athlete will progressively increase both stride length and rate while the body position becomes more vertical or incline in the new direction of movement. It may be warranted for the athlete to run with a lower center of gravity and a more pronounced forward lean when participating in sports that require frequent changes in direction. Powerful arm actions are a fundamental component of multidirectional movements, since the arm’s drive directly affects the athlete’s leg frequency during acceleration or reacceleration. This is not true during deceleration, when the arms’ frequency and radius are visibly reduced, effects that favor a quick deceleration and change of direction.
