summary: Effective change-of-direction performance relies on a combination of technique, sprinting speed, and muscle characteristics, involving precise leg and arm actions, body positioning, and deceleration/acceleration mechanics.

content: Force is essential for both acceleration and deceleration. In the case of acceleration, propulsion force is performed concentrically, while during deceleration force is expressed eccentrically. The latter requires more force since the athlete has to also overcome inertia. For successful deceleration, the athlete has to contact the ground with the full foot to maximize the surface area that is contacting the ground and reducing the eccentric load by engaging the entire lower extremity. Some coaches believe that straight-line sprint training directly affects the athlete’s ability to change direction. This is far from being the case in field and court-related sports. Straight-line sprinting ability explains only a small amount of the variance seen with change-of-direction activities. When only straight-line sprinting is used, there is very little improvement in performance of multidirectional change-of-direction tasks. The addition of the ball (e.g., a soccer ball or basketball) and agility drills can significantly improve the athlete’s ability to perform multidirectional tasks such as change-of-direction movements.
summary: Deceleration requires greater force than acceleration due to overcoming inertia. While straight-line sprinting contributes minimally to change-of-direction performance, incorporating ball drills and agility training significantly enhances multidirectional movement skills.

content: An athlete’s ability to perform multidirectional tasks such as change-of-direction movements is important. Both straight-line sprinting and change-of-direction tasks with and without implements must be incorporated at various stages of the athlete’s development and preparation for competition. There appears to be a relationship between muscular strength and power and change-of-direction performance. For many years it was commonly accepted that muscular strength and power characteristics determined the athlete’s ability to sprint.
summary: Both straight-line sprinting and change-of-direction training are crucial for athletic development, with a relationship between strength, power, and change-of-direction performance.

content: The addition of the ball (e.g., a soccer ball or basketball) and agility drills can significantly improve an athlete’s ability to perform multidirectional tasks such as change-of-direction movements. Both straight-line sprinting and change-of-direction tasks with and without implements must be incorporated at various stages of the athlete’s development and preparation for competition.
summary: Training should incorporate both straight-line sprinting and change-of-direction drills with and without implements to improve multidirectional athleticism.

content: For many years it was commonly accepted that muscular strength and power characteristics determined the athlete’s ability to sprint. There appears to be a relationship between muscular strength and power and change-of-direction performance. The literature indicates that this relationship may be stronger with distinct change-of-direction movements than with directional changes performed at speed over longer durations. The athlete should always strive to become stronger and more powerful because this will translate into change-of-direction ability.
summary: Strength and power are important for both sprinting and change-of-direction performance, with a stronger relationship observed in distinct change-of-direction movements.

content: Reactive strength, or the ability to engage the stretch shortening cycle (SSC), also appears to contribute to the athlete’s ability to change direction. The ability to engage the SSC in response to eccentric loading allows for greater forces to be generated during the concentric phase of a change-of-direction task. The ability to engage this mechanism may allow for a more rapid acceleration when changing direction. It is strongly recommended to incorporate plyometric activities into training, given their strong relationship to change-of-direction performance.
summary: Reactive strength, developed through plyometrics, enhances change-of-direction ability by utilizing the stretch-shortening cycle for greater force production.

content: Developing a training plan involves planning at several distinct levels, including the microcycle, macrocycle, and annual training plan. At each level of planning, the coach must consider the principles of developing speed and agility and understand the physiological and performance responses to specific training variables. To maximize training effects, speed training needs to be meticulously dosed and incorporate low training volumes interspersed with long periods of restoration. Excessive use of sprint activities will eventually result in overtraining. It is not wise to perform sprint and agility training under conditions of fatigue or with excessively short rest intervals.
summary: Effective speed and agility training requires careful planning across different time scales, appropriate training volume and rest, and avoidance of fatigue.

content: To develop the appropriate movement patterns, the coach must emphasize proper technique in all training activities. If the athlete performs drills with inadequate technique, she will stabilize inappropriate movement patterns that will hinder the development of speed and the expression of agility. Focus on proper technique should begin during the warm-up and continue into the main body of the training session. If fatigue causes technical breakdowns, it may be warranted to reduce the training volume of that session.
summary: Proper technique is crucial for developing speed and agility, requiring emphasis throughout training and adjustments based on fatigue.

content: When developing speed and agility, the athlete should develop skills in relationship to the demands of his sport. The coach should develop specific sprint and agility activities based on the needs of the sport. Many factors can contribute to the athlete’s ability to exhibit speed and effectively perform change-of-direction movements. Coaches must understand the bioenergetic demands of different sprint and agility training activities and how they relate to the athlete’s targeted sport. Additional aspects to consider are the role of strength training in the expression of speed and the development of change-of-direction abilities.
summary: Speed and agility training should be tailored to the specific demands of the athlete's sport, considering bioenergetic factors and the role of strength training.

content: It is important to record the athlete's objective and subjective feedback throughout the training process. Objective feedback can include recorded times and video analysis of performance; subjective feedback may include the concept of perceived maximum speed. With novice athletes or when the skill set is complex, the coach should provide constant feedback and reinforcement. This feedback appears to be essential during the early stages of skill development; as the athlete develops, less feedback is necessary. Consider providing information on proper performance and methods for correcting errors. Try to address one or two technical corrections per session, following a priority sequence. As the skill develops, feedback should be less frequent and should progress from qualitative to quantitative.
summary: Feedback, both objective and subjective, is crucial for skill development, with the frequency and type adapting to the athlete's experience level.

content: To develop speed or agility, the athlete must be highly motivated. High power output activities always require a high level of motivation and maximum concentration. Furthermore, sprint and agility training produces a large amount of fatigue, especially when targeting speed endurance. A motivated athlete is more likely to tolerate this type of training. Motivation can be cultivated by providing feedback, especially feedback that emphasizes the positive aspects of the athlete’s training, and by including the athlete in the planning process. If the plan is implemented correctly, the athlete will be more likely to push himself to higher levels.
summary: Motivation is essential for successful speed and agility training, which can be cultivated through positive feedback, athlete involvement, and a well-structured plan.

content: When integrating speed or agility training into the annual periodized training plan, the coach should consider manipulating several training variables. The density of training is the amount of training that occurs within a given time period (training session).
summary: Integrating speed and agility training into a periodized plan involves manipulating variables like training density.

content: A motivated athlete is more likely to tolerate this type of training. Motivation can be cultivated by providing feedback, especially feedback that emphasizes the positive aspects of the athlete’s training, and by including the athlete in the planning process. If the plan is implemented correctly, the athlete will be more likely to push himself to higher levels.
summary: Motivation is key to athlete adherence and success, achieved through feedback, athlete involvement, and a well-executed plan.

content: When integrating speed or agility training into the annual periodized training plan, the coach should consider manipulating several training variables. These variables include the density of training, which is the amount of training that occurs within a given time period (training session). When considering a sprint, sprint endurance, or agility session, the density is a ratio of the interval of exercise and rest within a set or series of sprints. If quality of work is expected, longer rest intervals are necessary to facilitate good recovery between sets of sprints or agility drills.
summary: Effective speed and agility training involves manipulating variables like training density and rest intervals to optimize quality and recovery.

content: A run can be calculated in either seconds or minutes to determine the duration of exercise. The distance traveled can also be quantified in meters or yards. For example, if an athlete sprints 100 m (distance) in 12 s (duration), both distance and duration have been determined and can be used to calculate an intensity factor. The duration or distance of an activity determines the bioenergetic pathway and the specific quality targeted.
summary: Training duration and distance determine the energy system utilized and the specific training quality targeted (e.g., acceleration, speed, endurance).

content: Short-duration or short-distance activities tend to target the phosphagen system and the development of acceleration or speed. Acceleration is emphasized when the athlete runs short sprints (10 to 40 m), whereas maximum speed is targeted with longer sprints (50 to 60 m). If, on the other hand, the objective is to train speed endurance, the distance is increased from 80 to 150 meters. Elite sprinters appear to reach maximum velocities after 5 to 6 s in about 50 to 60 m, whereas novice sprinters attain top speeds by 20 to 30 m. If the sprint is extended, reliance on the oxidative energy system will increase. Therefore, the distance and duration of the sprint are important considerations when an athlete is targeting acceleration, maximum speed, or endurance characteristics.
summary: Sprint distance and duration influence the targeted energy system and training quality (acceleration, speed, endurance).

content: In the case of agility training, duration has to be selected depending on the energy systems dominant in the selected sport and should be position-specific. In most cases the type and duration of an agility drill is relatively short and very intensive (between 5 and 10 s). This might be advisable for some but sports, such as volleyball and American football, where the dominant energy system is the phosphagen system. However, in other team sports where the oxidative system is the main supplier of the energy (e.g., soccer, rugby), the total duration of the agility drills can be much longer or the short agility drills can be separated by very short rest intervals. A similar discrepancy has to be envisioned for specific positions in team sports. For instance, the duration of agility drills for full or central back players in soccer has to be shorter (10 to 15 s), very intense, and to incorporate reaction time and movement time exercises. On the other hand, for midfielders, for whom the dominant energy system is aerobic, the coach has to create an agility drill of longer duration, such as 20 to 40 s or longer.
summary: Agility drill duration should be tailored to the sport, energy systems involved, and specific player position.

content: The exercise order is the sequence in which specific training tasks are performed. When prescribing the order of exercises in a speed, speed endurance, or agility session, the coach must consider both the management of fatigue and the development of fitness. Because sprint and agility training activities impose large metabolic, neuromuscular, and coordinative demands, they should be conducted when the athlete has a minimal level of fatigue. A warm-up primes the athlete for the training bout. It may be advisable to separate the training session into specific segments within the daily training plan. The morning session may focus on speed training, and the afternoon session may target speed endurance or strength training. Sessions in which maximum speed or high-intensity agility is the goal are best performed in the afternoon, especially if they follow a restful morning. Athletes should ideally be in a state of supercompensation since maximum speed is a determinant quality in speed training.
summary: Exercise order, fatigue management, and session timing are crucial for optimizing speed and agility training.

content: Speed and agility training sessions are best structured with brief work periods separated by frequent rest intervals that last between 3 and 5 minutes. The repetition method contains very short, high-intensity work bouts performed for low volumes and long rest intervals that maximize recovery and the development of speed and technical proficiency. It is possible to create sessions that target speed and speed endurance, but the coach must take care in ordering the exercises in these sessions.
summary: Effective speed and agility sessions utilize brief work periods, frequent rest intervals, and careful exercise ordering.

content: When an athlete is training for speed, speed endurance, or agility, the intensity is often quantified in relation to maximum velocity or speed of movement. Intensity = Distance / Time. If an athlete runs 100 m in 12 s, she would have an intensity of 8.33 m/s. Short sprints performed at high velocities provide a higher-intensity bout of exercise. The coach can establish intensity zones based on the athlete’s best times in a prescribed distance.
summary: Training intensity for speed, speed endurance, and agility is often measured as distance/time, with higher velocities indicating higher intensity.

content: When an athlete is training for speed, speed endurance, or agility, the intensity is often quantified in relation to maximum velocity or speed of movement. Intensity = Distance / Time. If an athlete runs 100 m in 12 s, she would have an intensity of 8.33 m/s. Short sprints performed at high velocities provide a higher-intensity bout of exercise.
summary: This chunk defines training intensity in relation to speed and distance, highlighting the relationship between velocity and intensity.

content: The coach can establish intensity zones based on the athlete’s best times in a prescribed distance. The coach can use the following formula to determine the percentage maximum intensity (velocity): Percentage maximum velocity = Maximum intensity x Percentage. If the athlete ran a maximum 100 m in a time of 11 s, his maximum intensity of 90% would be represented by the following equation: 90% maximum velocity = 9.1 m/s x 0.90 = 8.2 m/s.
summary: This chunk explains how coaches can set intensity zones based on an athlete's best times and calculate percentage maximum velocity.

content: An agility drill must not be of standard duration. Agility drills have to be created based on the dominant energy system in the selected sport and should also be position-specific. Therefore, the duration and intensity of agility drills is directly dependent on the desired sport-specific adaptation.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of tailoring agility drills to specific sports and positions, considering duration and intensity.

content: If the training zone were set at 90% to 100% of maximum, the limits for time would be calculated with the following equations: Training time (s) = Distance (m) / Percentage maximum velocity (m/s). Training time at 90% intensity = 100 m / 8.2 m/s = 12.2 s. In a 4-week macrocycle, the three loading microcycles could progress from 90% to 92.5% to 95%, requiring this athlete to run 100 m at 12.2 s, 11.9 s, and 11.6 s respectively.
summary: This chunk details how to calculate training time based on intensity and provides an example progression over a 4-week period.

content: Long rest intervals (work/rest = 1:50-1:100) should be used when targeting the development of absolute speed because they allow for a greater replenishment of stored phosphagens and allow for the expression of maximal power outputs. When targeting high-intensity interval endurance, the coach should use shorter rest intervals (work/rest = 1:4-1:24). Short rest intervals (work/rest = 1:0.3-1:1) are used when targeting the oxidative system (aerobic).
summary: This chunk outlines different rest interval strategies for developing various aspects of speed and endurance.

content: The work-to-rest ratio in rugby games is between 1:1-1.9 and 1-1.9:1. American football generally has a work-to-rest ratio of 1:6. Soccer exhibits a 1:7 to 1:8 work-to-rest ratio.
summary: This chunk provides examples of work-to-rest ratios in different sports.

content: The training intensity is associated with the rate of performing work or the rate at which energy is expended. The higher the training intensity, the higher the rate of performing work, which corresponds to higher energy expenditures. Training intensity m/s( ) = Training session volume load / Total distance covered in training.
summary: This chunk defines training intensity in relation to work rate and energy expenditure, and provides a formula for calculating it.

content: An athlete’s volume load is 4,112.94 and the total distance covered during the training session is 800 m. Therefore, the training intensity of this session is 5.14 m/s. As the volume load increases, there is a general trend for the intensity or velocity of movement to decrease.
summary: This chunk calculates training intensity based on volume load and distance, and notes the inverse relationship between volume load and intensity.

content: The volume of training represents the amount of work performed in a training session or training phase and is often expressed as the total repetitions of a prescribed workload or task assignment. The most accurate method for determining the volume of workload completed in a training session is the volume load, which is a product of the intensity and distance completed per repetition. Volume load is an excellent indicator of training stress and is generally calculated as a product of work volume and intensity.
summary: This chunk defines training volume and explains how to calculate volume load, emphasizing its importance as a measure of training stress.

content: In the context of sprint or agility training, the volume load of training can be determined using running speeds (intensity) and distances covered. Sprint or agility training volume load = Velocity (m/s) * Distance (m). When the intensity (speed) of the session decreases, the volume load can be increased.
summary: This chunk explains how to calculate volume load specifically for sprint and agility training, and notes the relationship between intensity and volume load.

content: When the coach is designing the training plan, it may be warranted to predict the volume load fluctuations for sprint training and integrate the volume loads with those established for resistance training activities. If done appropriately, the integration of the training loads will allow for a superior management of fatigue while maximizing fitness and preparedness for competition.
summary: This chunk discusses the importance of integrating volume load from different training modalities for optimal fatigue management and performance.

content: Developing a plan for speed and agility training begins with the development of the annual training plan. As noted in chapter 6, key information needed when developing the annual plan includes dates of important competitions and the individual preparation, competition, and transition phases. The annual training plan will be structured based on the sport’s characteristics.
summary: This chunk outlines the initial steps in developing a training plan for speed and agility, emphasizing the importance of considering competition schedule and sport-specific needs.

content: For example, a sprinter will typically use a bi-cycle annual training plan, whereas a team sport athlete may not, depending on league requirements. The annual training plan for an elite sprinter uses a tri-cycle model in which three major peaks are planned, whereas a monocycle structure may be warranted for less developed sprinters. The first peak (for the indoor season) occurs on March 20.
summary: This chunk provides examples of different annual training plan structures for various athletes, highlighting the need for customization based on experience and sport.

content: As noted in chapter 6, key information needed when developing the annual plan includes dates of important competitions and the individual preparation, competition, and transition phases. The annual training plan will be structured based on the sport’s characteristics. For example, a sprinter will typically use a bi-cycle annual training plan, whereas a team sport athlete may not, depending on league requirements. The annual training plan for an elite sprinter uses a tri-cycle model in which three major peaks are planned, whereas a monocycle structure may be warranted for less developed sprinters. The first peak (for the indoor season) occurs on March 20. The second peak (for the National Trials) is planned for July 7. The final peak (for the Olympics) is planned for August 17.
summary: This chunk outlines the key considerations and structures for developing annual training plans, highlighting differences between individual and team sports and providing a sample tri-cycle plan for a sprinter.

content: The dynamics of the annual plan structure for team sports can be vastly different compared with an individual sports athlete, such as a sprinter. The competitive phase is generally dictated by the league or conference in which the athlete’s team competes, and it entails weekly competitions. Figure 12.8 is an example of an annual training plan for a collegiate soccer program. The annual training plan presented in figure 12.8 has a bi-cycle structure in which major competitive emphasis occurs in competition phase 1. Note that as with traditional annual training plans, the year is broken into preparatory, competition, and transition phases.
summary: This chunk contrasts the annual training plan structures for team sports versus individual sports, using a soccer program example to illustrate the bi-cycle structure and emphasis on weekly competitions.

content: The annual training plan presented in figure 12.7, preparatory phase 1 is substantially longer than preparatory phase 2. Preparatory phase 1 contains two subphases, which are classified as general and specific preparation. The general preparation phase targets conditioning with an emphasis on anatomical adaptation, or what is also referred to as strength endurance. A secondary emphasis is placed on tempo work, which targets both anaerobic and aerobic development. In the early part of the general preparation work, long tempo work or aerobic intervals are completed; in the later stages of the phase, short-duration tempo work (anaerobic intervals) is used. As the athlete transitions into the specific preparation subphase, a greater emphasis is placed on the development of maximum strength and the use of anaerobic interval work. Anaerobic interval work is then used to develop repetitive speed endurance.
summary: This chunk details the structure and progression of the preparatory phase in an annual training plan, emphasizing the transition from general to specific preparation and the development of strength endurance and anaerobic capacity.

content: As the athlete transitions into the competitive phase, the focus shifts to developing maximum speed while increasing power-generating capacity and maintaining speed endurance. The annual training plan can be structured to target aspects of the athlete’s development, depending on the phase of training. For example, during the general preparation phase, the primary emphasis is developing strength and general endurance, whereas in the specific preparation phase anaerobic endurance as well as short speed endurance is emphasized. When structuring the phases of the annual training plan, the coach must consider the goals of each phase because this will dictate which factors associated with sprinting are targeted. For example, in the general preparation phase, aerobic capacity and power development is a secondary development emphasis. Selecting extensive tempo work early in the phase would
summary: This chunk explains the shift in training focus during the competitive phase and emphasizes the importance of tailoring the annual plan to the specific goals of each training phase.

content: The text presents an annual training plan for an American university soccer team. The plan is structured into macrocycles, microcycles, and subphases. Macrocycles are further divided into phases: Preparatory 1, Competition 1, Preparatory 2, Competition 2, and so on. Subphases within each macrocycle include General Preparation (GP), Specific Preparation (SP), Precompetition (PC), and Competition (C). The plan emphasizes the development of biomotor abilities such as strength, speed, agility, and endurance. Strength training focuses on anatomical adaptation (AA), maximum strength (MxS), and power (P). Speed training involves linear acceleration, linear accelerations and decelerations/change of direction (COD), and COD/agility. Endurance training includes extensive tempo (ET), intensive tempo (IT), and speed endurance.
summary: This chunk describes the macrocycle, microcycle, and subphase structure of an annual training plan for a collegiate soccer team, highlighting the focus on developing key biomotor abilities through specific training modalities.

content: The plan incorporates both technical and tactical activities alongside physical development. Macrocycle 1 emphasizes anatomical adaptation, linear speed, and aerobic endurance. Macrocycle 2 focuses on maximum strength, linear speed and agility, and aerobic or anaerobic endurance. Macrocycles 3 and 4 prioritize power, agility, and specific endurance. Microcycles are designed to manage fatigue and optimize performance. Speed and agility training should be performed with minimal fatigue to maximize technique and power output. Adequate rest and warm-up are crucial for effective speed and agility training.
summary: This chunk outlines the specific training emphases within each macrocycle of the soccer team's annual plan, emphasizing the integration of technical, tactical, and physical development, as well as the importance of microcycle management for optimal performance.

content: Macrocycle 2 focuses on maximum strength, linear speed and agility, and aerobic or anaerobic endurance. Macrocycles 3 and 4 prioritize power, agility, and specific endurance.
summary: This chunk outlines the focus of different macrocycles in a training plan, progressing from strength and endurance to power and specific endurance.

content: Microcycles are designed to manage fatigue and optimize performance. Speed and agility training should be performed with minimal fatigue to maximize technique and power output. Adequate rest and warm-up are crucial for effective speed and agility training.
summary: This chunk describes the role of microcycles in managing fatigue and the importance of rest, warm-up, and minimizing fatigue for optimal speed and agility training.

content: Speed endurance training aims to increase fatigue resistance by manipulating training variables like work-to-rest intervals, duration, and intensity. Increasing the volume of sprint bouts can lead to specific metabolic adaptations that enhance speed endurance. The development of speed, agility, and speed endurance is important for the majority of sports.
summary: This chunk explains the concept of speed endurance training, its benefits, and its relevance across various sports.

content: Speed, agility, and speed endurance development must reflect the physiological characteristics of the selected sport or position. Speed, agility, and speed endurance development must be integrated into the periodized training plan. Long-distance training methods will impede the development of both speed and agility.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of tailoring speed, agility, and speed endurance training to specific sports and integrating it into a periodized plan, while highlighting the negative impact of long-distance training on these qualities.

content: Maximum strength and power are important characteristics for athletes attempting to maximize speed performance. An integrated strength training program is needed for athletes attempting to maximize speed performance. Some very specific movement mechanics are essential to maximizing an athlete’s speed of movement and facilitate change-of-direction activities. Speed plays a role in change-of-direction performance.
summary: This chunk connects strength, power, and specific movement mechanics to maximizing speed performance and change-of-direction abilities.

content: Speed, agility, and speed endurance development must be integrated into the periodized training plan. It may be warranted to use more change-of-direction tasks that emphasize acceleration, deceleration, changes in direction, and reacceleration activities. It may be warranted to include the implements used in competition (e.g., a soccer ball or basketball).
summary: This chunk further emphasizes the integration of speed, agility, and speed endurance training into a periodized plan, suggesting the use of specific drills and competition-relevant implements.

content: In some sports, speed is considered a straight-line running activity (as with track or wide receivers in American football). In other sports (e.g., racquet sports), quickness or swift movement is, in fact, agility. Many athletes spend large amounts of time performing straight-line training tasks.
summary: This chunk differentiates between speed and agility in various sports, noting the common focus on straight-line speed training.

content: Absolute density is the ratio between the effective work an athlete performs and the absolute volume (duration) of the training session. Absolute strength is the amount of force that can be generated regardless of body size. Absolute volume is the total volume or duration of the work that the individual performs in a session, including rest intervals.
summary: This chunk defines key training terms: absolute density, absolute strength, and absolute volume.

content: Acceleration is the ability to increase movement velocity in a minimum amount of time. Acidosis is a condition in which an accumulation of H+ increases muscle acidity.
summary: This chunk defines acceleration and acidosis, two important physiological concepts in exercise science.

content: Acyclic combined skills are cyclic skills followed by an acyclic skill. Acyclic skills are integral functions performed in one action.
summary: This chunk defines acyclic combined skills and acyclic skills, which are relevant to skill acquisition and training.

content: Adenosine diphosphate (ADP) is a high-energy phosphate compound that can be used to form ATP. Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is a high-energy phosphate compound that allows for the release of energy when its phosphate bonds are broken.
summary: This chunk explains the roles of ADP and ATP in energy production during exercise.

content: Aerobic intervals are intervals that are designed to stress the aerobic system and are also referred to as pace-tempo training. Aerobic intervals can be performed in a continuous or intermittent fashion. Aerobic power is the maximum rate of ATP production via the aerobic energy system.
summary: This chunk defines aerobic intervals and aerobic power, highlighting their importance in aerobic training.

content: Agility is the ability to rapidly change direction and accelerate or decelerate in response to an external cue.
summary: This chunk defines agility, a key component of athletic performance.

content: An anabolic environment is one in which the building of tissue can occur. Anaerobic intervals are a form of interval training in which very high-intensity bouts are repeatedly performed for short periods of time, with periods of rest interspersed between efforts.
summary: This chunk defines anabolic environments and anaerobic intervals, both relevant to training adaptations and performance.

content: The anaerobic threshold is the intensity of exercise at which the body cannot meet its energy demand with aerobic means; the point at which lactate production outweighs buffering capacities.
summary: This chunk defines the anaerobic threshold, a crucial concept in understanding exercise physiology.

content: An annual training plan is a long-term training plan that typically lasts for 1 year. Arteriovenous oxygen difference (a-V.O2 difference) is the oxygen difference between the arterial and mixed venous blood. This reflects the amount of oxygen used by the tissue.
summary: This chunk defines annual training plans and arteriovenous oxygen difference, both important concepts in training periodization and exercise physiology.

content: Asynchronous motor unit firing occurs as a result of one motor unit deactivating while another activates.
summary: This chunk defines asynchronous motor unit firing, a key mechanism in muscle activation and force production.

content: Athletic shape is synonymous with readiness.
summary: This chunk defines athletic shape as a state of readiness for competition.

content: The anaerobic threshold is the intensity of exercise at which the body cannot meet its energy demand with aerobic means; the point at which lactate production outweighs buffering capacities. Lower-intensity exercise receives its ATP primarily from the oxidation of fat and some carbohydrates.  Fast glycolysis is one of the two ways in which glycolysis proceeds. Fast glycolysis results in the formation of lactic acid from the breakdown of glucose and has a faster rate of energy supply when compared to slow glycolysis. Fatigue is a general sense of tiredness that is often accompanied by a decrease in muscular performance.
summary: This chunk describes anaerobic metabolism, lactate production, and fatigue.

content: Bioenergetics is the process by which the body converts foods such as carbohydrates, proteins, and fats into a biologically usable form of energy. Ergogenesis literally translated as ‘energy creation,’ indicates the dominant energy systems of an activity.
summary: This chunk defines bioenergetics and ergogenesis.

content: An annual training plan is a long-term training plan that typically lasts for 1 year. A bi-cycle plan is an annual training plan with two major peaks.
summary: This chunk describes different types of annual training plans.

content: Block training is a sequential approach to structuring training in which individual blocks of training (which contain a distinct focus) are linked together. A block is a period of training that usually lasts 4 weeks and is sometimes referred to as a mesocycle.
summary: This chunk describes block training and its components.

content: A conjugated sequence is a method of sequencing training to take advantage of training residuals developed with periods of concentrated loading; it is also called the coupled successive system. Concentrated loading is a short-term period in which training loads are increased dramatically.
summary: This chunk describes conjugated sequence and concentrated loading.

content: Arteriovenous oxygen difference (a-V.O2 difference) is the oxygen difference between the arterial and mixed venous blood. This reflects the amount of oxygen used by the tissue. Cardiac output (Q) is the volume of blood pumped by the heart per minute. It is calculated as stroke volume multiplied by heart rate.
summary: This chunk defines arteriovenous oxygen difference and cardiac output.

content: Creatine phosphokinase (CPK) is an enzyme contained in the phosphagen system that adds a phosphate to adenosine diphosphate to create ATP.
summary: This chunk describes creatine phosphokinase and its role in ATP production.

content: Cross-bridges are projections around the myosin filament that latch onto the binding site on actin. Asynchronous motor unit firing occurs as a result of one motor unit deactivating while another activates.
summary: This chunk describes cross-bridges and asynchronous motor unit firing.

content: Capillarization is an increase in the capillary networks that bring oxygen and nutrients to the tissues of the body.
summary: This chunk describes capillarization.

content: Carbohydrate is a compound composed of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen.
summary: This chunk defines carbohydrate.

content: Bodybuilding is a sport in which muscle size, definition, and symmetry determine the winner.
summary: This chunk defines bodybuilding.

content: Biomotor abilities are abilities whereby the body can perform a range of activities, such as strength, speed, and endurance. Biomotor abilities are influenced by training and may be genetically determined.
summary: This chunk describes biomotor abilities.

content: Biological age is an indication of age based upon sexual maturation. Chronological age is the age of the individual.
summary: This chunk defines biological and chronological age.

content: Athletic shape is synonymous with readiness.
summary: This chunk defines athletic shape.

content: Detraining is the reversal of the adaptations stimulated by training. The effects of detraining can occur very rapidly when workloads are significantly reduced. Detraining syndrome is a syndrome that occurs when training is intentionally or unintentionally stopped and results in several maladaptations including insomnia, anxiety, depression, and alterations to the cardiovascular system; it is also known as relaxation syndrome, exercise abstinence, or exercise dependency syndrome.
summary: This chunk describes detraining and detraining syndrome.

content: A buffer is the difference between the percentage of 1RM necessary to go to failure with the number of repetitions performed in a set, and the percentage of 1RM actually used for that number of repetitions.
summary: This chunk defines a buffer.

content: The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings. A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.
summary: This chunk describes the squat exercise and a typical prescription.

content: The deadlift is a posterior chain exercise targeting the erector spinae, glutes, and hamstrings. Conventional deadlifts place greater demand on the erector spinae compared to sumo deadlifts.
summary: This chunk describes the deadlift and its variations.

content: Progressive overload is the gradual increase of stress placed on the body during training.
summary: This chunk defines progressive overload.

content: Complexity is the degree of sophistication and biomechanical difficulty of a skill. Differentiation is the process of dissecting skill into subunits and determining where errors are.
summary: This chunk describes complexity and differentiation in skill development.

content: A cluster set is a series of repetitions interspersed with a short rest interval. Exhaustion is training to the point of momentary muscular failure.
summary: This chunk describes cluster sets and exhaustion.

content: Cyclic skills are sporting activities that contain repetitive movements of the same motor skill.
summary: This chunk defines cyclic skills.

content: Delayed-onset muscle soreness (DOMS) is muscle soreness or pain that occurs 24 to 48 hours after a heavy bout of exercise.
summary: This chunk defines delayed-onset muscle soreness.

content: Density of training is the frequency at which an athlete performs a series of repetitions of work per unit of time.
summary: This chunk defines training density.

content: Cortisol is a corticosteroid hormone released from the adrenal cortex that stimulates the catabolism of proteins, the sparing of glucose utilization, an increase in gluconeogenesis, and an increase in free fatty acid mobilization.
summary: This chunk describes cortisol and its effects.

content: Discharge rate is the average number of action potentials per unit of time.
summary: This chunk defines discharge rate.

content: An enzyme is a protein compound that speeds a chemical reaction.
summary: This chunk defines an enzyme.

content: Excess postexercise oxygen consumption (EPOC) is the oxygen debt; an oxygen consumption that is elevated above resting after exercise.
summary: This chunk defines excess postexercise oxygen consumption.

content: Fartlek is the Swedish word for ‘speed play’; a classic method for developing endurance.
summary: This chunk describes fartlek training.

content: Slow glycolysis is one of the two ways in which glycolysis proceeds. Slow glycolysis results in the formation of lactic acid from the breakdown of glucose and has a slower rate of energy supply when compared to fast glycolysis.
summary: This chunk describes slow glycolysis.

content: Excess postexercise oxygen consumption (EPOC) is the oxygen debt; an oxygen consumption that is elevated above resting after exercise.  Fatigue is a general sense of tiredness that is often accompanied by a decrease in muscular performance.  Exhaustion is training to the point of momentary muscular failure.
summary: This chunk defines key concepts related to exercise recovery and intensity: EPOC (oxygen debt), fatigue, and exhaustion.

content: The fitness–fatigue relationship is the relationship between fitness and fatigue and how they modulate athlete preparedness. This concept is a major factor associated with periodization. The general adaptation syndrome (GAS) is a syndrome conceptualized by Hans Selye that explains the body’s response to stressors, including physiological and psychological stress. The GAS is often cited as being a foundational component of periodization theory.
summary: This chunk discusses the fitness-fatigue relationship and its connection to periodization, highlighting the general adaptation syndrome (GAS) as a key concept.

content: Flexibility is the range of motion of a joint or set of joints, dependent on the length of the muscles crossing the joints.
summary: This chunk defines flexibility as the range of motion of a joint, influenced by muscle length.

content: A force–velocity curve is a graphic representation of the relationship between force and velocity.
summary: This chunk defines a force-velocity curve as a graphical representation of the relationship between force and velocity.

content: Frequency of training is the number of training sessions within a given time frame.
summary: This chunk defines training frequency as the number of training sessions within a specific timeframe.

content: Hypertrophy is an index indicating the systemic impact of a training session. The index of overall demand (IOD) is an index indicating the systematic impact of a training session.
summary: This chunk defines hypertrophy and the index of overall demand (IOD) as measures of training impact.

content: Inflammation is a local cellular response marked by leukocytic infiltration, pain, swelling, and often loss of function.
summary: This chunk defines inflammation as a localized cellular response characterized by pain, swelling, and leukocyte infiltration.

content: Insulin is an anabolic hormone that facilitates the uptake of glucose and can stimulate protein synthesis. Growth hormone is an anabolic hormone that can enhance cellular amino acid uptake and stimulate protein synthesis; also known as somatotropin.
summary: This chunk describes insulin and growth hormone as anabolic hormones that promote glucose uptake and protein synthesis.

content: Hemoglobin (Hb) is the iron-containing compound in blood that binds oxygen.
summary: This chunk defines hemoglobin as the iron-containing protein in blood responsible for oxygen transport.

content: The Henneman size principle suggests that the size of the motor unit dictates its activation.
summary: This chunk explains the Henneman size principle, which states that motor unit recruitment is size-dependent.

content: High-intensity exercise endurance (HIEE) is a type of endurance that requires the athlete to sustain or repeat high-intensity or high-power movements with exercise durations lasting 2 min or less.
summary: This chunk defines high-intensity exercise endurance (HIEE) as the ability to sustain high-intensity movements for short durations.

content: Low-intensity exercise endurance (LIEE) is a form of endurance that relates to the ability to continuously perform work for a long duration of time.
summary: This chunk defines low-intensity exercise endurance (LIEE) as the ability to sustain work for extended periods.

content: Intramuscular coordination is the strategy of the nervous system in the recruitment of a muscle group. Intermuscular coordination is the strategy of the nervous system in the organization of a kinetic chain, in terms of timing of activation and deactivation of agonist and antagonist muscles.
summary: This chunk describes intramuscular and intermuscular coordination as strategies for muscle recruitment and activation timing.

content: Involution is also known as a decay or detraining.
summary: This chunk defines involution as the process of muscle decline or detraining.

content: Lactate is a salt formed from lactic acid. Lactate is not believed to be associated with fatigue but can be used to create energy. Lactate dehydrogenase is an enzyme contained in the glycolytic energy system that converts pyruvate to lactate.
summary: This chunk explains lactate as a byproduct of glycolysis, clarifying its role in energy production and debunking its association with fatigue.

content: The lactate threshold (LT) is the point at which lactate formation begins to abruptly increase above baseline. Lactic acid is the end product of the fast glycolytic system that is often associated with fatigue because it can inhibit calcium binding to troponin or interfere with cross-bridge formation.
summary: This chunk defines the lactate threshold and explains the role of lactic acid in fatigue.

content: The glycolytic system is the energy system that provides energy via the breakdown of glucose; also known as glycolysis. It generally supplies energy for higher-intensity activities lasting 20 s to 2 min. Fast glycolysis is one of the two ways in which glycolysis proceeds. Fast glycolysis results in the formation of lactic acid from the breakdown of glucose and has a faster rate of energy supply when compared to slow glycolysis.
summary: This chunk describes the glycolytic system and differentiates between fast and slow glycolysis.

content: Glucose is the most common mechanism for transporting carbohydrates in the body; primarily broken down by the glycolytic energy system. Glucose transporter-4 (GLUT4) is a contraction-sensitive glucose transport protein that aids in the uptake of glucose by working skeletal muscle.
summary: This chunk explains glucose transport and the role of GLUT4 in muscle glucose uptake.

content: Glycogen is a storage form of carbohydrate that is found in the skeletal muscle and liver.
summary: This chunk defines glycogen as a stored form of carbohydrate in muscle and liver tissue.

content: Interval training is the repeated performance of short to long bouts of exercise usually performed at or above the lactate threshold interspersed with periods of rest or low-intensity exercise.
summary: This chunk defines interval training as repeated bouts of high-intensity exercise interspersed with rest or low-intensity periods.

content: Long slow distance (LSD) is endurance training that can be classified as conversational exercise, where the athlete can carry on a conversation without respiratory stress.
summary: This chunk defines long slow distance (LSD) training as endurance training at a conversational pace.

content: Integration is a process of constructing whole skills.
summary: This chunk defines integration as the process of building complete skills.

content: Intensity is the qualitative element of training such as speed, maximum strength, and power. In strength training, intensity is often expressed in load related to the 1RM.
summary: This chunk defines intensity in training and its common expression in strength training as a percentage of 1RM.

content: Interexercise recovery is the form of recovery that occurs during the exercise bout and relates to the bioenergetics of the activity being performed.
summary: This chunk defines interexercise recovery as the recovery that occurs during an exercise session, influenced by the bioenergetics of the activity.

content: The line of pull is the median direction of contraction of the main muscles involved in the technical skill.
summary: This chunk defines the line of pull as the central direction of muscle contraction during a skill.

content: Fartlek is the Swedish word for ‘speed play’; a classic method for developing endurance.
summary: This chunk defines fartlek as a classic method for developing endurance, involving varied speeds.

content: General strength is the strength of the whole muscular system.
summary: This chunk defines general strength as the overall strength of the entire muscular system.

content: A Golgi tendon organ is a sensory receptor that monitors tension and is located in the muscle tendon.
summary: This chunk defines a Golgi tendon organ as a sensory receptor that detects muscle tension.

content: The lactate threshold (LT) is the point at which lactate formation begins to abruptly increase above baseline. The onset of blood lactate accumulation (OBLA) is the point at which the blood lactate concentration reaches 4 mmol/L.
summary: Describes lactate threshold and OBLA, key indicators of lactate accumulation during exercise.

content: Lactic acid is the end product of the fast glycolytic system that is often associated with fatigue because it can inhibit calcium binding to troponin or interfere with cross-bridge formation.
summary: Explains the role of lactic acid in fatigue during high-intensity exercise.

content: Maximal aerobic capacity is the maximal capacity for oxygen consumption; also known as the V.O2max, maximal oxygen uptake, or maximal aerobic power. Performance oxygen uptake is the highest value of oxygen consumption during the performance of an exercise without the accumulation of lactate.
summary: Defines maximal aerobic capacity and performance oxygen uptake, measures of aerobic fitness.

content: The oxidative system is the primary source of ATP at rest and during low-intensity exercise, also known as the aerobic system. The system is active in the mitochondria and requires oxygen to make energy. Oxidative metabolism is the metabolism that occurs during the aerobic (oxidative) energy system.
summary: Describes the oxidative energy system and its role in ATP production during low-intensity exercise.

content: Oxygen deficit is the anaerobic contribution to the total energy cost of an exercise.
summary: Defines oxygen deficit, the difference between oxygen demand and supply during exercise.

content: The phosphagen system (ATP-PC) is an anaerobic energy system that primarily provides energy for short-term, high-intensity activities and contains three major enzymatic reactions including the ATPase, creatine kinase, and myokinase reactions. Phosphocreatine (PCr) provides energy for muscle actions by maintaining ATP stores. Myokinase (MK) is an enzyme of the phosphagen system that takes a phosphate from an adenosine diphosphate and adds the phosphate and adenosine diphosphate to make adenosine triphosphate.
summary: Explains the phosphagen system, its role in short-duration, high-intensity activities, and key enzymes involved.

content: Phosphorylase (PHOS) is the enzyme responsible for stimulating muscle glycogen breakdown. Phosphofructokinase (PFK) is the rate-limiting enzyme of the glycolytic system.
summary: Describes the roles of phosphorylase and phosphofructokinase in glycogen breakdown and glycolysis.

content: Muscle endurance is the ability of the neuromuscular system to produce force in a repetitive manner.
summary: Defines muscle endurance as the capacity for repeated force production.

content: Myoglobin is a compound found in muscle that carries oxygen from the cell membrane to the mitochondria.
summary: Explains the role of myoglobin in oxygen transport within muscle cells.

content: Movement time is the athlete’s ability to quickly move a limb in the desired direction.
summary: Defines movement time as the speed of limb movement in a specific direction.

content: The line of pull is the median direction of contraction of the main muscles involved in the technical skill.
summary: Describes the line of pull as the central direction of muscle contraction during a movement.

content: Maximum strength is the highest force that the neuromuscular system can generate during a maximal voluntary contraction.
summary: Defines maximum strength as the peak force output of the neuromuscular system.

content: Metabolism is the sum of the anabolic and catabolic reactions occurring in the body.
summary: Explains metabolism as the collective process of building up and breaking down substances in the body.

content: Mitochondria are specialized cell organelles in which oxidative production of ATP occurs.
summary: Describes mitochondria as the sites of ATP production through oxidative phosphorylation.

content: A macrocycle is a medium-term training cycle that lasts between 2 and 6 weeks. A microcycle is a short training cycle that lasts 3 to 7 days.
summary: Defines macrocycles and microcycles as different timeframes in training periodization.

content: A monocycle is an annual training plan with one major peak. A multipeak plan is an annual plan with multiple competition phases.
summary: Describes monocyclic and multipeak training plans based on the number of competition peaks.

content: Periodization is the logical and systematic sequencing of training factors in an integrative fashion in order to optimize specific training outcomes at predetermined time points.
summary: Defines periodization as the structured planning of training variables to achieve specific goals.

content: Peaking is an athlete’s high physiological and psychological state of competitiveness prior to an important competition, characterized by no residual fatigue and maximum adaptations to the previous training phases. The peaking curve depicts the dynamic of the athlete’s athletic shape throughout the annual plan.
summary: Explains peaking as the optimal performance state before competition and the peaking curve as a visual representation of this process.

content: A motor unit is the motor nerve and all of the muscle fibers it innervates.
summary: Defines a motor unit as the functional unit of muscle contraction, consisting of a nerve and its associated muscle fibers.

content: Power is the quick application of force against resistance. This unit of work is expressed per unit of time (i.e., power = work/time) and is often considered a factor of intensity. It may also be calculated by multiplying force by displacement.
summary: Defines power as the rate of work done, emphasizing its relationship to force, time, and intensity.

content: Long slow distance (LSD) is endurance training that can be classified as conversational exercise, where the athlete can carry on a conversation without respiratory stress. Low-intensity exercise endurance (LIEE) is a form of endurance that relates to the ability to continuously perform work for a long duration of time.
summary: Describes LSD and LIEE as forms of endurance training at low intensities.

content: Progressive overload is the gradual increase of stress placed on the body during training.
summary: Defines progressive overload as the systematic increase in training demands to stimulate adaptation.

content: Monotonous program overtraining is a form of overtraining that occurs in response to a lack of training variation and results in a reduction or stagnation of performance gains.
summary: Describes monotonous program overtraining as a consequence of insufficient training variation.

content: Overreaching is a short-term period during which the athlete intentionally overtrains. Overtraining is a long-term decrement in performance that occurs in response to accumulation of training and non-training stressors.
summary: Differentiates overreaching (short-term) and overtraining (long-term) as responses to excessive training stress.

content: A nonprogressive taper is a taper that is marked by standardized reductions in training load; also known as a step taper.
summary: Defines a nonprogressive taper as a structured reduction in training load with consistent steps.

content: The postactivation potentiation complex is performing a heavy or an explosive exercise to excite the neuromuscular system before jumping or sprinting, thus obtaining a short-term performance improvement.
summary: Describes the postactivation potentiation complex as a technique to enhance performance through pre-exercise activation.

content: Postexercise recovery is a form of recovery that occurs after the cessation of exercise.
summary: Defines postexercise recovery as the process of returning to a resting state after exercise.

content: Myosin heavy chain (MHC) consists primarily of the head of the cross-bridge and is typically associated with the fiber type of the muscle.
summary: Describes the myosin heavy chain as a key component of the cross-bridge and its relationship to muscle fiber type.

content: Phosphofructokinase (PFK) is the rate-limiting enzyme of the glycolytic system. Phosphorylase (PHOS) is the enzyme responsible for stimulating muscle glycogen breakdown.
summary: This chunk describes key enzymes involved in energy production: PFK regulates glycolysis, while PHOS stimulates glycogen breakdown.

content: The postactivation potentiation complex is performing a heavy or an explosive exercise to excite the neuromuscular system before jumping or sprinting, thus obtaining a short-term performance improvement.
summary: This chunk explains the postactivation potentiation complex, which uses heavy exercise to enhance subsequent explosive movements.

content: Power is the quick application of force against resistance. This unit of work is expressed per unit of time (i.e., power = work/time) and is often considered a factor of intensity. It may also be calculated by multiplying force by displacement.
summary: This chunk defines power as the rate of work done, emphasizing its importance in exercise intensity.

content: The power clean is a weightlifting movement in which the barbell is lifted from the ground to the shoulders in one movement. The power snatch is a weightlifting movement in which the barbell is lifted from the ground to a position an arms-length over the head in one movement.
summary: This chunk describes two powerlifting movements: the power clean and the power snatch.

content: Preparedness is the stable component of the athlete’s athletic shape, representing his physical preparation, as well as the acquisition of skills and tactical maneuvers. For this reason, it is the foundation on which to base other training states.
summary: This chunk defines preparedness as the foundational element of an athlete's training, encompassing physical and technical aspects.

content: Prime movers are the muscles that contract to perform the technical skills.
summary: This chunk identifies prime movers as the muscles responsible for executing specific movements.

content: Progressive overloading is a progressive increase in the training load (i.e., intensity, volume) above a normal magnitude.
summary: This chunk explains progressive overloading, a key principle of training involving gradual increases in training stress.

content: Progressive taper is a systematic reduction in training load.
summary: This chunk describes progressive taper, a strategy of gradually decreasing training volume before competition.

content: Proteins are combinations of linked amino acids, all of which contain nitrogen.
summary: This chunk provides a basic definition of proteins and their composition.

content: The pulmonary system is a series of structures that work together to ventilate the body.
summary: This chunk defines the pulmonary system and its function in respiration.

content: A quadrennial plan is a 4-year training plan, typically used with high school, collegiate, and Olympic athletes.
summary: This chunk describes a quadrennial plan, a long-term training strategy often employed by elite athletes.

content: Rate coding is the firing rate of a motor unit.
summary: This chunk defines rate coding as the frequency of nerve impulses sent to a muscle.

content: Rate of force development (RFD) is the rate at which force is developed; it is calculated by dividing the change in force by the change in time.
summary: This chunk defines rate of force development (RFD) as the speed at which force is generated.

content: Reactive agility is agility training where reactive exercises predominate.
summary: This chunk describes reactive agility training, which emphasizes rapid responses to stimuli.

content: Readiness is the ability of the athlete to display his preparation level in the specific performance.
summary: This chunk defines readiness as an athlete's ability to demonstrate their training progress in competition.

content: Recovery is the process of returning to preexercise state.
summary: This chunk defines recovery as the process of restoring the body to its pre-exercise condition.

content: Recruited means activated.
summary: This chunk provides a simple definition of the term 'recruited' in the context of muscle activation.

content: Relative density is the percentage of work volume the athlete performs compared with total volume in the training session.
summary: This chunk defines relative density as the proportion of work performed within a training session.

content: Relative strength is the ratio between the athlete’s maximum strength and his body weight or lean body mass.
summary: This chunk defines relative strength as a measure of strength adjusted for body size.

content: Repetitions are the number of work intervals within a set.
summary: This chunk defines repetitions as the number of times an exercise is performed consecutively.

content: A repetition maximum (RM) is the heaviest weight that can be lifted for a predetermined number of repetitions. A 1RM is the heaviest weight that can be lifted one time, whereas a 10RM is the heaviest weight that can be lifted 10 times.
summary: This chunk explains the concept of repetition maximum (RM) and its variations (e.g., 1RM, 10RM).

content: The repetition method is a higher intensity, longer rest interval type of interval training.
summary: This chunk describes the repetition method, a type of interval training characterized by higher intensity and longer rest periods.

content: The sarcoplasmic reticulum is an extensive, lattice-like, longitudinal network of tubules and structures that store Ca2+.
summary: This chunk describes the sarcoplasmic reticulum, a cellular structure responsible for calcium storage in muscle cells.

content: Sequenced training is the same as block training.
summary: This chunk states that sequenced training and block training are synonymous.

content: A set is the total number of repetitions an athlete performs before taking a rest.
summary: This chunk defines a set as a group of repetitions performed consecutively before resting.

content: A short ton equals 1,102.3 kg.
summary: This chunk provides a conversion factor between short tons and kilograms.

content: Slow glycolysis is one of the two ways in which glycolysis proceeds. Slow glycolysis results in the formation of pyruvate from the breakdown of glucose and proceeds with a rate slower than fast glycolysis.
summary: This chunk describes slow glycolysis, a slower pathway of glucose breakdown compared to fast glycolysis.

content: Specific strength is the strength that is related to the movement patterns of a specific sport.
summary: This chunk defines specific strength as strength tailored to the demands of a particular sport.

content: Speed is the ability to cover a distance in the fastest time possible.
summary: This chunk defines speed as the capacity to move quickly over a distance.

content: Speed-endurance is the ability to maintain speed or repetitively express high speeds of movements.
summary: This chunk defines speed-endurance as the ability to sustain high speeds for extended periods or repeatedly.

content: Speed-strength is the ability to develop force rapidly and at high velocities.
summary: This chunk defines speed-strength as the combination of rapid force production and high movement velocities.

content: Stability (of training stimuli) refers to the body needing a certain number of exposures to a training stimulus for an adaptation to be induced.
summary: This chunk explains the concept of stability in training, emphasizing the need for repeated exposure to a stimulus for adaptation.

content: The repetitiveness of the training stimulus defines its stability.
summary: This chunk clarifies that the frequency of training repetitions determines its stability.

content: Strength is the maximal force or torque a muscle or muscle group can generate.
summary: This chunk defines strength as the maximum force a muscle can produce.

content: The stretch reflex is the contraction of a muscle in response to a stretch; it is also known as the myototic reflex.
summary: This chunk describes the stretch reflex, a muscle contraction triggered by stretching.

content: A stretch shortening cycle (SSC) is a combination of eccentric and concentric muscle actions.
summary: This chunk defines the stretch-shortening cycle (SSC) as a sequence involving both lengthening and shortening of a muscle.

content: Stroke volume (SV) is the amount of blood ejected from the left ventricle during contraction.
summary: This chunk defines stroke volume as the volume of blood pumped out of the left ventricle with each heartbeat.

content: Strength is the maximal force or torque a muscle or muscle group can generate. Torque is the rotational force a muscle or muscle group can generate.
summary: Definitions of strength and torque in exercise science.

content: The stretch reflex is the contraction of a muscle in response to a stretch; it is also known as the myototic reflex. A stretch shortening cycle (SSC) is a combination of eccentric and concentric muscle actions.
summary: Descriptions of the stretch reflex and stretch shortening cycle.

content: Type I muscle fibers have a low force-generating capacity; they are also known as slow-twitch fibers. Type I muscle fibers tend to be smaller, have higher concentrations of oxidative enzymes, and be more fatigue resistant than type II fibers. Type II muscle fibers have a high force-generating capacity; they are also known as fast-twitch fibers. Type II muscle fibers tend to be larger, have higher concentrations of anaerobic enzymes, and be more fatigue sensitive than type I fibers.
summary: Characteristics and distinctions between Type I and Type II muscle fibers.

content: V.O2max is also known as maximal aerobic capacity. Stroke volume (SV) is the amount of blood ejected from the left ventricle during contraction.
summary: Definitions of VO2max and stroke volume in exercise physiology.

content: The sympathetic nervous system is the part of the automatic nervous system that affects, among other things, the cardiovascular system. The sympathetic nervous system releases norepinephrine from its postganglionic nerve endings.
summary: Description of the sympathetic nervous system and its role in cardiovascular function.

content: Synchronization is the simultaneous activation of numerous motor units.
summary: Definition of motor unit synchronization in exercise.

content: Training is a structured exercise program that is designed to develop specific performance characteristics related to sports performance. Training age is the number of years an individual has been training. A training effect is a physiological, performance, or psychological response to a training program.
summary: Definitions of training, training age, and training effect.

content: Training intensity is the intensity determined by dividing the total volume load by the total number of repetitions. Total training demand is a summation of all the training factors contained in the plan.
summary: Definitions of training intensity and total training demand.

content: A training log is a document used to record training information. A taper is an unloading phase of training prior to a major competition that generally lasts between 8 and 14 days.
summary: Descriptions of training logs and tapering strategies.

content: A tri-cycle plan is an annual training plan that contains three major peaks.
summary: Description of a tri-cycle training plan.

content: Volume is a quantitative element of training that can be measured as time or duration of training, the distance covered, the volume load of resistance training, or the number of repetitions performed. Volume load is a method for quantifying volume in speed and agility training; it is calculated by the intensity and distance completed per repetition. Tonnage is a method for quantifying volume in resistance training; it is calculated by multiplying the number of repetitions performed by the number of sets and the resistance in kilograms used.
summary: Definitions and calculations for volume, volume load, and tonnage in training.

content: Work is calculated by multiplying force by displacement.
summary: Definition of work in physics and its application to exercise.

content: Progressive overload is the gradual increase of stress placed on the body during training. Supercompensation is also known as the general adaptation syndrome (GAS).
summary: Definitions of progressive overload and supercompensation in training.

content: Testosterone is the predominant male sex hormone, which is produced in the testes in men and the adrenal cortex and ovaries in women. Testosterone is often used as an index of anabolism or the anabolic status of the body. The testosterone/cortisol ratio (T:C ratio) is an indicator of the anabolic-to-catabolic balance.
summary: Description of testosterone and its role in anabolism, along with the T:C ratio as an indicator of anabolic-catabolic balance.

content: Tmax is the duration at which maximal velocity or power can be maintained during an endurance bout. Velocity is the speed of movement of the body or an object.
summary: Definitions of Tmax and velocity in exercise performance.

content: The repetitiveness of the training stimulus defines its stability.
summary: Relationship between training stimulus repetitiveness and stability.

content: Variability refers to changes or variations in training volume, intensity (load), and frequency in order to stimulate an athlete’s improvement.
summary: Definition of variability in training and its purpose.

content: The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings. A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.

content: The deadlift is a posterior chain exercise targeting the erector spinae, glutes, and hamstrings. Conventional deadlifts place greater demand on the erector spinae compared to sumo deadlifts.

content: The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings. A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.
summary: The squat targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings, typically prescribed at 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps for strength.

content: The deadlift is a posterior chain exercise targeting the erector spinae, glutes, and hamstrings. Conventional deadlifts place greater demand on the erector spinae compared to sumo deadlifts.
summary: The deadlift targets the posterior chain, with conventional and sumo variations differing in erector spinae demand.

content: Progressive overload is the gradual increase of stress placed on the body during training.
summary: Progressive overload involves gradually increasing training stress over time.

content: The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings. A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.
summary: This chunk describes the squat exercise, its primary muscle targets, and the benefits of deep squats for glute activation.

content: Tudor O. Bompa, PhD, revolutionized Western training methods when he introduced his groundbreaking theory of periodization in his native Romania in 1963. After adopting his training system, the Eastern Bloc countries dominated international sports through the 1970s and 1980s. In 1988, Bompa began applying his principles of periodization to the sport of bodybuilding. Bompa has personally trained 11 Olympic medalists (including four gold medalists) and has served as a consultant to coaches and athletes worldwide. Bompa’s books on training methods, including Theory and Methodology of Training: The Key to Athletic Performance and Periodization of Training for Sports, have been translated into 19 languages and used in more than 180 countries for training athletes and educating and certifying coaches. Bompa has been invited to speak about training in more than 30 countries and has been awarded certificates of honor and appreciation from such prestigious organizations as the Ministry of Culture of Argentina, the Australian Sports Council, the Spanish Olympic Committee, NSCA (2014 Alvin Roy Award for Career Achievement), and the International Olympic Committee. A member of the Canadian Olympic Association and the Romanian National Council of Sports, Bompa is a professor emeritus at York University, where he taught training theories since 1987. In 2017, Bompa was awarded the honorary title of doctor honoris causa in his home country of Romania.
summary: This chunk details the life and achievements of Tudor O. Bompa, a pioneer in periodization and a renowned figure in the world of sports training.

content: Buzzichelli has held seminars and lectures at various universities and sport institutes worldwide and was an invited lecturer at the 2012 International Workshop on Strength and Conditioning of Trivandrum (India), the 2015 Performance Training Summit of Beijing (China), the 2016 International Workshop on Strength and Conditioning in Bucharest (Romania), and the 2017 Track and Field National Team Coaches Forum in Havana.
summary: This chunk describes Buzzichelli's international speaking engagements and contributions to various workshops and conferences.

content: Buzzichelli’s coaching experience includes the 2002 Commonwealth Games, the 2003 and 2017 World Track and Field Championships, and the 2016 Summer Olympics. As a strength and conditioning coach for team sports, his senior teams have achieved a first place and a second place in their respective league cups, and his junior teams have won two regional cups.
summary: This chunk highlights Buzzichelli's extensive experience coaching at major international events and in team sports.

content: As a coach of individual sports, Buzzichelli’s athletes have won 23 medals at national championships in four different sports (track and field, swimming, Brazilian jiujitsu, and powerlifting), set nine national records in powerlifting and track and field, and won 10 medals at international competitions. In 2015, Buzzichelli coached two Italian champions in two different sports; in 2016, two of his athletes earned international titles in two different combat sports.
summary: This chunk showcases Buzzichelli's success coaching individual athletes across various disciplines, achieving national and international accolades.
